name: Contact Era

name: Virginia
description: A state whose physiographic regions, rivers, resources, and archaeological periods are the primary focus of this volume's discussion of pre-European Contact American Indian past.

name: Christopher Egghart
description: A scholar dedicated to the Commonwealth's past who authored over a third of the chapters in this volume and discusses Virginia's physiographic regions, rivers, resources, and several archaeological periods.

name: Clifford Boyd
description: Author of Chapter 3 who emphasizes the Paleoindian period and explores the pre-Paleoindian period more thoroughly than was possible in 1989.

name: Michael Barber
description: Author of Chapter 4 who considers the Early Archaic period, which saw major transformations in climate as the last major Ice Age ended.

name: Thomas Jefferson
description: Historical figure who was fascinated by Ice Age megafauna that became extinct during the Early Archaic period.

name: Carole Nash
description: Scholar who considers the ambiguity that has long characterized the Middle Woodland period in Virginia and the Middle Atlantic region.

name: Keith Egloff
description: Scholar who focuses on the Late Woodland cultures of southern Virginia.

name: Christopher Shephard
description: Co-author who examines Late Woodland cultures of eastern Virginia alongside Martin Gallivan.

description: Co-author who examines Late Woodland cultures of eastern Virginia alongside Christopher Shephard.

name: Bernard K. Means
description: Co-author who examines northern Virginia Late Woodland cultures alongside Elizabeth Moore.

description: Co-author who examines northern Virginia Late Woodland cultures alongside Bernard K. Means.

name: Paleoindian period
description: An archaeological period emphasized in Chapter 3, preceded by a pre-Paleoindian period that is explored more thoroughly in this volume than was possible in 1989.

name: pre-Paleoindian period
description: An archaeological period that precedes the Paleoindian period and is explored more thoroughly in this volume than was possible in 1989.

name: Early Archaic period
description: An archaeological period characterized by major transformations in climate as the last major Ice Age ended and Ice Age megafauna became extinct.

name: Archaic period
description: An archaeological period that includes the Early Archaic and continues through the beginnings of the ceramic era.

name: Early Woodland
description: The beginning of the ceramic era, characterized by the introduction of ceramic technology and an environment that becomes more modern with increased human settlement size and duration.

name: Middle Woodland period
description: An archaeological period characterized by ambiguity in Virginia and the Middle Atlantic region.

name: Late Woodland period
description: An archaeological period with a richer archaeological and ethnohistoric record, where the ambiguity of earlier periods vanishes to some degree.

name: Ice Age
description: A geological period that ended during the Early Archaic period, causing major climate transformations.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: A geographic region that, like Virginia, has long been characterized by ambiguity during the Middle Woodland period.

name: southern Virginia
description: A region of Virginia where Dr. Keith Egloff focuses on Late Woodland cultures.

name: eastern Virginia
description: A region of Virginia where Late Woodland cultures are examined by Drs. Shephard and Gallivan, and where much more is known about the Late Woodland period due to sustained European contact.

name: northern Virginia
description: A region of Virginia where Late Woodland cultures are examined by Drs. Means and Moore.

name: Chickahominy
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Eastern Chickahominy
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Upper Mattaponi
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Rappahannock
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Monacan
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Nansemond
description: One of six American Indian tribes in Virginia that received federal recognition in the same year this volume was published.

name: Pamunkey Indians
description: The first Virginia tribe to receive federal recognition, which occurred two years before the other six tribes were recognized.

name: European Contact
description: The historical event marking the first contact between American Indians and Europeans, which disrupted but did not eradicate native societies.

name: Atlantic
description: Ocean bordering Virginia's eastern coast, referenced in relation to the Coastal Plain and barrier islands.

name: Allegheny Mountains
description: High summit ridge lines representing part of Virginia's mountainous western region.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Major estuary in Virginia formed by the flooded ancient Susquehanna River valley, receiving drainage from great tidal rivers.

name: Coastal Plain
description: One of Virginia's five primary physiographic provinces, consisting of a broad, relatively level expanse fronting the Atlantic shore comprised of fluvial and marine terraces.

name: Piedmont
description: Physiographic province in Virginia characterized by rolling hills with steady increase in elevations from east to west, featuring mixed pine/oak stands.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Physiographic province featuring high ridgelines with increasing elevation from north to south, part of Virginia's mountainous region.

name: Great Valley
description: Valley system in Virginia's western region, part of the Ridge and Valley physiographic complex.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Physiographic province characterized by repeating ridges and valleys with elevations increasing from east to west.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: Physiographic province in Virginia characterized by rugged hills and heavily dissected terrain with deep hollows.

name: Mississippi River
description: Major river system that receives westward-flowing drainage from Virginia via the mid-continent.

name: Gulf of Mexico
description: Ultimate destination for westward-flowing Virginia rivers via the Mississippi River system.

name: Carolina Sounds
description: Coastal water bodies that receive drainage from Virginia rivers flowing eastward.

name: Eastern Continental Divide
description: Major drainage divide that Virginia straddles, separating eastward and westward flowing river systems.

name: North Carolina
description: State bordering Virginia to the south, where the Virginia Coastal Plain reaches its maximum width of approximately 90 miles.

name: Tidewater region
description: Region in Virginia where the Coastal Plain begins to narrow as it extends northward.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Northern portion of Virginia where the Coastal Plain is squeezed between the Potomac River shore and the Fall Line.

name: Potomac River
description: One of the great tidal rivers feeding Chesapeake Bay, forming the boundary between Virginia and Maryland in the northern region.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary in Northern Virginia that forms one edge of the compressed Coastal Plain province.

name: Maryland
description: State across the Potomac River from Virginia, containing the majority of the Atlantic Coastal Plain in the northern region.

name: Susquehanna River
description: Ancient river valley now flooded to form the Chesapeake Bay, bisecting the Atlantic Coastal Plain.

name: Delmarva peninsula
description: Peninsula lying east of the Chesapeake Bay, bounded by the Delaware Bay and Atlantic Ocean, with its lower portion constituting Virginia's Eastern Shore.

name: Delaware Bay
description: Bay forming one boundary of the far end of the Delmarva peninsula.

name: Eastern Shore of Virginia
description: Geographically separate part of the Commonwealth located on the lower reaches of the Delmarva peninsula.

name: Rappahannock River
description: One of the great tidal rivers that divides the Virginia Coastal Plain and feeds into Chesapeake Bay.

name: York River
description: One of the great tidal rivers that divides the Virginia Coastal Plain and feeds into Chesapeake Bay.

name: James River
description: One of the great tidal rivers that divides the Virginia Coastal Plain and feeds into Chesapeake Bay.

name: College of William and Mary
description: Educational institution whose Department of Geology website provided geological formation descriptions for Virginia's physiographic provinces.

name: Virginia Peninsula
description: The southernmost peninsula in Virginia's Coastal Plain, defined by the James River on one side and the York River on the other.

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia that forms one boundary of the Virginia Peninsula and contains the Inner Lower James region.

name: York River
description: A river in Virginia that forms the other boundary of the Virginia Peninsula.

name: Middle Peninsula
description: The land between the York and Rappahannock rivers in Virginia.

name: Rappahannock River
description: A river in Virginia that forms boundaries for the Middle Peninsula and Northern Neck regions.

name: Northern Neck
description: The region of Virginia that lies between the Rappahannock River and the Potomac shore.

name: Potomac
description: A river and shore area that forms the boundary of Virginia's Northern Neck region.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A major physiographic province in Virginia that can be divided into Inner, Middle, and Outer sections.

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: A subdivision of Virginia's Coastal Plain characterized by broad upland terraces lying up to 60 feet above mean sea level.

name: Middle Coastal Plain
description: A subdivision of Virginia's Coastal Plain corresponding with mid-tidal drainage areas of the Lower James and Rappahannock rivers.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: The eastern subdivision of Virginia's Coastal Plain where elevation drops to around 10 feet with nearly table-top level terrain.

name: Inner Lower James
description: The area lying between the base of the Falls and the mouth of the Appomattox on the James River.

name: Appomattox
description: A river whose mouth marks the boundary of the Inner Lower James region.

name: Route 17
description: A highway route that serves as a geographical reference point for the Outer Coastal Plain boundary on the Middle Peninsula.

name: Gloucester Point
description: A location on the Middle Peninsula where the Outer Coastal Plain is mostly limited to areas just east of Route 17.

name: Mathews County
description: A low-lying county on the north side of Mobjack Bay, mostly encompassed by the Outer Coastal Plain.

name: Mobjack Bay
description: A bay in Virginia with Mathews County located on its north side.

name: Fall Line
description: A geological feature that characterizes the western shore of the Potomac with distinctive topography.

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic province near the Potomac river channel interface with distinctive terrain features.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A region of Virginia with topography similar to the greater Delmarva region, characterized by minimal relief and table-top flat uplands.

name: Delmarva
description: A peninsula region that includes Virginia's Eastern Shore, flanked on its eastern side by barrier islands.

name: Carolina bays
description: Large closed drainage depressions surrounded by dune-like ridges that occur across the Delmarva peninsula interior, some measuring more than a mile in diameter.

name: Atlantic Ocean
description: The ocean that does not directly front the Eastern Shore mainland, which is instead flanked by barrier islands.

name: Atlantic barrier island chain
description: A chain of low barrier islands extending from New Jersey to Georgia that includes islands flanking Delmarva's eastern side.

name: New Jersey
description: The northern extent of the Atlantic barrier island chain.

name: Georgia
description: The southern extent of the Atlantic barrier island chain.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: A major swamp feature in far southeast Virginia characterized by very low and topographically differentiated terrain.

name: Lake Drummond
description: One of only two natural lakes in the Commonwealth of Virginia, located at the center of the Dismal Swamp.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to the Commonwealth of Virginia.

name: Suffolk Scarp
description: An abrupt rise in elevation that marks the western edge of the Dismal Swamp, with well-drained and fertile soils.

name: Middle Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region that transitions to the Outer Coastal Plain, expressed in the Yorktown area.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic region that transitions from the Middle Coastal Plain.

name: Yorktown
description: An area on the north side of the James River where the Middle Coastal Plain-Outer Coastal Plain transition is expressed.

name: James
description: A major river mentioned in relation to geographic features and the Middle Coastal Plain south of it.

name: Carolina bays
description: Occasional landform features visible across the landscape west of the Suffolk Scarp.

name: Fall Line
description: The eastern boundary of the Piedmont province, roughly followed by Interstate 95.

name: Piedmont
description: By far the largest physiographic province in the Commonwealth, bounded by the Fall Line on the east and the Blue Ridge massif on the west.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range that forms the western boundary of the Piedmont province.

name: Interstate 95
description: A highway that roughly follows the Fall Line.

name: Emporia
description: A town along the Fall Line roughly followed by Interstate 95.

name: Richmond
description: A city along the Fall Line roughly followed by Interstate 95.

name: Petersburg
description: A city along the Fall Line roughly followed by Interstate 95.

name: Fredericksburg
description: A city along the Fall Line roughly followed by Interstate 95.

name: Washington DC
description: A city along the Fall Line, less than 40 miles from the crest of the Blue Ridge in far northern Virginia.

name: North Carolina
description: A state that borders Virginia, where the Piedmont stretches over 150 miles in width.

name: Virginia
description: The state being described, where the Piedmont pinches out in the far northern region.

name: Appalachian Mountains
description: A mountain range whose internal core comprises the same formations as the Piedmont geology.

name: Proterozoic
description: An ancient geological era dating some of the near surface formations in the Piedmont region.

name: Paleozoic
description: An ancient geological era dating some of the near surface formations in the Piedmont region.

name: Triassic
description: A geological period when sedimentary rock found in the Piedmont was formed.

name: Outer Piedmont
description: The largest of three Piedmont subprovinces, characterized by gently rolling topography and fronting the Fall Line with western limits extending from Manassas to Martinsville.

name: Mesozoic Lowlands
description: A Piedmont subprovince occurring in two separate sections with fairly undifferentiated topography, moderately to poorly drained soils, and elevations between 200-400 feet.

name: Foothills
description: A narrow Piedmont subprovince along the Blue Ridge margin with hilly terrain and individual summits often exceeding 2,000 feet in elevation.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary that the Outer Piedmont fronts in Virginia.

name: Manassas
description: A Virginia location that serves as a reference point for both the Outer Piedmont's western boundary and the northern extent of the first Mesozoic Lowland section.

name: Charlottesville
description: A Virginia city along the western boundary line of the Outer Piedmont subprovince.

name: Lynchburg
description: A Virginia city along the western boundary line of the Outer Piedmont subprovince.

name: Martinsville
description: A Virginia location marking the southwestern extent of the Outer Piedmont's western boundary.

name: Potomac River
description: A river that marks the northern boundary of the first section of the Mesozoic Lowland subprovince.

name: Washington DC
description: A city referenced as a geographic landmark near the Potomac River in relation to the Mesozoic Lowland boundaries.

name: Orange
description: A Virginia location marking the approximate southwestern extent of the first Mesozoic Lowland section.

name: Richmond
description: A Virginia city that serves as an eastern reference point for the second section of the Mesozoic Lowland.

name: Farmville
description: A Virginia location marking the northern extent of the second Mesozoic Lowland section.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A prominent physical feature and iconic Virginia landmark consisting of an imposing ridge line that rises abruptly from the Piedmont, with ancient and complex geologic history including Mesoproterozoic crystalline rock at its core.

name: Allegheny mountain chain
description: A greater mountain chain whose northeast-southwest orientation is closely paralleled by the Blue Ridge ridge line.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region containing early Paleozoic rocks that the Blue Ridge has been thrust over during its formation.

name: Mesoproterozoic
description: A geological time period during which the crystalline rock at the core of the Blue Ridge was formed.

name: Paleozoic
description: A geological era during which rocks in the adjacent Ridge and Valley region were formed.

name: Northern Blue Ridge
description: A sub-province of the Blue Ridge Province in Virginia, characterized by elevations around 1500 feet near Washington DC and reaching up to 4,000 feet in central areas, with single well-defined ridge lines in the north becoming more complex moving south.

name: Southern Blue Ridge
description: A sub-province of the Blue Ridge Province in Virginia, characterized by a broad prominent escarpment rather than a single ridge line, with higher overall elevations than the Northern Blue Ridge despite less pronounced local relief.

name: James River
description: A river that provides the demarcation between the Northern Blue Ridge and Southern Blue Ridge sub-provinces, breaking through the Blue Ridge.

name: Washington DC
description: A reference point for describing the elevation of the far Northern Blue Ridge, which is only around 1500 feet above mean sea level in that area.

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic region adjacent to the Blue Ridge, referenced for elevation comparisons and local relief measurements.

name: Mount Rogers
description: Located in southwestern reaches of the Virginia Blue Ridge at 5,729 feet above sea level, it is the highest point in the Commonwealth of Virginia.

name: Ridge and Valley Province
description: A physiographic province defined by a series of parallel running ridges and intervening valleys, formed by differential weathering of linear rock belts subjected to thrusting and folding.

name: Great Valley
description: A major component of the Ridge and Valley Province extending from Hagerstown south through Virginia to Bristol and beyond, with its widest point in Virginia measuring over 22 miles across.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: The middle and northern reaches of the Great Valley, featuring generally fertile soils and relief varying between nearly flat terrain and low rolling hills.

name: Hagerstown
description: The northern starting point of the Great Valley that extends south through Virginia.

name: Bristol
description: A location marking the southern extent of the Great Valley in Virginia before it continues beyond state borders.

name: Roanoke Valley
description: The local name for the southern portion of the Great Valley south of where the James River breaks through the Blue Ridge.

name: Christiansburg
description: An area where the valley floor rises in elevation, after which terrain falls toward the New River.

name: New River
description: A river toward which terrain falls after the area of Christiansburg in the Great Valley.

name: Roanoke
description: A reference point for describing elevation changes in the Great Valley, with valley floor elevations north and south of this location differing significantly.

name: Blue Ridge massif
description: The eastern boundary of the Great Valley, forming a major mountain system in Virginia.

name: West Virginia
description: The neighboring state whose border is referenced for describing peak ridge line summits around 4,000 feet in the Ridge and Valley Province.

name: Virginia
description: The state being described in terms of its physiographic provinces and geographic features.

name: Massanutten Mountain
description: A long, linear ridge that rises from the center of the northern Shenandoah Valley, splitting it in two and rising as much as 3000 feet from the Great Valley floor.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A valley that is split in two by Massanutten Mountain ridge rising from its center.

name: Great Valley
description: A valley from which Massanutten Mountain ridge rises abruptly as much as 3000 feet.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: A physiographic province of which only a small portion lies within Virginia, characterized by extremely rugged terrain with steep hills, deep hollows, and narrow entrenched drainages.

name: Buchanan County
description: One of the far southwest counties of Virginia that falls almost entirely within the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Dickerson County
description: One of the far southwest counties of Virginia that falls almost entirely within the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Wise County
description: One of the far southwest counties of Virginia that falls almost entirely within the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Tazewell County
description: A Virginia county whose very western margins encompass the Appalachian Plateau topography.

name: Russell County
description: A Virginia county whose very western margins encompass the Appalachian Plateau topography.

name: Lee County
description: The southwestern most county in the Commonwealth, whose very western margins encompass the Appalachian Plateau topography and where Cumberland Mountain rises dramatically.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A physiographic province adjacent to the Appalachian Plateau, characterized by broad valleys and long ridgelines with extensive floodplains and karst lands with deep caves.

name: Cumberland Mountain
description: An imposing ridge line on the western edge of Lee County that rises from the adjoining broad and gently undulating limestone valley floor.

name: Allegheny structural front
description: The northern interface between the Appalachian Plateau and the Ridge and Valley provinces.

name: High Knob
description: The highest point in the Virginia Appalachian Plateau province, cresting at just over 4,200 feet.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to the Commonwealth of Virginia in the context of Lee County being the southwestern most county.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major body of water that receives drainage from rivers in the eastern and northern halves of Virginia.

name: Carolina Sounds
description: A collective term for the Albemarle, Pamlico and Currituck sounds into which rivers in the southern tier of Virginia counties empty.

name: Albemarle
description: One of the sounds that comprise the Carolina Sounds drainage system.

name: Pamlico
description: One of the sounds that comprise the Carolina Sounds drainage system.

name: Currituck
description: One of the sounds that comprise the Carolina Sounds drainage system.

name: Ohio River
description: A major river that forms part of Virginia's fourth regional watershed and ultimately meets the Tennessee River before joining the Mississippi.

name: Tennessee River
description: A major river system with headwaters in far Southwestern Virginia, ultimately meeting the Ohio River before joining the Mississippi.

name: Potomac River
description: A major river in the eastern and northern halves of Virginia that feeds directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Rappahannock River
description: A major river in the eastern and northern halves of Virginia that feeds directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: York River
description: A major river in the eastern and northern halves of Virginia that feeds directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: James River
description: A major river in the eastern and northern halves of Virginia that feeds directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Roanoke River
description: A relatively significant drainage within the Carolina Sounds watershed, though smaller than the Potomac or James rivers.

name: Dan River
description: A relatively significant drainage within the Carolina Sounds watershed, though smaller than the Potomac or James rivers.

name: Nottoway River
description: A relatively modest river that drains far southeastern Virginia.

name: Meherrin River
description: A relatively modest river that drains far southeastern Virginia.

name: Blackwater River
description: A relatively modest river that drains far southeastern Virginia.

name: Clinch River
description: A river that comprises part of the Tennessee headwaters in far Southwestern Virginia.

name: Powell River
description: A river that comprises part of the Tennessee headwaters in far Southwestern Virginia.

name: Holston River
description: A river that comprises part of the Tennessee headwaters in far Southwestern Virginia.

name: Big Sandy River
description: A direct Ohio tributary whose headwater tributaries drain much of the Appalachian Plateau portion in Virginia.

name: New River
description: A river that empties into the Ohio by way of the Kanawha, flowing northwest from the North Carolina/Tennessee/Virginia border region and draining Virginia's west-central Ridge and Valley.

name: Kanawha River
description: A river through which the New River empties into the Ohio River.

name: Mississippi River
description: A major river that the combined Tennessee and Ohio rivers join at Cairo, Illinois.

name: Virginia
description: The state being described, which has four regional watersheds and various river systems.

name: North Carolina
description: A state that borders Virginia in the region where the New River originates.

name: Tennessee
description: A state that borders Virginia in the region where the New River originates.

name: Cairo
description: A city in Illinois where the combined Tennessee and Ohio rivers join the Mississippi River.

name: Illinois
description: A state containing the city of Cairo where major rivers converge.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: A geographic region in Virginia drained by the Big Sandy River's headwater tributaries.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region in west-central Virginia drained by the New River.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major watershed and tidal bay system that was a focal point of early English settlements in North America and connected Virginia to other colonies and England.

description: A major river component of the Upper Ohio watershed system.

name: Virginia
description: The state whose waterways and river systems are being characterized for their role in shaping settlement and lifeways from prehistory through historic times.

name: North America
description: The continent where early English settlements were established, particularly in the Chesapeake region.

description: The country connected to Virginia through waterways that served as avenues for commerce.

name: United States
description: The newly independent nation in whose growth and development Virginia's rivers continued to play a major role.
