name: Hunting Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Douge Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Accotink Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Quantico Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Chopawamsic Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Aquia Creek
description: A tributary creek that flows into the Lower Potomac River.

name: Quantico
description: A location along the Potomac River where the Virginia shoreline south of it is characterized by bluffs or pronounced upland slopes.

name: Lower Potomac
description: The lower portion of the Potomac River that turns sharply northeast near Fredericksburg and is brackish year-round at that point.

name: Fredericksburg
description: A location where the Lower Potomac turns sharply northeast and where the Rappahannock River crosses the Falls.

name: Dahlgren
description: A location where the Potomac River turns southeast and widens into a major arm of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Potomac
description: A major river that widens into an arm of the Chesapeake Bay and reaches eight miles in width near its juncture with the bay.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major bay that the Potomac River joins, with the main body of the Potomac reaching the bay between Point Lookout, Maryland and Smith Point, Virginia.

name: Nomini Creek
description: A side arm of the Potomac River in the lower reaches near the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Yeocomico Creek
description: A side arm of the Potomac River in the lower reaches near the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Coan River
description: A side arm of the Potomac River in the lower reaches near the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Glebe River
description: A side arm of the Potomac River in the lower reaches near the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Little Wicomico River
description: A side arm of the Potomac River in the lower reaches near the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Point Lookout
description: A location in Maryland at the juncture of the Potomac River with the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Maryland
description: The state where Point Lookout is located at the Potomac-Chesapeake Bay juncture.

name: Smith Point
description: A location in Virginia at the juncture of the Potomac River with the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Virginia
description: The state where Smith Point is located and where the Virginia shoreline along the Potomac is described.

name: Rappahannock River
description: A river that originates east of the Blue Ridge with a watershed of 2,669 square miles, flowing through rural farmland and crossing the Falls at Fredericksburg.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range east of which the Rappahannock River originates, at the foot of which the river has its headwaters.

name: Rapidan
description: One of the main Piedmont tributaries of the Rappahannock River.

name: Hazel River
description: One of the main Piedmont tributaries of the Rappahannock River.

name: Falls
description: A geographic feature at Fredericksburg where the Rappahannock River crosses.

name: Port Royal
description: A location on the Rappahannock River where the river broadens into a wide estuary.

name: Tappahannock
description: A location on the Rappahannock River below which waters transition to brackish and the river broadens considerably.

name: Corratoman River
description: The largest of the broadly embayed tributary streams in the lower Rappahannock area.

name: Windmill Point
description: A location where the tidal Rappahannock meets the main body of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Stingray Point
description: A location where the tidal Rappahannock meets the main body of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: York River
description: A river formed by the confluence of the Mattaponi and Pamunkey rivers at West Point, with its main stem falling entirely within the Coastal Plain physiographic province.

name: Mattaponi River
description: One of the two rivers that form the York River at their confluence at West Point.

name: Pamunkey River
description: One of the two rivers that form the York River at their confluence at West Point.

name: West Point
description: The location where the Mattaponi and Pamunkey rivers converge to form the York River.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic province within which the main stem of the York River falls entirely.

name: Gloucester Point
description: A prominent peninsula feature along the York River that extends from the north bank opposite Yorktown, forming a constriction in the river.

name: Yorktown
description: A location on the York River opposite Gloucester Point, below which the river opens to meet Mobjack Bay and Poquoson Flats.

name: Mobjack Bay
description: A large, shallow bay along the Chesapeake's western shore that the York River opens to meet below Yorktown.

name: Poquoson Flats
description: A large, shallow bay along the Chesapeake's western shore that the York River opens to meet below Yorktown.

name: West Point
description: A geographic location upstream of which the Mattaponi and Pamunkey rivers wind through the west-central coastal plain.

name: Mattaponi River
description: A river that winds through the west-central coastal plain, characterized by sinuous main channels flanked by extensive marshes and wetlands, with headwater branches known as the Matta, Po, and Ni.

name: Pamunkey River
description: A river that winds through the west-central coastal plain alongside the Mattaponi, characterized by sinuous main channels with extensive marshes and wetlands.

name: North Anna River
description: A major Piedmont tributary to the Pamunkey River.

name: South Anna River
description: A major Piedmont tributary to the Pamunkey River.

name: Matta River
description: One of the headwater branches of the Mattaponi River.

name: Po River
description: One of the headwater branches of the Mattaponi River.

name: Ni River
description: One of the headwater branches of the Mattaponi River.

name: York River watershed
description: A watershed whose uppermost reaches fall in the central Piedmont, well east of the Blue Ridge slope.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range mentioned in relation to the York watershed and where the James River breaks through near Glasgow.

name: James River
description: Virginia's major river flowing approximately 330 miles from western mountains to the Chesapeake Bay, draining about 10,000 square miles and serving as the largest tributary to the Chesapeake Bay other than the Susquehanna and Potomac.

name: Commonwealth of Virginia
description: The state that entirely contains the James River watershed.

name: Atlantic Ocean
description: The ocean into which the James River flows near the mouth of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: The large bay into which the James River flows, with the James being its largest tributary other than the Susquehanna and Potomac rivers.

name: Susquehanna River
description: One of the major tributaries to the Chesapeake Bay, larger than the James River.

name: Potomac River
description: One of the major tributaries to the Chesapeake Bay, larger than the James River.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A physiographic region along the Virginia-West Virginia border where the upper reaches of the James River's Mountain Headwaters lie.

name: West Virginia
description: The state that borders Virginia where the upper reaches of the James River's Mountain Headwaters are located.

name: Jackson River
description: One of the two rivers that confluence to form the James River proper just below Clifton Forge.

name: Cowpasture River
description: One of the two rivers that confluence to form the James River proper just below Clifton Forge.

name: Clifton Forge
description: The location just below where the Jackson and Cowpasture rivers confluence to form the James River proper.

name: Glasgow
description: The location near where the James River breaks through the Blue Ridge.

name: Rivanna River
description: One of the major Piedmont tributaries to the James River.

name: Rockfish River
description: One of the major Piedmont tributaries to the James River.

name: Fall Line
description: The geological feature that the James River crosses in Richmond, where it cascades down rapids and drop-offs.

name: Richmond
description: The city where the James River crosses the Fall Line and cascades down rapids and drop-offs, much of it within the city limits.

name: Appomattox River
description: A major tributary to the Lower James River, with brackish water extending upstream to its confluence during summer droughts.

name: Chickahominy River
description: A major tributary to the Lower James River that flows due east from its headwaters at the Fall Line around Richmond, bisecting the Virginia Peninsula before turning south to meet the James upstream from Jamestown Island.

name: Hopewell
description: A location east of which the James River widens into an expansive tidal estuary and marks the beginning of the fresh-brackish water transition zone.

name: Virginia Peninsula
description: A peninsula that is bisected lengthwise by the Chickahominy River and narrows to less than ten miles on the north side of the James River.

name: Jamestown Island
description: An island location a few miles downstream from where the Chickahominy River meets the James River.

name: Williamsburg
description: A location below which brackish water is normally restricted in the Lower James River, though it can extend upstream to the Appomattox during summer droughts.

name: Nansemond River
description: A major James tributary on the south side of the river, virtually entirely embayed with interior headwater branches that border the Great Dismal Swamp.

name: Elizabeth River
description: A major James tributary on the south side of the river, virtually entirely embayed with interior headwater branches that border the Great Dismal Swamp.

name: Great Dismal Swamp
description: A large swamp that borders the interior headwater branches of the Nansemond and Elizabeth rivers.

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic region where the Piedmont Section of the James River flows and where major tributaries like the North Anna, South Anna, Rivanna, and Rockfish rivers are located.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major bay into which drainage from eastern Virginia flows directly, with small portions of eastern Virginia draining into it.

name: Northern Neck
description: A peninsula region in Virginia whose ends drain directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Middle Peninsula
description: A peninsula region in Virginia that drains directly into the Chesapeake Bay and is roughly split by the Piankatank River.

name: Virginia Peninsula
description: A peninsula region in Virginia that drains directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Cape Henry
description: A coastal area in Virginia that drains directly into the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Piankatank River
description: A river that roughly splits the Middle Peninsula, with its estuary extending approximately 12 miles inland from the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Saluda
description: A town marking the inland extent of the Piankatank estuary, above which the drainage is freshwater and known as Dragon Run.

name: Dragon Run
description: The freshwater portion of the Piankatank drainage above Saluda, bordered by rich marshlands and extensive cypress swamps.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A region of Virginia with a western bayside flank that drains to the Chesapeake and an ocean side that drains to the Atlantic.

name: Route 13
description: A road marking the division between Atlantic and Chesapeake drainage on the Eastern Shore.

name: Virginia Barrier islands
description: Islands along Virginia's Atlantic coast with broad shallow bays on their landward side.

name: Virginia Beach
description: A city in Virginia with a small portion considered Atlantic drainage limited to the immediate Rudee Inlet drainage.

name: Rudee Inlet
description: An inlet that drains a very small portion of Virginia Beach to the Atlantic.

name: Albemarle Coastal watershed
description: A watershed that encompasses most of far southeast Virginia, containing the North Landing River and Northwest River.

name: North Landing River
description: One of the two larger watercourses in the Albemarle Coastal watershed that empties into Currituck Sound.

name: Northwest River
description: One of the two larger watercourses in the Albemarle Coastal watershed that empties into Currituck Sound and is the primary outflow for the Great Dismal Swamp.

name: Currituck Sound
description: A sound into which the North Landing River and Northwest River empty.

name: Back Bay
description: A bay that can be regarded as the northernmost extension of the Carolina Sounds complex, into which minor drainages in the Albemarle area enter.

name: Carolina Sounds complex
description: A system of sounds of which Back Bay is the northernmost extension.

name: North Carolina Outer Banks
description: Barrier islands along North Carolina's coast containing inlets that provide connection to the Atlantic, with inlet locations shifting over time.

name: Great Dismal Swamp
description: A large swamp whose primary outflow is the Northwest River.

name: Chowan watershed
description: A watershed defined by three main Coastal Plain drainages south of the James watershed, covering 3,675 square miles.

name: James watershed
description: A watershed north of the Chowan watershed in Virginia's Coastal Plain.

name: Nottoway River
description: One of three rivers that collectively drain the Chowan watershed and converge to form the Chowan River at the North Carolina-Virginia border.

name: Meherrin River
description: One of three rivers that collectively drain the Chowan watershed and converge to form the Chowan River at the North Carolina-Virginia border.

name: Blackwater River
description: One of three rivers that collectively drain the Chowan watershed and converge to form the Chowan River at the North Carolina-Virginia border.

name: Chowan River
description: A river formed by the convergence of the Nottoway, Meherrin, and Blackwater rivers in the North Carolina-Virginia border area.

name: North Carolina-Virginia border
description: The border area between North Carolina and Virginia where the three rivers converge to form the Chowan River.

name: Yadkin River
description: A river whose drainage extends into a very small portion of Virginia, limited to south-facing Blue Ridge slopes and foothills along the North Carolina line.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range with south-facing slopes and foothills along the North Carolina line that drain into the Yadkin River system.

name: North Carolina line
description: The border between Virginia and North Carolina where Carroll and Patrick counties are located.

name: Carroll County
description: A Virginia county along the North Carolina line that contains part of the Yadkin River drainage.

name: Patrick County
description: A Virginia county along the North Carolina line that contains part of the Yadkin River drainage.

name: Ararat River
description: A minor tributary of the Yadkin River that drains the Virginia portion of the Yadkin watershed.

name: Pee Dee River
description: A river that the Yadkin River meets and which flows south to empty into the Atlantic at Winyah Bay, South Carolina.

name: Winyah Bay
description: A bay in South Carolina where the Pee Dee River empties into the Atlantic.

name: South Carolina
description: The state where Winyah Bay is located and where the Pee Dee River empties into the Atlantic.

name: Atlantic
description: The ocean into which various Virginia drainage systems ultimately flow, either directly or through rivers like the Pee Dee.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic region containing the three main drainages south of the James watershed that form the Chowan watershed.

name: Roanoke River
description: One of the largest rivers in the Southeast, originating at the foot of the Alleghenies near Roanoke and following an east/southeasterly course across southern Virginia and North Carolina Piedmont.

name: Chesapeake
description: Referenced as having major tributaries that are larger than the Roanoke River.

name: Alleghenies
description: Mountain range where the Roanoke River originates at its foot.

name: Roanoke
description: City near where the Roanoke River originates at the foot of the Alleghenies.

name: Virginia
description: State through which the Roanoke River flows in its southern portion.

name: North Carolina
description: State through which the Roanoke River flows after Virginia, with sections flooded by impoundments.

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region through which the Roanoke River flows in both Virginia and North Carolina.

name: Kerr Reservoir
description: Large impoundment that floods sections of the Roanoke River's middle reaches.

name: Lake Gaston
description: Impoundment that floods sections of the Roanoke River's middle reaches.

name: Roanoke Rapids
description: Location in North Carolina below which the Roanoke River is under tidal influence.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region containing the tidal estuary portions of the Roanoke River.

name: Albemarle Sound
description: Body of water formed by the main stem of the Roanoke River at Batchelor Bay.

name: Batchelor Bay
description: Location where the Roanoke River forms the main stem of Albemarle Sound.

name: Dan River
description: Major tributary of the Roanoke River, described as a significant waterway sometimes referred to as the South Branch of the Roanoke.

name: Bannister River
description: Major tributary of the Roanoke River.

name: New River
description: Anomalous river that is the only major river in the United States flowing south to north and purportedly one of the oldest active rivers on earth.

name: United States
description: Country in which the New River is the only major river flowing south to north.

name: Tennessee
description: State along whose border with North Carolina the New River originates.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Terrain type through which the New River winds in a northeast orientation.

name: Radford
description: Area where the New River turns northwest and extremely broad floodplains are present.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Cultural period during which very substantial settlements were established in the expansive floodplain settings of the New River area.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: Geographic region that the New River cuts through via the New River Gorge.

name: New River Gorge
description: Gorge through which the New River cuts through the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Kanawha River
description: Major Ohio tributary that the New River meets after cutting through the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Ohio
description: River system of which the Kanawha River is a major tributary.

name: Holston River
description: One of the Tennessee River's primary headwater branches that drains the northeast-southwest trending valleys of far Southwest Virginia.

name: Tennessee River
description: Major river system for which the Holston comprises one of the primary headwater branches.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Region containing northeast-southwest trending valleys drained by the Holston River.

name: North Fork
description: One of the three forks of the Holston River separated by parallel running ridge lines.

name: Middle Fork
description: One of the three forks of the Holston River separated by parallel running ridge lines.

name: South Fork
description: One of the three forks of the Holston River separated by parallel running ridge lines.

name: Southwestern Virginia
description: Limestone region with characteristic valley features exhibited by the Holston River valleys.

name: French Broad River
description: River that the Holston meets in downtown Knoxville, with their confluence marking the beginning of the Tennessee River proper.

name: Knoxville
description: City where the Holston and French Broad Rivers meet, marking the beginning of the Tennessee River proper.

name: Clinch River
description: A river in the Ridge and Valley region that follows a southwest-northeast orientation dictated by the trending mountains, with broad and level floodplains.

name: Powell River
description: The western of two rivers that follows a very wide, nearly level valley floor at the interface between the limestone Ridge and Valley region and the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Holston River
description: A river system with forks that shares attributes with the Clinch and Powell Rivers and is considered a headwater branch of the greater Tennessee River.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A limestone geological region in Southwest Virginia characterized by southwest-northeast trending mountains.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: A region containing the limestone Ridge and Valley area that interfaces with the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: A region with extremely rugged terrain where Big Sandy tributaries drain deeply entrenched stream valleys generally lacking stable floodplain landforms.

name: Tennessee
description: The state where the Clinch and Powell Rivers merge.

name: Tennessee River
description: A major river system formed by the combination of the Clinch, Powell, Holston, and French Broad rivers as headwater branches.

name: French Broad River
description: One of the headwater branches of the greater Tennessee River along with the Clinch, Powell, and Holston rivers.

name: Big Sandy River
description: A river system that lies outside Virginia but has major headwater tributaries within the Commonwealth.

name: Levisa Fork
description: A major headwater tributary to the Big Sandy River within Virginia that drains the extremely rugged terrain of the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Tug Fork
description: A major headwater tributary to the Big Sandy River within Virginia that drains the extremely rugged terrain of the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Russel Fork
description: A major headwater tributary to the Big Sandy River within Virginia that drains the extremely rugged terrain of the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to Virginia, containing the headwater tributaries to the Big Sandy River.

name: Virginia
description: The state containing various river systems and the Coastal Plain Province with its diverse topographic settings.

name: Coastal Plain Province
description: A physically diverse region in Virginia containing a wide variety of topographic settings with divergent environmental conditions.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: A physically diverse region whose most prominent feature is the greater Chesapeake estuary.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: The most prominent feature of the Coastal Plain that provided Native Americans with rich marine resources, saltwater marshes, and abundant seasonal resources.

name: Fall Line
description: The boundary marking the upstream extent of tidal reaches of major tributary rivers to the Chesapeake Bay.

name: North America
description: The continent where the Chesapeake's abundance of seasonally available migrating/wintering waterfowl and spawning anadromous fish was perhaps unparalleled.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region characterized by uneven occurrence of ecologically productive settings with resource rich areas often concentrated geographically.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: A wetland area with a western margin that served as a settlement hotspot, containing nearly undifferentiated wetland expanses bordering Lake Drummond.

name: Suffolk Scarp
description: A topographic feature that rises from the wetland expanses bordering Lake Drummond, with well drained, fertile-soil terraces cut by numerous low order streams.

name: Lake Drummond
description: A lake bordered by wetland expanses, with numerous low order streams feeding into it from the Suffolk Scarp.

name: Nansemond River
description: A tidal river whose tidal reaches lie in proximity to the Suffolk Scarp, part of an exceptionally favorable environmental setting for Native American groups.

name: Elizabeth River
description: A tidal river whose tidal reaches lie in proximity to the Suffolk Scarp, later extended to by English settlement in the 1630s.

name: Lynnhaven River
description: A river along the mouth of the Chesapeake that was an apparent focal point of prehistoric settlement within the Outer Coastal Plain.

name: Broad Bay
description: A bay along the mouth of the Chesapeake that was an apparent focal point of prehistoric settlement within the Outer Coastal Plain.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major estuary with waters that contributed to extensive Native American settlement and use, particularly in the Northern Neck region.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: A subregion of the Coastal Plain where the Lynnhaven River and Broad Bay served as focal points of prehistoric settlement.

name: Tidewater
description: A region with interior, low lying uplands that appear to have been very sparsely settled for much of prehistory.

name: Chickahominy River
description: A second tier river with mid-drainage areas containing diverse and ecologically productive local environments that were extensively settled during Woodland subperiods.

name: Pamunkey River
description: A second tier river with mid-drainage areas containing diverse and ecologically productive local environments that were extensively settled during Woodland subperiods.

name: Mattaponi River
description: A second tier river with mid-drainage areas containing diverse and ecologically productive local environments that were extensively settled during Woodland subperiods.

name: Northern Neck
description: A Virginia region that experienced extensive Native American settlement due to fertile soils, freshwater streams, tidal creeks and marshes, particularly on the Potomac facing side.

name: Lower James River
description: The initial focal point of early English settlement in North America, with settlements extending along its shores almost to the Fall Line within years of Jamestown's founding.

name: Jamestown
description: The founding site of early English settlement in North America, established along the Lower James River.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary along the James River that marked the extent of early English settlement expansion.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A region where select areas were first settled by the English around the same time as Jamestown's founding.

name: York River
description: A river along whose south side English settlement took hold by the 1630s.

name: Powhatan Confederacy
description: A Native American confederacy whose final defeat allowed English settlements to push into the interior of the Middle Peninsula and up various rivers.

name: Middle Peninsula
description: A Virginia region into whose interior English settlements pushed following the final defeat of the Powhatan Indians.

name: Rappahannock River
description: A river up which English settlements pushed following the defeat of the Powhatan Indians.

name: Potomac River
description: A river up which English settlements pushed following the defeat of the Powhatan Indians, with much of its shoreline settled by the end of the 1650s.

name: Woodland Period
description: A cultural period during which the mid-drainage areas of second tier rivers were extensively settled.

name: Tidewater Plantation system
description: A plantation system based on large estates along the James River that formed the center point of power and wealth in the colony through the American Revolution.

name: American Revolution
description: A historical event through which the Tidewater plantations remained the center of colonial power and wealth, after which their influence waned.

name: Hampton Roads
description: A deep water port that became a major economic driver in Virginia by the early 19th century.

name: Falls
description: Location of rising industrial concerns that eclipsed Tidewater plantations as main economic drivers in Virginia by early 19th century.

name: American Civil War
description: Major American conflict by whose eve tobacco cultivation had shifted to the southern Piedmont.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Region that served as a breadbasket along with central and northern Piedmont by the American Civil War era.

name: Richmond
description: City where grain and cereal crops were milled and shipped worldwide via canal transport.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region with varied contemporary rural land use across Virginia.

name: James River
description: Major Virginia river with terraces containing prime farmland and crossing the Piedmont region.

name: Virginia Peninsula
description: Heavily forested area with continuing cultivation of prime farmland along James River terraces.

name: Middle Peninsula
description: Virginia region characterized by a mix of forest and agricultural uses.

name: York River
description: Virginia river with terraces containing prime farmland.

name: Rappahannock River
description: Major Virginia river with terraces containing prime farmland and crossing the Piedmont region.

name: Northern Neck
description: Virginia region containing some of the best agricultural lands in the Commonwealth, producing corn, soybean, sorghum, and other row crops.

name: Piedmont Province
description: Geologically complex and diverse region with implications for both prehistoric and historic use, containing stone outcrops, quartz deposits, and steatite sources.

name: Woodland sub-periods
description: Prehistoric cultural periods during which Piedmont floodplain settings were a clear focus of settlement.

name: Dan River
description: One of the principal large rivers crossing the Virginia Piedmont.

name: Potomac River
description: One of the principal large rivers crossing the Virginia Piedmont.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary that marked the western limits of English expansion during the 17th century.

name: Tidewater
description: Virginia region where tobacco cultivation was originally concentrated before shifting to the southern Piedmont.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic feature that served as the westernmost limit of English settlement during the 17th century and influenced settlement patterns.

name: Virginia
description: Colony and state where the Fall Line influenced settlement and development history.

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region settled during the first half of the 18th century, characterized by intensive farming and 19th century mining activity.

name: Richmond
description: Major town established along the falls that became a center of 19th century industrialization and water-powered development.

name: Petersburg
description: Area that became a center of 19th century industrialization driven by water power along the Falls.

name: Midlothian coal mines
description: Some of the earliest coal mines in the US, located west of Richmond in the central Piedmont.

name: James River
description: River used for loading coal from tramways onto boats and canal barges.

name: Arvonia quarries
description: Slate quarries that have produced high quality roofing slate from the mid-19th century onwards, continuing to operate today.

name: Southside Virginia
description: Region in the southern Piedmont known for tobacco production, though in far lesser quantities than during the 20th century.

name: Blue Ridge Province
description: Mountain region rich in seasonal resources that were extensively utilized during the prehistoric period, though with little long-term prehistoric settlement.

name: Big Meadows
description: High elevation bog, fen, or alpine meadow area in Shenandoah National Park that exemplifies the type of settings extensively visited by Native Americans.

name: Shenandoah National Park
description: National park that contains the Big Meadows locality, representing high elevation areas visited by Native Americans.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A major mountain chain that formed the western limits of the Virginia colony for much of the 18th century and offered Native Americans a wide range of lithic materials for tool manufacture.

name: Catoctin Mountain
description: A northern Blue Ridge outlier that was a major source of rhyolite extensively traded across the Middle Atlantic region during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: A geographic region across which rhyolite from Catoctin Mountain was extensively traded during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Middle Woodland period
description: A cultural period during which rhyolite from Catoctin Mountain was extensively traded across the Middle Atlantic region.

name: Virginia colony
description: A colonial entity whose western limits were formed by the Blue Ridge mountain chain for much of the 18th century.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A fertile valley where initial settlement of western Virginia spread quickly north to south, with most settlement originating in Maryland and Pennsylvania.

name: Maryland
description: A state from which most of the settlement in the Shenandoah Valley originated.

name: Pennsylvania
description: A state from which most of the settlement in the Shenandoah Valley originated.

name: northern Blue Ridge Province
description: A region that was extensively settled during the nineteenth century by mountain farmers who lived at the edge of subsistence.

name: Shenandoah National Park
description: A federal park whose creation involved forcible relocation of inhabitants from remaining farmsteads taken over by the federal government.

name: Washington DC area
description: A region containing population centers and markets near which mountain farmers along the northern Blue Ridge lived.

name: southern Blue Ridge
description: A region whose inhabitants remained isolated well into the 20th century by distance, terrain, and lack of reliable transportation.

name: Harpers Ferry
description: A strategic town established where the Potomac and Shenandoah break through the Blue Ridge, which became an important rail junction during the mid 19th century.

name: Potomac
description: A river that breaks through the Blue Ridge at the location where Harpers Ferry was established.

name: Shenandoah
description: A river that breaks through the Blue Ridge at the location where Harpers Ferry was established.

name: Roanoke
description: The largest city in Southwest Virginia, which owes its existence to initial railway development that took advantage of a low pass in the nearby Blue Ridge.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: A region where Roanoke is located as the largest city.

name: George Washington & Jefferson National Forests
description: Federal forest holdings that, along with Shenandoah National Park, span nearly the full length of the Virginia Blue Ridge from Front Royal to Bristol.

name: Front Royal
description: A location marking one end of the Virginia Blue Ridge span covered by federal holdings.

name: Bristol
description: A location marking one end of the Virginia Blue Ridge span covered by federal holdings.

name: Ridge and Valley Province
description: A geographic province whose physical attributes had a significant effect on human settlement during prehistoric times.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: A region of Virginia known for dense Native American populations during the Late Woodland period, with limestone-derived soils well suited to horticulture and extensive cave usage.

name: Late Woodland period
description: A cultural period when Southwest Virginia supported very dense Native American populations, characterized by village settlements and horticultural practices.

name: Woodland
description: A cultural period mentioned in the context of prehistoric settlement in Southwest Virginia.

name: Archaic period
description: A cultural period with sites well represented in the archaeological record of Southwest Virginia.

name: Late Archaic
description: A sub-period of the Archaic that is particularly well represented in the archaeological record of Southwest Virginia.

name: Commonwealth
description: A reference to Virginia, contrasted with Southwest Virginia regarding the rarity of Late Woodland village settlements outside floodplain settings.

name: Southwest Virginia Ridge and Valley
description: A specific physiographic region within Southwest Virginia that experienced intensive prehistoric settlement not limited to the Woodland period.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: The prominent feature within the Ridge and Valley region, with prehistoric settlement oriented to stream/river settings and later European settlement from Pennsylvania and Maryland.

name: Ridge and Valley region
description: A physiographic region of which the Shenandoah Valley is the prominent feature, characterized by agricultural use and specific settlement patterns.

name: Shenandoah
description: The river system with forks and attendant floodplains in the northern Valley that influenced prehistoric settlement patterns.

name: Pennsylvania
description: One of the origins of initial European settlement in the Shenandoah Valley, rather than from Virginia.

name: western Maryland
description: Along with Pennsylvania, an origin point for initial European settlement of the Shenandoah Valley.

name: Virginia
description: The state contrasted with Pennsylvania and western Maryland as NOT being the origin of initial European settlement in the Shenandoah Valley.

name: Civil War
description: A conflict during which control of the Shenandoah Valley remained of strategic importance due to its rich agricultural production.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic region mentioned in contrast to the Ridge and Valley regarding forest cover on valley floors.

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic region mentioned alongside the Coastal Plain in contrast to the Ridge and Valley regarding forest cover.

name: Appalachian Plateau Province
description: A geographic region in Virginia characterized by extremely rugged terrain with rare streamside and floodplain settings suitable for prehistoric settlement.

name: Virginia
description: The state containing areas of the Appalachian Plateau that were sparsely settled throughout most of the 19th century.

name: Eastern markets
description: Markets to which newly discovered coal was transported via railroad from the Appalachian Plateau region.

name: US National Forest
description: Federal lands in the mountainous areas of the Appalachian Plateau that are managed for timber and watershed protection.

name: 19th century
description: The time period during which the Appalachian Plateau areas of Virginia were sparsely settled throughout most of this century.

name: prehistoric period
description: The time period during which rock overhangs and shallow caves were likely utilized for shelter and/or habitation.

name: Chapter 2
description: A chapter titled 'Paleoclimate and Prehistoric Environmental Context' in a work about Virginia's first peoples.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region that is the focus of this archaeological work on first peoples.

name: Christopher Egghart
description: Author of the chapter on paleoclimate and prehistoric environmental context.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region and state where archaeological findings are being placed into environmental context.

name: eastern North America
description: Geographic region referenced in paleoclimate reconstructions and climate studies.

name: Greenland
description: Arctic location where ice core studies have been conducted that yield climate data applicable to Atlantic coastal areas.

name: Atlantic
description: Geographic region referenced in dendrochronological and ice core climate studies.

name: North Atlantic
description: Geographic region relevant to understanding prehistoric climate conditions.

name: Europe
description: Geographic region providing historical weather accounts from the High Middle Ages through seventeenth century.

name: High Middle Ages
description: Historical period referenced for European weather phenomena and climate conditions.

name: seventeenth century
description: Historical period through which European weather accounts are available for climate reconstruction.

name: Holocene
description: Geological time period during which floodplain morphologies and depositional patterns express precipitation trends.

name: Wisconsin
description: Major climate variation period mentioned in the context of paleoclimatic studies.

name: 8.2k Cold Event
description: A major cold climate event that occurred during the first half of the Holocene, related to glacial melt water release into the North Atlantic.

name: North Atlantic
description: Ocean basin where glacial melt water releases triggered dramatic cold events and where Bond Events are signaled by ice rafted sediment on the sea floor.

name: Holocene
description: The current geological period that experienced pronounced cool episodes independent of glacial-interglacial cycles and recurring Bond Events.

name: Bond Events
description: Episodic cool downs of the North Atlantic climate that recurred on a 1500 +/- 500 year interval throughout much of the Holocene, named after their discoverer.

name: Little Ice Age
description: A historically documented cold period that aligned with solar activity variations and is reflected in isotope ratios from Chinese peat bog studies.

name: Medieval Warm Period
description: A historically documented warm period that aligned with solar activity variations and is reflected in isotope ratios from Chinese peat bog studies.

name: Pleistocene-Holocene transition
description: A major global climate transition that was so sudden and dramatic that its effects could have been readily noticeable by humans living at the time.

name: Middle Archaic
description: An archaeological period whose second half roughly correlates with the Hypsithermal warming trend.

name: Hypsithermal
description: The most pronounced warming trend post-dating the Pleistocene-Holocene transition, roughly correlating with the second half of the Middle Archaic.

name: Medieval Warm period
description: A warming episode historically documented in Europe.

name: Roman Optimum
description: A warming episode dating from ca 250 B.C. to 400AD.

name: Holocene
description: A geological epoch during which major cold episodes occurred in the first half, with conditions that no longer exist today.

name: Europe
description: The continent where the Medieval Warm period was historically documented.

name: North American climate
description: The climate system that experienced a brief but sharp warming around 1200 BC and periodic cool downs triggered by glacial melt water releases.

name: Northern hemisphere
description: The region that is no longer subject to the atmospheric influence of the remnant Laurentide ice sheet and continental glacial lakes.

name: Laurentide ice sheet
description: A remnant ice sheet that previously influenced the atmosphere of the Northern hemisphere but no longer exists.

name: Greenland
description: The location of ice core data used as paleoenvironmental evidence for early-mid Holocene warm episodes.

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region where prehistoric environmental conditions and archaeological research are being studied, particularly in relation to sea level rise and climate change.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary marking the western limit of direct environmental influences from sea level rise in Virginia.

name: Holocene
description: Geological epoch during which inundation of the coastal plain occurred and the Chesapeake estuary was formed.

name: Susquehanna River
description: Major river system on the coastal plain that was inundated by rising sea levels, along with its tributaries creating the Chesapeake estuary.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: World's largest and most ecologically productive estuary created by Holocene inundation of the Susquehanna River system, with development largely complete by the end of the Late Archaic.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: Prehistoric cultural period by the end of which the development of the Chesapeake estuary was largely complete.

name: Archaic-Woodland transition
description: Cultural transition period that postdated prehistoric adaptations to formative environmental changes related to sea level rise.

name: Wisconsin glacial episode
description: Glacial period that began approximately 40,000 years ago with dropping sea levels, reaching a low point between 18,000 and 14,000 years ago before ending with global warming.

name: Atlantic coastline
description: Eastern seaboard coastline that was actively pushed westward by rising sea levels following the inundation of the continental shelf.

name: North American interior
description: Geographic region where the earth's crust sagged under the weight of ice sheets and later lifted up through isostatic rebound.

name: Holocene period
description: Geological time period during which sea level changes affected the Chesapeake Bay region.

name: Delaware Bay
description: Coastal bay for which Kraft generated relative sea level curves used in Middle Atlantic Coastal Plain reconstructions.

name: Middle Atlantic Coastal Plain
description: Coastal region where sea level rise reconstructions have been conducted.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Major estuary formed by rising sea levels backing into the ancestral Susquehanna River, reaching modern configuration by 3000 BP.

name: Susquehanna River
description: Major river that was backed into by rising ocean waters, eventually leading to formation of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Potomac
description: Major tributary of the Susquehanna River affected by rising waters by 4500 BP.

name: Rappahannock
description: Major tributary of the Susquehanna River affected by rising waters by 4500 BP.

name: James River
description: Major tributary of the Susquehanna River whose lower section was embayed by around 5000 BP, with full tidal influence extending to the Falls.

name: Falls
description: Location on the James River where full tidal influence likely extended during the millennium following 5000 BP.

name: Lake Agassiz-Ojibway
description: Glacial lake whose catastrophic melt water release into the North Atlantic triggered the 8.2k climate event.

name: North Atlantic
description: Ocean region where catastrophic release of glacial melt waters occurred, triggering climate events.

name: North American
description: Continental region from which melt water was released, contributing to sea level rise.

name: Antarctic ice sheet
description: Ice sheet that underwent accelerated decay following resumption of the AMOC after the 8.2k cooling event.

name: 8.2k climate event
description: Climate event triggered by catastrophic release of Lake Agassiz-Ojibway glacial melt waters into the North Atlantic, occurring between 8.2 and 7.6 cal ka.

name: Atlantic meridional overturning circulation
description: Ocean circulation system that was interrupted by melt water, then vigorously resumed, resulting in inter-hemisphere warming and Antarctic ice sheet decay.

name: Atlantic Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where rivers responded to sea level rise during the mid-Holocene by meandering and cutting multiple braided channels.

name: mid-Holocene
description: A chronological period when Atlantic Coastal Plain rivers responded to sea level rise-induced reduction of gradient by meandering and cutting multiple braided channels.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A body of water that developed as a result of rising sea levels, transforming Coastal Plain tributaries from flowing rivers to broad tidal estuaries.

name: Atlantic barrier islands
description: Geographic features where archaeological sites have been lost due to rising sea levels and wave/tidal action along transgressive shorelines.

name: Paleoindian
description: An early chronological period whose coastal occupations were lost due to sea level rise and inundation of former coastal sites.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A chronological period whose coastal occupations were lost due to sea level rise and inundation of former coastal sites.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period that had major prime locations for settlement that were lost due to sea level rise along the Chesapeake Bay and Atlantic barrier islands.

name: Woodland period
description: A chronological period during which some low lying site locations may have been compromised by sea level rise.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major estuary with depths mostly between 10 and 20 feet, whose development involved rising ocean waters filling ancient river channels and surrounding terraces.

name: Tangier Sound
description: A shallow area on the eastern flank of the Chesapeake Bay, averaging approximately 5 to 10 feet in depth.

name: Pocomoke Sound
description: A shallow area on the eastern flank of the Chesapeake Bay, averaging approximately 5 to 10 feet in depth.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: A prehistoric period when shellfish beds and other estuarine resources became important to groups, preceded by several millennia the modern Chesapeake Bay development conditions.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary west of which researchers have studied Holocene floodplain depositional patterns.

name: Holocene
description: A geological epoch during which dynamic floodplain depositional patterns occurred in inland areas west of the Fall Line.

name: Pennsylvania
description: State referenced in the context of drainage systems and paleosol analysis by Vento et al.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Geographic region where floodplain morphology trends have been noted in archaeological studies.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period between 3000 BP and 2000 BP characterized by extended floodplain stability.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period between 4500 BP and 3000 BP that was more dynamic with alluviation dominant along larger drainages.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period broadly categorized as having slow, continuous floodplain aggradation.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period during which floodplain stability was noted in some locations, particularly in the latter part.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period during which alluviation is again evident according to Vento et al.

name: Eastern United States
description: Geographic region where paleoclimate studies have traditionally relied on pollen profiles.

name: Virginia
description: State where archaeologists have long cited synthetic work based on pollen core studies.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic location where Carbone conducted pollen core studies cited by archaeologists working in Virginia.

name: Carbone
description: Researcher who conducted synthetic work in 1976 based on pollen core studies in the Shenandoah Valley and other regional locales.

name: Carbone
description: Researcher whose paleoclimate work is referenced as a baseline for subsequent Middle Atlantic paleoclimate research.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region for which paleoclimate research and reconstructions have been conducted.

name: Delcourt and Delcourt 1985
description: Researchers who conducted pollen assemblage studies contributing to regional climate and vegetation histories.

name: Jacobson et al. 1987
description: Researchers who conducted studies contributing to regional climate and vegetation histories.

name: Watts 1979, 1980
description: Researcher who conducted studies on regional climate and vegetation histories.

name: Webb et al. 1987
description: Researchers who contributed to pollen assemblage studies for regional climate reconstruction.

name: Gaudreau 1988
description: Researcher who conducted pollen assemblage studies for climate and vegetation history.

name: Greenland
description: Location of ice cores analyzed for paleoclimatological research.

name: Atlantic
description: Ocean region referenced in context of dendrochronological database development.

name: Virginia
description: State that escaped landscape-scouring effects of glaciation and was inhabited by Paleoindians under different climate conditions.

name: New York
description: Northern boundary of the region that experienced landscape-scouring effects of glaciation.

name: Paleoindians
description: Early indigenous people who roamed Virginia and experienced unique climate and environmental conditions in North American human history.

name: North America
description: Continent where human history includes the unique Paleoindian period climate conditions.

name: Maine
description: Contemporary northern state whose forests resembled those that covered Virginia during the Paleoindian period.

name: Canada
description: Country whose forests resembled those that covered Virginia during the Paleoindian period.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountain region in Virginia where areas east were dominated by spruce, hemlock, and white pine, while mid-to high elevations experienced tundra-like conditions.

name: Inter-Allerod period
description: Paleoclimatic period characterized by significantly higher temperatures than glacial times but still 5-10 degrees centigrade cooler than modern temperatures.

name: Edwards and Merrill 1977
description: Researchers who studied temperature conditions during the Inter-Allerod period.

name: Mid-Glacial Period
description: Paleoclimatic period when eastern North America was significantly cooler and much drier than today.

name: eastern North America
description: Region that was significantly cooler and much drier during the Mid-Glacial Period compared to today.

name: Watts and Stuiver 1980
description: Researchers who studied climate conditions during the Mid-Glacial Period in eastern North America.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to Virginia in the context of reconstructing past climate regimes and environmental conditions.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region for which the generalized climate history is provided in the table.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Late Glacial
description: Climatic period from 15,000-13,000 years BP characterized by cold and wet conditions with tundra-like conditions along the ice front.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Pre-Boreal
description: Climatic period from 13,000-10,700 years BP characterized by cold and wet conditions with grasslands and boreal forest.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Boreal
description: Climatic period from 10,700-9200 years BP characterized by significant warming with diminishing spruce and fir and expanding hardwoods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Atlantic
description: Climatic period from 9200-6600 years BP characterized by development of modern climate conditions and expanding hardwood forests.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Sub-Boreal
description: Climatic period from 6,600-2,000 years BP characterized by warming and drying climate with oak-hickory forests dominating.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Subatlantic
description: Climatic period from 2,000 years BP to present characterized by general and progressive cooling trend with significant precipitation shifts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Carbone
description: Author cited as the source for the generalized climate history table, publication dated 1976.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Cactus Hill
description: Archaeological site where macrobotanicals were recovered showing white pine established in southeastern Virginia by 15,000 BP.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: southeastern Virginia
description: Geographic region where white pine was established by 15,000 BP and where vegetative communities are analyzed.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: McWeeney
description: Co-author of 2001 publication analyzing macrobotanicals from Cactus Hill site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Kellogg
description: Co-author of 2001 publication analyzing macrobotanicals from Cactus Hill site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Whitehead
description: Researcher who conducted pollen analysis for Dismal Swamp cores in 1972 and 1981 publications.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Location where pollen analysis cores were taken to study vegetative communities for the period 10,600-8200 BP.

name: Younger Dryas
description: Abrupt cooling and drying period around 12,900 BP that ended around 11,500 years ago, affecting the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Northern Europe
description: Region initially thought to be the only area affected by the Younger Dryas cooling event.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Northern Hemisphere
description: Geographic region where effects of the Younger Dryas cooling were strongly felt.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: North Atlantic
description: Region where thermohaline circulation changes were a principal driver in the Younger Dryas climate reversal.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Paleoindian
description: Cultural period from 11,500-10,900 BC characterized by post-glacial cool conditions with warming trend.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Late Paleo Transition
description: Cultural period from 10,900-8000 BC during the Younger Dryas with sharp reversal in warming followed by rapid warming.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Early Archaic
description: Cultural period from 8000-6500 BC during the Boreal period with significant warming and dry episodes.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period from 6500-2500 BC during the Atlantic period with initial cooling, 8.2k Event, then Hypsithermal peak.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period from 2500-1100 BC during Sub-boreal period with some amelioration and periodic instability.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period from 1100 BC-AD 250 during Sub-boreal to Subatlantic transition with initial warm then sharp cooling.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period from AD 250-900 during Subatlantic period with moderately cool and moist conditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period from AD 900-1650 during Medieval Warm Period and Little Ice Age with slight warming then cooling.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Medieval Warm Period
description: Climatic period characterized by slight warming with generally more stable conditions during Late Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Little Ice Age
description: Climatic period characterized by cool conditions with severe droughts and variable precipitation during Proto/Early Historic times.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Proto/Early Historic
description: Cultural period occurring during the Little Ice Age with cool conditions and variable precipitation.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: Virginia
description: State referenced in the context of prehistoric research and paleoclimate applicability.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 2.1. Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic

name: 8.2k Event
description: Climatic event occurring during the Middle Archaic period causing initial cooling during the Atlantic climatic period.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Peak warm and dry climatic conditions occurring during the Middle Archaic period after the 8.2k Event.
