name: Anderson 2001
description: Author referenced as source for information about climate episodes and cultural transitions.

name: Late Paleo/Transition
description: Cultural sub-period identified to accommodate distinct climate episodes including the Younger Dryas.

name: Protohistoric/Historic
description: Cultural sub-period identified to accommodate distinct climate episodes including the Little Ice Age.

name: Younger Dryas
description: Major climate episode characterized by a sharp reversal in Northern Hemisphere warming that was coeval with final megafauna extinction.

name: Little Ice Age
description: Major climate episode used to define one of the cultural sub-periods mentioned in the document.

name: Lake Agassiz
description: Large glacial lake whose drainage diversion from Gulf of Mexico to St. Lawrence valley impacted ocean circulation and climate.

name: Gulf of Mexico
description: Original drainage destination for Lake Agassiz before diversion to the Atlantic.

name: St. Lawrence valley
description: Valley where Lake Agassiz drainage was diverted, leading to climate changes.

name: Atlantic
description: Ocean into which Lake Agassiz drainage was diverted through the St. Lawrence valley.

name: North Atlantic
description: Ocean region where thermohaline circulation was impacted by climate forcing mechanisms.

name: Late Glacial
description: Period preceding the Holocene, marking the transition when Younger Dryas climate reversal occurred.

name: Holocene
description: Current geological epoch that began after the Late Glacial period and Younger Dryas climate reversal.

name: Northern Hemisphere
description: Geographic region where warming reversal occurred during the Younger Dryas period.

name: Clovis culture
description: Essentially continental wide culture that was replaced by more diverse, regional traditions following megafauna extinction.

name: Early Archaic
description: Cultural period from 8000-6500 BC characterized by dramatic warming and spread of warm-adapted tree species.

name: Eastern North America
description: Geographic region that experienced dramatic warming following the Younger Dryas period.

name: Southeast
description: Region from which warm-adapted tree species like oak and hickories spread northward.

name: New England
description: Region where evidence of climate drying includes reduced lake levels and sediment hiatuses.

name: Virginia
description: State near which Carolina bays in the Delaware Coastal Plain showed evidence of significant water level drops.

name: Delaware Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region containing Carolina bays that documented significant water level drops during 11,000-6000 BP.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Location that experienced pronounced water level drops around 10,000 BP with breaks in pollen deposition.

name: Rockyhock Bay
description: Depression in North Carolina that experienced water level drops around 10,000 BP similar to Dismal Swamp.

name: North Carolina
description: State containing Rockyhock Bay depression that showed evidence of climate drying around 10,000 BP.

name: Browns Pond
description: Location in Augusta County with depositional hiatus zone dated to between circa 9200 to 7900 BP.

name: Augusta County
description: County containing Browns Pond, which showed evidence of depositional hiatus during Early Archaic period.

name: South Carolina Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where the change from boreal to deciduous hardwood forest was underway around 13,000 years ago.

name: Indian Creek site
description: Archaeological site in Prince George's County, Maryland that provided one of the more complete Early to Middle Holocene pollen sequences for the region.

name: Prince George's County
description: County in Maryland where the Indian Creek site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where Prince George's County and the Indian Creek site are located.

name: New Castle County
description: County in Delaware where pollen analysis showed oak-hickory forest conditions were fully established by 5800 BP.

name: Delaware
description: State where New Castle County is located and oak-hickory forest conditions were established by 5800 BP.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where forest transitions occurred, part of the broader pattern moving west across the Piedmont.

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region west of the Virginia Coastal Plain that experienced similar temporal progression in forest composition.

name: Appalachian summits
description: Mountain region where spruce and hemlock stands can still be found within sheltered coves today.

name: Early Archaic
description: Cultural period that experienced some of the most pronounced climate differences in North American prehistory, bracketed by the Younger Dryas cold period and peak post-glacial warm/dry conditions.

name: Younger Dryas
description: Period of extreme cold that marked one boundary of the Early Archaic period.

name: Paleoindian period
description: Cultural period preceding the Early Archaic, characterized by forests similar to terminal glacial times.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary west of which remnant boreal forests of spruce and fir covered higher elevations during the late Paleoindian period.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountain region east of which oak hickory forests dominated the uplands by the mid-point of the Early Archaic.

name: Virginia
description: State where forest transitions occurred east of the Blue Ridge, mirroring contemporary conditions by the mid-point of the Early Archaic.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period essentially coeval with the Mid-Holocene peak in warmth/dryness.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Mid-Holocene episode of peak warmth and dryness coeval with the Middle Archaic period.

name: North American prehistory
description: Broad temporal context during which the Early Archaic experienced some of the most pronounced climate differences.

name: Middle Archaic
description: An archaeological period characterized by Hypsithermal climate conditions with peak warmth/dryness followed by a cooling period.

name: Hypsithermal
description: A climatic period of maximum postglacial warmth and aridity from 7500-5000 BP, characterized by seasonal extremes.

name: Browns Pond
description: A pond in Augusta County containing zone BR-4 deposits that post-date circa 7900 BP with significant charcoal amounts.

name: Augusta County
description: A county location where Browns Pond is situated, containing important paleoenvironmental evidence.

name: Great Plains
description: A region mentioned in agreement with climate reconstructions for the postglacial warmth period.

name: Southeast
description: A region where warm adapted pine species expanded dramatically during the Middle Archaic, replacing oak forest.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A valley region with floor portions that may have supported open grasslands during precipitation-poor conditions.

name: Indian Creek Site
description: An archaeological site where goldenrod and cinnamon fern were reported for contexts bracketed between ca. 7660 and 5000 BP.

name: 8.2k Cold Event
description: A sudden and severe cold period between 8400 and 8000 BP linked to the final melting of the Laurentide Ice Sheet.

name: Laurentide Ice Sheet
description: A remnant ice sheet whose final melting was linked to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: Agassiz glacial lake
description: One of the massive glacial lakes that catastrophically drained, contributing to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: Ojibway glacial lake
description: One of the massive glacial lakes that catastrophically drained, contributing to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: eastern North America
description: The broader region characterized by Hypsithermal conditions during the balance of the Middle Archaic period.

name: Mid-Holocene
description: A climatic period characterized by dynamism, including significant cooling at the start of the Middle Archaic.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period dating to 2500-1000 BC characterized by ameliorated climate conditions compared to the Middle Archaic.

name: Sub-boreal
description: Climatic period associated with the Late Archaic, characterized by somewhat ameliorated conditions.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period preceding the Late Archaic, associated with different climate conditions.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountain region where chestnut groves became well established by 3500 BP along mid-to high elevation benches and ridge lines.

name: Southeast Virginia
description: Region that would have supported mixed pine and hardwood forests during the Late Archaic period.

name: Piedmont
description: Region that would have supported mixed pine and hardwood forests during the Late Archaic period.

name: Middle Atlantic Coastal Plain
description: Region where researchers have noted the presence of aeolian soils over prehistoric site surfaces dating to around 4500 BP to 3000 BP.

name: Delmarva
description: Region particularly noted for aeolian soils over prehistoric site surfaces dating to around 4500 BP to 3000 BP.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Region characterized by unstable floodplain surfaces during the Late Archaic period.

name: North Atlantic
description: Climate region that was warmer around 1200 BC than any time since 7500 BC according to Greenland ice core data.

name: Subatlantic
description: Climatic period characterized as a shift from warm/dry to cool/moist conditions, with onset around 3000 BP.

name: Delaware River
description: River with site locations that experienced increased sedimentation for the period 3200-2500 BP.

name: Delaware watershed
description: Watershed region studied for floodplain stability following 3000 BP.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period associated with floodplain surface stability along the Potomac drainage and other Virginia locations.

name: Potomac drainage
description: Drainage system that experienced floodplain surface stability during the Early Woodland period.

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region with locations that experienced Early Woodland floodplain surface stability.

name: mid-post-glacial xerothermic
description: Term used by Custer to describe a very warm and drought prone period within the Sub-boreal period.

name: Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period in eastern North America with dendrochronological climate data available for reconstruction.

name: Southwest United States
description: Geographic region where dendrochronological studies were largely limited before recent East Coast developments.

name: East Coast
description: Geographic region where recent dendrochronological studies have established robust sequences.

name: Nottoway River
description: River in southeast Virginia where quality bald cypress dendrochronological data has been obtained.

name: Virginia
description: State containing the Nottoway River where dendrochronological data was collected from bald cypress.

name: Ontario
description: Canadian province where white cedar dendrochronological data has been obtained.

name: Northern Europe
description: Geographic region with comprehensive Irish oak and other dendrochronological sequences applicable to broader studies.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period that experienced several sudden cooling episodes around 1000 BC and other climate events, with a flattening radiocarbon curve between cal 800 BC and 400 cal BC.

name: Greenland
description: Arctic region providing ice core data that correlates with European tree ring data showing Early Woodland cooling events.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period preceding the Early Woodland, which had a somewhat warmer and less moist climate.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Climatic period with remnant grasslands or broken forest that would have filled in during the moister, more stable Early Woodland weather patterns.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period from AD 250-900 that experienced relatively moderate Subatlantic conditions continuing from the Early Woodland, with increased precipitation and flooding.

name: Subatlantic
description: Climatic period that continued from Early Woodland into Middle Woodland with relatively moderate conditions.

name: Scandic Phase
description: Cooler and moister phase within the Subatlantic extending from 1750 BP to 1200 BP, linked to dynamic floodplain conditions during the Middle Woodland.

name: Subatlantic climate period
description: A climate period that appears to have had two distinct components, roughly corresponding to the first and second half of the Middle Woodland.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period with first half characterized by relatively stable and mild conditions, second half experiencing dramatic cool events.

name: Roman Optimum
description: A climate period on the east side of the Atlantic that may be linked to the first half of the Middle Woodland's mild conditions.

name: Scandic phase
description: A phase of the Subatlantic that transitioned rapidly to the Medieval Warm Period beginning around AD 800.

name: Medieval Warm Period
description: Climate period beginning around AD 800 that lasted almost 500 years, characterized by mild weather and predictable precipitation.

name: High Middle Ages
description: An era closely tied to the Medieval Warm Period that witnessed rapid population growth, increased agricultural production, and social and political advances.

name: Nottoway River
description: River where tree ring data from bald cypress indicates substantial inter-annual and decadal variance of growing season moisture from AD 1185 to ca. AD 1585.

name: Delaware watershed
description: Watershed where floodplain morphologies suggest a continuation of active fluvial conditions coeval with the latter half of the Middle Woodland.

name: Little Ice Age
description: Climate period that began around AD 1300, characterized by cooling conditions with the 14th century AD having the coldest conditions in 700 years.

name: Greenland
description: Location where Norse settlement collapsed during the Little Ice Age and where ice cores have been analyzed to quantify the climate episode.

name: London
description: City described by William Shakespeare and contemporary writers as snow and ice bound during the Little Ice Age.

name: Flanders
description: European region depicted in period genre paintings showing frozen rivers and canals during the Little Ice Age.

name: North Atlantic
description: Region where climate cooling during the Little Ice Age affected the temperature-sensitive cod fishery.

name: Northern Europe
description: Region where dendrochronological sequences have been analyzed to place quantitative parameters on the Little Ice Age climate episode.

name: Western Europe
description: Region where Little Ice Age conditions are well documented historically, with uncertainty about analogs in eastern North America.

name: eastern North America
description: Region where there is uncertainty about the extent to which Little Ice Age conditions had analogs compared to Western Europe.

name: Virginia
description: Location of early English settlement where written accounts refer to harsh winters and generally difficult conditions during the Little Ice Age.

name: William Shakespeare
description: Writer who, along with contemporary writers, described a snow and ice bound London during the Little Ice Age.

name: North America
description: Geographic region where initial English settlement occurred, mentioned in context of colonial ventures and drought conditions.

name: Roanoke Island
description: Location of a colonial venture in 1585 that experienced the worst drought conditions in 800 years, which may have contributed to the colony's demise.

name: Jamestown
description: English colonial settlement founded in 1607 that experienced severe drought conditions and appalling death rates during its early years.

name: Roanoke Island colonial venture
description: Colonial venture of 1585 that experienced severe drought conditions and ultimately failed, possibly due in part to these environmental stresses.

name: founding of Jamestown
description: Colonial settlement established in 1607 that coincided with a seven-year drought period from 1606-1612.
Year of the Roanoke Island colonial venture that experienced the worst drought conditions in 800 years.
Year of Jamestown's founding, which coincided with the beginning of a severe seven-year drought period.
Period of severe drought that coincided with Jamestown's founding and early years, contributing to high death rates.

name: Virginia
description: State where Paleoindians roamed and experienced different climate and ecological conditions than today.

name: Paleoindian
description: Prehistoric period characterized by post-glacial warming conditions followed by climate reversals, with people experiencing very different environmental conditions than today.

name: Maine
description: Northern state whose forests resembled those that likely existed in Virginia during Paleoindian times.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where major rivers flowed in narrow channels toward an Atlantic shoreline located much further east during Paleoindian times.

name: Atlantic
description: Ocean whose shoreline lay as much as 100 miles further east during Paleoindian times.

name: Early Archaic
description: Prehistoric period that followed Paleoindian times, characterized by environmental changes and the fade out of Paleoindian lifeways.

name: Archaic period
description: Prehistoric period characterized by numerous and diverse traditions that arose as Paleoindian lifeways faded out.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Highly productive massive estuary that developed and was largely complete by the end of the Archaic, offering Native Americans unprecedented resource opportunities.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary up to which freshwater tidal embayment of major tributary rivers extended in the Chesapeake Bay system.

name: Native Americans
description: Indigenous peoples who benefited from unprecedented resource opportunities offered by the newly developed estuarine environments of the Chesapeake Bay.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Pronounced climatic episode that was warm and drought prone, ecologically stressing but unlikely to have significantly altered overall forest makeup long-term.

name: 8.2k Cold Event
description: Centuries-long pronounced climatic episode that was ecologically stressing but unlikely to have significantly altered overall forest makeup long-term.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Geographic region characterized by resilient temperate, mixed forests that could withstand pronounced climatic episodes.

name: Younger Dryas
description: Climate shift that may have been an exception in terms of cultural responses to periodic climate shifts.

name: Late Archaic
description: Later part of the Archaic period, prior to which cultural responses to climate shifts would likely have been muted.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Part of the Archaic period characterized by relatively small and mobile populations with generalized hunting and foraging subsistence economy.

name: Woodland cultures
description: Later prehistoric cultures with larger populations, semi-sedentary settlement, and increasing reliance on cultigens, making them more susceptible to climate shifts.

name: Late Archaic period
description: A cultural period characterized by population growth, increasing sedentism, and intensification of wild food resource exploitation.

name: Hypsithermal
description: A warm and dry climatic period that preceded the Late Archaic.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by stable floodplain surfaces and cool, moderating conditions that may have prompted active floodplain clearing for horticultural practices.

name: Subatlantic period
description: A climatic period characterized by moister conditions that resulted in forest closing and entrenchment of climax conditions.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period during which the first half appears conducive to horticultural production, but which experienced a sudden cold episode beginning in the 6th century.

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period during which maize horticulture was adopted by Native American groups, though societies remained susceptible to climate changes.

name: Woodland period
description: A broad cultural period encompassing Early, Middle, and Late Woodland phases, characterized by developing social complexity and tribal identities.

name: Archaic cultures
description: Cultural groups preceding Late Archaic peoples, characterized by lesser numbers, dispersed settlement, and pure hunting/foraging lifeway.

name: Late Archaic peoples
description: Cultural groups of the Late Archaic period who focused settlement on geographically restricted, resource rich environmental zones and were more sensitive to environmental changes.

name: Late Woodland societies
description: Cultural groups that adopted maize horticulture but remained susceptible to climate changes due to the specific requirements of maize cultivation.

name: Native American groups
description: Indigenous groups that adopted maize horticulture during the Late Woodland period to produce high quality food stores.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A geographic region where even slight changes in weather patterns could result in significantly reduced frost free seasons affecting maize cultivation.

name: maize
description: A cultivated crop that requires a specific number of frost free days to mature and was adopted by Late Woodland peoples despite risks of crop failure.

name: Little Tennessee River
description: River whose floodplains were largely cleared by Native Americans by at least 4000 BP, as evidenced by archaeological data showing increased use of cedar and pine for fuel and increased charcoal in sediment cores.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Bay area where significant increases in metals in bottom sediments over the last 4,000 years are attributed to extensive burning by Native Americans that released metals from soils and vegetation.

name: Eastern North America
description: Region recognized as one of many loci for independent plant domestication, where Native Americans promoted pioneering indigenous seed-bearing species through intensive exploitation and nascent horticultural practices.

name: Chapman
description: Researcher who provided data suggesting Little Tennessee River floodplains were cleared by 4000 BP and noted sharp increases in charcoal in river sediment cores.

name: Delcourt
description: Researcher who collaborated in providing data on forest clearing and charcoal increases in the Little Tennessee River region around 4000 BP.

name: Brush
description: Researcher who noted significant increases in metals in Chesapeake Bay bottom sediments over the last 4,000 years and attributed them to extensive burning by Native Americans.

name: Stevens
description: Researcher who synthesized regional data arguing that human intervention, particularly burning and clearing of floodplains, is reflected in the geomorphologic record through increased stream sedimentation and localized aeolian soil deposition.

name: Bruce D. Smith
description: Researcher who convincingly argued that Eastern North America should be recognized as a locus for independent plant domestication, where intensive exploitation of indigenous seed-bearing plants developed into nascent horticultural practices before the introduction of tropical cultigens.

name: Vento
description: Researcher who studied the burning and modification of forests that appeared to have increased around AD 0.

name: Chenopodium sp.
description: Indigenous seed-bearing plant species that was intensively exploited by Native Americans and developed into nascent horticultural practices in Eastern North America.

name: Iva annua
description: Indigenous seed-bearing plant species that was intensively exploited by Native Americans and developed into nascent horticultural practices in Eastern North America.

name: 4000 BP
description: Time period by which the floodplains of the Little Tennessee River were largely cleared by Native Americans, marked by dramatic increases in cedar and pine fuel use and charcoal in sediment cores.

name: AD 0
description: Time period around which the burning and modification of forests by Native Americans appeared to have increased.

name: Delaware watershed
description: A watershed area where alluvial deposits younger than 2000 years show marked presence of grass and chenopodium remains, suggesting anthropogenic disruption of local floodplain environments.

name: Delmarva
description: A region where Brush conducted analysis of St. Jones River sediment cores, showing evidence of increased fire activity between 2000 and 1500 years ago.

name: St. Jones River
description: A river just downstream from Dover, Delaware, where sediment core analysis revealed a dramatic 400 percent spike in charcoal dating between 2000 and 1500 years ago.

name: Dover
description: A location in Delaware, upstream from where St. Jones River sediment cores were analyzed by Brush.

name: Delaware
description: A state where Dover is located and where the St. Jones River sediment analysis was conducted.

name: Scandic Phase
description: A cooler/wetter phase of the Subatlantic period that corresponds with the four-fold increase in charcoal documented in St Jones River sediments.

name: Subatlantic
description: A chronological period that includes the Scandic Phase, during which significant increases in burn frequencies occurred.

name: Holocene
description: A geological epoch characterized by dynamic climate changes, particularly during the end of Paleoindian times and the first half of the Archaic period.

name: Paleoindian
description: An early cultural period in North America that experienced dramatic climate shifts at its end.

name: Archaic
description: A cultural period following Paleoindian times that experienced significant climate changes during its first half and had distinct cultural adaptations.

name: Woodland
description: A cultural period that had different cultural adaptations compared to the Archaic period.

name: North America
description: The continent where Native American cultures experienced dynamic Holocene climate changes and developed various cultural adaptations.

name: eastern North America
description: A region that experienced clearly dynamic Holocene climate conditions.

name: Atlantic
description: A region mentioned in the context of future climate studies that will benefit North American archaeologists.

name: Arctic
description: A region where future climate studies are expected to yield data beneficial to North American archaeologists.

name: Cliff Boyd
description: Author of the archaeological plan and research design for the Paleoindian Period in Virginia.

name: Virginia
description: State where the archaeological research and Paleoindian occupations discussed in this chapter are located.

name: North America
description: Continent where the earliest periods of human occupation, including Virginia, are located.

name: Paleoindian Period
description: The earliest period of human occupation in North America and Virginia, dating from ca. 16,000-10,000 BP.

name: Pre-Clovis
description: Cultural period preceding Clovis occupations, represented at sites like Cactus Hill with dates averaging 17,650 cal BP.

name: Clovis
description: A 200-year long cultural horizon that rapidly spread across North America, traditionally dated to 11,500-10,900 14C yr BP but now narrowed to 11,050-10,800 14C yr BP.

name: Mid-Paleo
description: A middle Paleoindian period mentioned as part of the general time span of Paleoindian occupations in Virginia.

name: Late Paleoindian

name: Late/Terminal Pleistocene epoch
description: Geological epoch during which Paleoindian occupations occurred in Virginia.

name: Cactus Hill site
description: Archaeological site in Virginia containing pre-Clovis blade component features dating to approximately 17,650 cal BP and Clovis hearth dating to 10,920 +/- 250 14C yr BP.

name: Brook Run

name: Culpepper County
description: County in Virginia where the Brook Run archaeological site is located.

name: North Carolina
description: Neighboring state to Virginia where the Late Paleoindian Hardaway-Dalton Complex has been studied.

name: projectile points
description: Temporally diagnostic stone tools that are used to identify cultural periods, though none were found in association with dated contexts at Brook Run.

name: blade component
description: Pre-Clovis lithic technology found in features at Cactus Hill site.

name: Clovis hearth
description: Fire feature at Cactus Hill site associated with Clovis occupation, dated to 10,920 +/- 250 14C yr BP.

name: jasper quarry
description: Type of archaeological site represented by Brook Run, where jasper stone was extracted for tool making.

name: Virginia
description: State containing various physiographic provinces and archaeological sites, remained unglaciated during the Late Wisconsin period but was affected by proximity to ice sheets.

description: Easternmost physiographic province of Virginia.

description: Physiographic province in Virginia located west of the Piedmont.

name: Ridge and Valley Province

name: Cumberland Mountains
description: Mountain range found in portions of extreme southwestern Virginia.

name: Lake Champlain-Hudson River
description: Location of ice lobe that had significant effect on Virginia's environment during glacial period.

description: Geographic boundary, 290 km north of the Polar Frontal Zone during Wisconsin maximum.

name: Gulf of Mexico
description: Source of warm, moist air that converged with cold arctic air to produce precipitation during glacial period.

name: Virginia
description: State where periglacial environments existed during the Late Wisconsin glacial stage, particularly in higher elevations of the Appalachians.

name: Late Wisconsin glacial stage
description: Glacial period during which periglacial environments affected Virginia's geomorphology and sea levels dropped significantly.

name: Appalachians
description: Mountain range where periglacial environments existed during the Late Wisconsin, with areas of continuous or discontinuous permafrost.

name: northern Virginia
description: Region that was in the permafrost zone and had areas of continuous or discontinuous permafrost during the Late Wisconsin.

name: North America
description: Continental region for which Haynes divided hydrologic changes into broad stratigraphic units during glacial periods.

name: Holocene
description: Current geological epoch characterized by alternating cycles of cutting and filling of streambeds.

name: Atlantic continental shelf
description: Underwater area that was exposed during the Wisconsin stage due to lowered sea levels, forming a poorly drained plain.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Body of water that would have been a large river valley during the Wisconsin stage when sea levels were lowered.

name: Virginia coast
description: Coastal area where sea level drop of 60-80 meters may have occurred due to crustal depression from the Laurentide ice sheet.

name: Laurentide ice sheet
description: Large glacial formation whose weight caused depression of the earth's crust and whose melting produced variable effects on Atlantic coastal shorelines.

name: New Jersey
description: Coastal area that rose during 16,000-10,000 BP due to isostatic rebound from glacier removal, then was gradually submerged again.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountainous province in Virginia where cold temperatures at higher elevations may have inhibited human occupation during glacial periods.

name: Late Wisconsin glacial stage
description: A glacial period during which the continental shelf was exposed and there was reduced seasonal temperature variation, providing opportunities for Paleoindians.

name: Paleoindians
description: Early Native American peoples who experienced unique environmental opportunities during the Late Wisconsin glacial stage and lived in an evolving floral environment between 12,000-10,000 BP.

name: Late Pleistocene
description: A geological epoch whose inland environments in North America lack modern counterparts and were characterized by very diverse and complex plant and animal communities.

name: North America
description: The continent whose Late Pleistocene inland environments lacked modern counterparts.

name: Laurentide glacier
description: A major glacier whose southern boundary was bordered by a belt of tundra characterized by specific vegetation types.

name: Appalachians
description: Mountain range along whose crests the tundra belt extended south to the Great Smoky Mountains, and where relict populations of spruce and fir continued to exist at high elevations.

name: Great Smoky Mountains
description: Mountains that marked the southern extent of the tundra belt that extended along the Appalachian crests.

name: Virginia
description: State that was dominated by boreal forest during the glacial maximum and experienced vegetation changes as the climate warmed, with mixed forests established by 12,500 BP.

name: eastern United States
description: Region extending down to Georgia that was dominated by boreal forest including jack pine, spruce and fir species during the glacial period.

name: Georgia
description: Southern extent of the region dominated by boreal forest during the glacial maximum.

name: central and eastern Canada
description: Region where jack pine, spruce and fir species that dominated Virginia during glacial times are common today.

name: Gulf Coast
description: Coastal region that had a warm-temperate forest of oak, hickory, and southern pine during the glacial maximum.

name: lower Atlantic Coast
description: Coastal region that had a warm-temperate forest of oak, hickory, and southern pine during the glacial maximum.

name: Ridge and Valley of Virginia
description: Geographic region in Virginia where mixed conifer-northern hardwood forest may have been established by 12,500 BP.

name: Southwest Virginia Ridge and Valley
description: Specific region in Virginia where hemlock appears to have been a dominant species starting at 10,000 BP.

name: Eastern Woodlands
description: A major geographic and ecological region of North America where significant changes in plant and animal species occurred, affecting early hunter-gatherer populations.

name: North America
description: The continent where significant faunal extinctions occurred during the Late Wisconsin to Holocene transition period.

name: Late Wisconsin to Holocene transition
aliases: Late Wisconsin to Holocene transition 12,000 - 10,000 BP
description: A critical period 12,000-10,000 BP when 33 to 35 large animal species became extinct across North America.

name: Holocene
description: The geological epoch that began after the Late Wisconsin period, during which none of the extinct Ice Age creatures survived in Virginia.

name: Virginia
description: A region where none of the extinct Ice Age creatures survived into the Holocene period.

name: Late Wisconsin
description: A geological period characterized by megafauna that became extinct during the transition to the Holocene.

name: Ice Age
description: A geological period characterized by large animals including mastodon, wooly mammoth, and other megafauna that became extinct.

name: Paleoindian
description: A cultural period characterized by hunters who, according to the overkill hypothesis, intensively exploited Late Wisconsin megafauna.

name: Clovis
description: An archaeological culture dated to around 10,900 14C yr BP whose main florescence coincides with mammoth and mastodon extinctions.

name: Younger Dryas Glacial Event
description: A 1,400 year-long cold spell lasting from 13,000 to 11,600 cal BP that reversed terminal Pleistocene warming and dramatically affected human populations.

name: terminal Pleistocene

name: Pleistocene
description: Earlier geological period characterized by rapid climate shifts, preceding the Middle Archaic.

name: Holocene
description: Geological period that includes the Middle Archaic, characterized by postglacial warming and forest establishment.

name: Southeast
description: Region where transition to modern oak/hickory and mixed hardwood forests occurred before the Middle Archaic began.

name: Ridge and Valley province
description: Geographic region in Virginia where mixed oak-pine communities became established during the Middle Archaic.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where oak-hickory forest conditions were established during the Middle Archaic.

name: New Castle County
description: County in Delaware where pollen profiles suggest oak-hickory forest conditions were fully established by 5800 BP.

name: Delaware
description: State containing New Castle County where oak-hickory forests were established by 5800 BP.

name: Appalachians
description: Mountain range where spruce, white pine and other cold-adapted species retreated to the highest elevations during the Middle Archaic.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Mid-Holocene peak in warm conditions that was essentially coeval with the Middle Archaic period.

name: 8.2k Cold Event
description: A significant cooling episode that preceded the Hypsithermal around the beginning of the Middle Archaic, linked to melting of the Laurentide Ice Sheet.

name: Laurentide Ice Sheet
description: Remnant ice sheet whose final melting was linked to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: Agassiz
description: One of the massive glacial lakes whose catastrophic draining was linked to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: Ojibway
description: One of the massive glacial lakes whose catastrophic draining was linked to the 8.2k Cold Event.

name: North America
description: Continental region characterized by Hypsithermal conditions during the balance of the Middle Archaic period.

name: Delaware Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where a significant drop in water levels occurred between 11,000 BP to 6000 BP.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Wetland location that experienced a pronounced drop in water levels around 10,000 BP.

name: Rockyhock Bay
description: Bay depression in North Carolina that experienced water level drops around 10,000 BP.

name: North Carolina
description: State containing the Rockyhock Bay depression that experienced water level changes during the Middle Archaic.

name: Browns Pond
description: Pond in Augusta County with a depositional hiatus zone dated between circa 9200 to 7900 BP.

name: Augusta County
description: County containing Browns Pond, which shows evidence of depositional hiatus and charcoal deposits during the Middle Archaic.

name: Indian Creek V Site
description: Archaeological site just outside of Washington DC where sediment core samples were obtained from an abandoned stream channel for pollen analysis.

name: Washington DC
description: City referenced as the location near the Indian Creek V Site.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period to which the Zone 3 pollen assemblages from the Indian Creek V coring are relevant, characterized by oak, hazel, and alder dominance between circa 7700 BP and 5000 BP.

name: Zone 3
description: A stratigraphic zone in the Indian Creek V core samples containing pollen assemblages relevant to the Middle Archaic period.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Major estuary in eastern Virginia that underwent development and expansion during the Archaic period, with fresh-brackish transition occurring between 8.2 and 7.4 cal ka.

name: eastern Virginia
description: Geographic region where environmental conditions were shaped by modern forest communities, warm/dry climate, and Chesapeake Bay estuary expansion.

name: Chesapeake basin
description: The physical basin containing the Chesapeake Bay, with a stepped configuration that may have facilitated rapid up-basin extension of estuarine conditions.

name: Archaic period
description: Archaeological period during which settlement and subsistence patterns would have been significantly impacted by environmental changes including estuary development.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A cultural or chronological period in Virginia archaeology characterized by specific material culture traits that occur across large geographic areas.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain region in Virginia that forms one of the major geographic divisions for discussing Middle Archaic material culture.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region in Virginia that, along with the Fall Line, forms one of the major divisions for discussing Middle Archaic archaeology.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic feature in Virginia that is grouped with the Piedmont region for discussing Middle Archaic material culture.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia that, along with coastal zones, forms one of the major divisions for discussing Middle Archaic archaeology.

name: Virginia
description: The Commonwealth where Middle Archaic archaeological discussion is being addressed through major geographic divisions.

name: Middle Archaic Period
description: A chronological period in Virginia archaeology that saw increased settlement intensity and site components compared to the Early Archaic, with notable changes in settlement patterns and artifact distributions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: COVA
description: A publication from 1990 that provides foundational information on Virginia archaeology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: C.G. Holland
description: An archaeologist who conducted surveys in Albemarle County in 1970, whose collections provided site data for Parker's analysis.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Albemarle County
description: A county in Virginia where C.G. Holland conducted archaeological surveys, providing site data for analysis.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Bath County Pump Storage Facility
description: A location in Bath County where archaeological surveys were conducted by Geier and colleagues in the late 1970s and early 1980s.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Laboratory of Archaeology, University of Virginia
description: An archaeological research institution at the University of Virginia that investigated sites studied in Parker's analysis.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Early Archaic Period
description: A chronological period preceding the Middle Archaic, characterized by different settlement patterns and artifact distributions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Madison County
description: A county in Virginia that encompasses diverse physiographic settings from Piedmont lowlands to high elevation Blue Ridge, studied by Nash for settlement patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Piedmont Mesozoic Basin
description: A lowland physiographic region in the eastern part of Madison County.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Inner Piedmont
description: An upland physiographic region within Madison County.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Blue Ridge Mountains
description: A mountain range that includes peaks and summit ridgelines, with both low and high elevation settings in Madison County that saw intensive Middle Archaic settlement.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: LeCroy points
description: A type of projectile point characteristic of the initial Middle Archaic settlement, focused on higher order stream terraces and floodplains.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Morrow Mountain phase
description: A phase within the Middle Archaic that marked the beginning of intensive settlement of the high Blue Ridge and summit areas.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Arnold Valley
description: A topographically diverse valley situated along the Blue Ridge slope near the James River's entrance to the Piedmont, extensively settled throughout prehistory.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia that enters the Piedmont near Arnold Valley, serving as a key geographic reference point.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic province in Virginia where the James River enters from the Blue Ridge region.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: US Forest Service
description: A federal agency whose staff conducted wide-ranging archaeological surveys across the Blue Ridge and Ridge and Valley highlands.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A physiographic province with highland settings that were surveyed by US Forest Service staff and showed increasing settlement intensity throughout the Archaic.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Robert Ogle Collection
description: An artifact collection assembled from surface sites along the Nottoway River Fall Zone, with carefully recorded find locations that provided insights into Middle Archaic settlement patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Robert Ogle
description: An artifact collector who carefully recorded locations of finds along the Nottoway River Fall Zone and segregated finds accordingly.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Nottoway River Fall Zone
description: A geographic area along the Nottoway River where Robert Ogle collected artifacts from surface sites, particularly around its confluence with Stony Creek.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Stony Creek
description: A stream that confluences with the Nottoway River, where Kirk Serrated points showed peak occurrence and where gravelly shallows provided ideal locations for intercepting anadromous fish.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Kirk Serrated points
description: A type of projectile point that showed anomalous peak occurrence along Stony Creek, well upstream of the river confluence, associated with fish interception locations.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Morrow Mountain points
description: A type of projectile point that exhibited confluence-centric settlement patterns typical of the Early Archaic period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Stanly points
description: A type of projectile point that, along with Morrow Mountain points, exhibited confluence-centric settlement patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Guilford points
description: A type of projectile point that occurred with nearly equal frequency across different site settings, suggesting more generalized settlement patterns compared to earlier types.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Archaic Period
description: A broad chronological period in Virginia prehistory that includes both Early and Middle Archaic phases, characterized by changing settlement patterns and population levels.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Middle Archaic Research to Date

name: Nottoway River Fall Line
description: A geographic area along the Nottoway River where the fall line occurs, the site of extensive Middle Archaic archaeological investigations.

name: Joseph McAvoy
description: Archaeologist who conducted extensive work on Middle Archaic sites, particularly through the Nottoway River Survey.

name: Nottoway River Survey
description: Archaeological survey group that conducted systematic investigations of Middle Archaic sites along the Nottoway River.

name: Indian Creek V Site
description: A Middle Archaic site located in the Northern Virginia/Washington, D.C. Metro area that was systematically investigated.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region in Virginia where the Indian Creek V Site is located.

name: Washington, D.C.
description: Metropolitan area near Northern Virginia where the Indian Creek V Site is located.

name: Dust Cave
description: Archaeological site in Alabama with extensive and detailed Middle Archaic findings relevant to Southwest Virginia.

name: Alabama
description: State where Dust Cave is located, with Middle Archaic findings relevant to Southwest Virginia.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Daugherty's Cave is located and to which the Dust Cave findings are relevant.

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: A cave site along the Clinch River containing stratified deposits including a Middle Archaic occupation floor with rock hearths and Cedar Creek points.

name: Clinch River
description: River in the Ridge and Valley portion of Southwest Virginia where Daugherty's Cave is located.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic physiographic region in Southwest Virginia where Daugherty's Cave is located.

name: Cedar Creek points
description: Projectile points found in the Middle Archaic level at Daugherty's Cave.

name: Cactus Hill Site
description: Archaeological site along the Nottoway River Fall Zone in Sussex County, best known for Paleoindian and pre-Clovis occupations but also extensively settled during the Middle Archaic.

name: Sussex County
description: County in Virginia where the Cactus Hill Site is located.

name: Virginia
description: State where multiple Middle Archaic sites discussed in the text are located.

name: LeCroy level
description: Archaeological level at Cactus Hill that yielded slab mortars, hammerstones, pitted cobble tools, and carbonized hickory shell.

name: slab mortars
description: Stone tools found in the LeCroy level at Cactus Hill used for grinding or processing materials.

name: hammerstones
description: Stone tools found in the LeCroy level at Cactus Hill used for percussion activities.

name: pitted cobble tools
description: Stone implements with pitted surfaces found in the LeCroy level at Cactus Hill.

name: Kirk Serrated points
description: Projectile point type found at Cactus Hill associated with cobble net sinkers and similar assemblages to the LeCroy level.

name: cobble net sinkers
description: Stone weights used with fishing nets, found in association with Kirk Serrated points at Cactus Hill.

name: Morrow Mountain points
description: Projectile point type associated with the greatest Middle Archaic site use at Cactus Hill and found at multiple sites in the Nottoway River area.

name: Guilford points
description: Projectile point type well represented in the Cactus Hill assemblage and associated with chipped stone axes and other tools.

name: chipped stone axes
description: Stone tools found in Morrow Mountain and Guilford contexts at Cactus Hill.

name: core choppers
description: Stone tools found in Morrow Mountain and Guilford contexts at Cactus Hill.

name: abrading stones
description: Stone tools used for grinding or smoothing, found in Morrow Mountain and Guilford contexts at Cactus Hill.

name: Halifax level
description: Archaeological level at Cactus Hill where pit features originated and which yielded radiocarbon dates.

name: Slade Farm Complex
description: Archaeological site complex investigated by the McAvoys and NRS group with intact Middle Archaic components.

name: Fannin Site
description: Archaeological site with intact Middle Archaic components that primarily had a quarry function, investigated by the McAvoys and NRS group.

name: Nay Site
description: Archaeological site where excavations identified four fire-cracked rock features in Morrow Mountain context, with 13 Morrow Mountain I points recovered.

name: fire-cracked rock features
description: Archaeological features found at the Nay Site in Morrow Mountain context, likely representing hearths or cooking areas.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period during which various sites in Virginia were occupied, characterized by specific tool types and subsistence practices.

name: Paleoindian
description: Earlier cultural period represented at Cactus Hill, preceding the Middle Archaic occupation.

name: pre-Clovis
description: Very early cultural period apparently represented at Cactus Hill, preceding both Paleoindian and Middle Archaic occupations.

name: Indian Creek V
description: An archaeological site located in Prince Georges County, Maryland, near Washington D.C., situated along an interior Coastal Plain stream near the Fall Line. The site was interpreted as a gathering camp/processing camp repeatedly visited for short periods to exploit seasonally available plant resources throughout much of the Archaic period.

name: Prince Georges County
description: A county in Maryland where the Indian Creek V site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where the Indian Creek V site is located.

name: Washington, D.C.
description: City near which the Indian Creek V site is located.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where the Indian Creek site is situated, with similar settings found throughout northern and eastern Virginia.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic feature near which the Indian Creek site is situated.

name: Virginia
description: State mentioned as having analogous site settings to Indian Creek V in its northern and eastern Coastal Plain and Fall Line locations.

name: Pleistocene
description: Geological period from which pollen records begin at wetlands adjacent to the Indian Creek V site.

name: Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which the Indian Creek V site was repeatedly visited for short periods to exploit seasonally available plant resources.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period well represented at the Indian Creek V site, with the greatest site use appearing to be related to the late Middle Archaic Halifax occupations.

name: Morrow Mountain II
description: Projectile point type representing Middle Archaic occupation at the Indian Creek V site.

name: Brewerton
description: Projectile point type representing Middle Archaic occupation at the Indian Creek V site.

name: Otter Creek
description: Projectile point type representing Middle Archaic occupation at the Indian Creek V site.

name: Vernon
description: Projectile point type representing Middle Archaic occupation at the Indian Creek V site.

name: Halifax
description: Projectile point type representing Middle Archaic occupation at the Indian Creek V site, with Halifax occupations showing the greatest site use during the late Middle Archaic.

name: Dust Cave
description: A karstic vestibule archaeological site located in the middle Tennessee River Valley of northern Alabama. The limestone cave setting was subject to periodical alluviation, resulting in intact occupation stratigraphy with excellent organic preservation.

name: Alabama
description: State where Dust Cave is located.

name: Tennessee River Valley
description: Geographic region in northern Alabama where Dust Cave is located.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Geographic region mentioned as distinct from where Dust Cave is located.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in western Virginia with limestone areas relevant to findings at Dust Cave.

name: Tennessee Valley drainage
description: Geographic region to which findings at Dust Cave are directly relevant.

name: pit features
description: Archaeological features common in the Middle Archaic levels at Dust Cave.

name: clay floors
description: Archaeological features common in the Middle Archaic levels at Dust Cave.

name: projectile points
description: Stone tools found associated with violent trauma in Middle Archaic burials at Dust Cave.

name: LeCroy
description: A Middle Archaic projectile point type characterized by a small, trianguloid blade with a pronounced, bifurcated base, with blade edges often serrated. First formally described by Broyles in 1971 at the St. Albans site.

name: Broyles 1971
description: Researcher who formally described the LeCroy point type at the St. Albans site in West Virginia in 1971.

name: Early Archaic
description: Archaeological period traditionally associated with bifurcate points, preceding the Middle Archaic period.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period that begins with the appearance of the LeCroy point, characterized by increased frequency of points across various site settings and preference for local material in manufacture.

name: St Albans site
description: Archaeological site in West Virginia where Broyles formally described the LeCroy point type in 1971.

name: Slade Farm Complex
description: Archaeological site where McAvoy and McAvoy obtained dates for LeCroy and Stanly points, including a date of 8300 ± 110 BP for LeCroy and 7420 ± 160 BP for Stanly.

name: McAvoy and McAvoy 1997
description: Researchers who reported dates for LeCroy points and described a Small Stanly subtype along the Nottoway River Fall Line.

name: Chapman 1977
description: Researcher who obtained LeCroy dates along the Little Tennessee River and provided radiocarbon dates from sites referenced by Egloff for Stanly points.

name: Little Tennessee River
description: River location where Chapman obtained LeCroy dates and radiocarbon dates from sites used by Egloff for dating Stanly points.

name: Kirk Serrated
description: An early Middle Archaic projectile point with a relatively straight stem and elongated blade with often shallow serrations, originally described by Coe in 1964.

name: Coe 1964
description: Researcher who originally described Kirk Serrated, Kirk Stemmed, and Stanly point types in 1964.

name: Kirk Stemmed
description: A projectile point type originally described by Coe that appears to have shoulders removed during manufacture creating wide notches and/or a flaring stem.

name: Hardaway Site
description: Archaeological site in North Carolina where Coe noted the stratigraphic occurrence of Kirk Serrated points above Kirk Stemmed points.

name: Nottoway River
description: River location where Egloff studied stratigraphic contexts for dating Kirk Serrated points and where McAvoy described Small Stanly subtype along the Fall Line.

name: Egloff 2016
description: Researcher who provided dating for Kirk Serrated, Stanly, and Morrow Mountain I points based on regional radiocarbon dates and stratigraphic contexts.

name: Stanly Point
description: A Middle Archaic projectile point first described by Coe based on specimens from the Doerschuk Site, representing a continuation of the bifurcate tradition but with less pronounced bifurcation and significantly larger than LeCroy.

name: Doerschuk Site
description: Archaeological site in the North Carolina Piedmont where Coe first described the Stanly Point based on specimens from the deepest levels.

name: North Carolina Piedmont
description: Geographic region where the Doerschuk Site is located, from which Coe first described the Stanly Point.

name: McAvoy and McAvoy 2015
description: Researchers who obtained an uncalibrated radiocarbon date of 7420 ± 160 BP from Stanly contexts at the Slade Farm Complex and reported dates for Morrow Mountain I at Cactus Hill.

name: Cedar Creek
description: A relatively small projectile point with triangular-to-lanceolate blades and variable stem configuration, described by Benthall based on findings at Daugherty's Cave.

name: Benthall 1990
description: Researcher who described the Cedar Creek point based on findings at Daugherty's Cave and reported a date of 5690 ± 260 BP from Cedar Creek contexts.

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Archaeological cave site where Benthall found and described Cedar Creek points, with a reported date of 5690 ± 260 BP from Cedar Creek contexts.

name: McLearen 1991a
description: Researcher who documented similar stemmed and broadly side-notched to corner-removed points occurring across much of Virginia west of the Fall Line.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary referenced in relation to the distribution of certain point types across Virginia and along the Nottoway River.

name: Johnson 1968
description: Researcher who illustrated two specimens from the Jeffrey Harris Rock Shelter site that closely align with Cedar Creek description.

name: Jeffrey Harris Rock Shelter site
description: Archaeological rock shelter site where Johnson found specimens similar to Cedar Creek points in a sealed level with an uncalibrated radiocarbon date of 5120 ± 160 BP.

name: Morrow Mountain I
description: A projectile point with a distinctive triangular blade and vestigial contracting stem, varying in size but typically ranging from 30-70 mm in length with a mean of 45 mm.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period associated with the Cattle Run Savannah River variant points, which are sometimes misidentified as Morrow Mountain I.

name: Cattle Run Savannah River variant
description: A Late Archaic point that is much larger than Morrow Mountain I (50-175 mm in length), with typically lanceolate rather than triangular blade and a preference for quartzite in manufacture.

name: Savannah River
description: A projectile point type that shares flaking technique and quartzite preference with Cattle Run points.

name: Cactus Hill
description: Archaeological site where McAvoy and McAvoy reported a date of 6700 ± 130 BP for Morrow Mountain I points.

name: Morrow Mountain II
description: A contracting stemmed projectile point type characterized by triangular blade, pronounced shoulders, and distinctive pointed stem, considered slightly younger than Morrow Mountain I.

name: Morrow Mountain I
aliases: short stemmed Morrow Mountain I form
description: A projectile point type similar to Morrow Mountain II but with less pronounced contracting stem characteristics.

name: Coe
description: A researcher who defined various projectile point types including Morrow Mountain II, Guilford, and Halifax in 1964.

name: Egloff
description: A researcher who has studied and written about Virginia projectile points, placing Morrow Mountain II slightly younger than Morrow Mountain I in 2016.

name: Slade Farm Complex
description: An archaeological site where an uncalibrated radiocarbon date of 6470 ± 90 BP was obtained for Morrow Mountain II.

name: McAvoy and McAvoy
description: Researchers who obtained radiocarbon dates from the Slade Farm Complex and Cactus Hill in 2015.

name: Guilford
description: A projectile point type with long, spike-like form and characteristically thick cross section, with four distinct varieties based on stem configurations.

name: Virginia
description: A state where Guilford points are relatively common but seem most abundant in the southern half.

name: Cactus Hill
description: An archaeological site where uncalibrated radiocarbon dates were obtained for Guilford and Halifax points.

name: Halifax
description: A projectile point type with slender blade, thick cross section, and wide shallow side notching, one of the youngest Middle Archaic point types.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region where Halifax points are typically manufactured on milky quartz.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where quartzite Halifax specimens are not uncommon and tend to be larger than quartz versions.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A chronological period during which Halifax points were made, with Halifax being one of the youngest point types of this era.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period that Halifax points may extend into, following the Middle Archaic period.

name: Gaston Site
description: An archaeological site where a circa 3450 BC date was originally reported by Coe in 1964.

name: Middle Archaic Side-Notched Cluster
description: A collective term for several side-notched point types from the Middle Atlantic and greater Northeast region.

name: Halifax
description: A formally described Middle Archaic side-notched projectile point type, part of the Middle Archaic Side-Notched Cluster.

name: Clagett
description: A side-notched point type originally described by Stevenson in 1963, very similar to Halifax but with a longer, slenderer blade and distinctive flare.

name: Rowan
description: A point type adapted from collector terminology, similar to Halifax but with deep notches and a squared base, found at Cactus Hill.

name: Otter Creek
description: A point type described by Ritchie in 1971, showing strong similarity to Rowan points.

name: Vernon
description: A point type first described by Stevenson and Ferguson in 1963, similar to Halifax but smaller, with wide side notches, thick cross section, and preference for quartz.

name: Piscataway
description: A point type whose frequency distribution across the Ogle sites closely follows that of Vernon points, possibly indicating Early Woodland association.

name: Accokeek Creek site
description: An archaeological site where Clagett and Vernon point types were originally described based on findings.

name: Cactus Hill
description: An archaeological site where McAvoy and McAvoy applied the name Rowan to Halifax-like points with deep notches and squared bases.

name: Ogle sites
description: Archaeological sites where Vernon and Piscataway point frequency distributions were compared.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A geographical region where Middle Archaic Side-Notched Cluster point types are found.

name: Northeast region
description: A geographical region where Middle Archaic Side-Notched Cluster point types are found.

name: North Carolina
description: A state where the Rowan term was commonly used by collectors and avocational archaeologists.

name: Virginia
description: A state where researchers like Egloff and Nash assign Early Woodland dates to Vernon points.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A cultural period associated with side-notched point types like Halifax and potentially Vernon.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period originally proposed for Vernon points and supported by some contemporary researchers.

name: Stevenson
description: Researcher who originally described Clagett points in 1963 based on findings at Accokeek Creek site.

name: Egloff
description: Researcher who noted similarities between Clagett and Halifax points and ascribes Early Woodland date to Vernon type.

name: McAvoy
description: Researcher who, along with another McAvoy, applied the name Rowan to Halifax-like points at Cactus Hill in 1997.

name: Coe
description: Researcher who originally described the Halifax point type.

name: Ritchie
description: Researcher who described the Otter Creek type in 1971 and the Brewerton Cluster.

name: Ferguson
description: Researcher who, along with Stevenson, first described Vernon points in 1963.

name: Nash
description: Researcher who ascribes an Early Woodland date to the Vernon point type.

name: Egghart
description: Researcher who studied frequency distribution of Vernon points across the Ogle sites in 2016.

name: Brewerton Cluster
description: A cluster of artifact types described by Ritchie in 1971.

name: Cactus Hill
description: Archaeological site where Middle Archaic occupations and tool assemblages have been documented, including LeCroy occupations and ground stone tools.

name: Slade Farm Complex
description: Archaeological site complex where Middle Archaic levels and tool assemblages have been documented, including LeCroy deposits and fire-cracked rock clusters.

name: Nottoway River
description: River in Virginia where multiple Middle Archaic sites have been documented, including sites with ground stone implements and fire-cracked rock clusters.

name: Edd's Mill Site
description: Archaeological site in southwest Virginia where two fire-cracked rock features were reported in Middle Archaic levels.

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Archaeological site where three fire-cracked rock clusters were identified in association with Cedar Creek points.

name: Deep Bottom Site Complex
description: Archaeological site complex along the Lower James River where substantial fire-cracked rock clusters were encountered in Morrow Mountain context.

name: Lower James River
description: River region in Virginia where the Deep Bottom Site Complex is located.

name: Nay Site
description: Archaeological site along the Nottoway Fall Zone where a Halifax artifact cache was reported.

name: Nottoway Fall Zone
description: Geographic region along the Nottoway River where the Nay Site is located.

name: Hardaway Site
description: Seminal archaeological site on the Roanoke River used for defining regional cultural sequences.

name: Roanoke River
description: River where the Hardaway Site is located.

name: St Albans Site
description: Seminal archaeological site on the Kanawha River used for defining regional cultural sequences.

name: Kanawha River
description: River where the St Albans Site is located.

name: Ice House Bottom
description: Archaeological site used for defining regional cultural sequences relevant to the Middle Archaic.

name: Middle Archaic period
description: Cultural period characterized by increased utilization of local lithics and greater use of expedient cutting tools.

name: LeCroy occupations
description: Archaeological levels/occupations within the Middle Archaic period documented at Cactus Hill and Slade Farm Complex.

name: Morrow Mountain
description: Archaeological period/context within the Middle Archaic associated with substantial fire-cracked rock clusters.

name: Halifax period
description: Archaeological period within the Middle Archaic associated with specific point types and artifact caches.

name: Cedar Creek points
description: Projectile point type found in association with fire-cracked rock clusters at Daugherty's Cave.

name: Halifax points
description: Finished projectile points found in an artifact cache at the Nay Site, part of the Halifax period assemblage.

name: core choppers

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period characterized by shifts toward locally available lithic materials and changes in subsistence patterns.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where projectile points are typically made from quartz and quartzite materials.

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region where projectile points are typically made from quartz and metavolcanic stone, and source of some lithic materials found at other sites.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region where projectile points are typically made from mid-grade chert materials.

name: Morrow Mountain I
description: Type of projectile points often made from various lithic materials including those from the Piedmont region, found in Coastal Plain and Fall Line areas.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic area where Morrow Mountain I points made from various lithic materials are found.

name: Cactus Hill
description: Archaeological site with Morrow Mountain I assemblages made from extra-local materials and where notched cobble net-sinkers were found with Kirk Serrated points.

name: Nay sites
description: Archaeological sites with Morrow Mountain I assemblages made from extra-local materials.

name: Ogle Collection sites
description: Archaeological sites with Morrow Mountain I assemblages and a peak occurrence of Kirk Serrated points in tributary settings suggesting anadromous fish exploitation.

name: Deep Bottom Site Complex
description: Archaeological site complex with Morrow Mountain I assemblages made from extra-local materials.

name: Commonwealth
description: Geographic region where Middle Archaic sites lack subsistence remains and have poor organic preservation.

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Archaeological site where various small mammal, deer, and avian faunal remains were recovered from Middle Archaic levels.

name: Slade Farm Complex
description: Archaeological site with limited Middle Archaic faunal remains and carbonized hickory shell recovered in LeCroy levels.

name: Kirk Serrated
description: Type of projectile points found in association with notched cobble net-sinkers, representing early evidence for fishing in Archaic period sites.

name: notched cobble net-sinkers
description: Fishing implements found at Cactus Hill in association with Kirk Serrated points, representing early evidence for fishing.

description: Archaeological site along the Little Tennessee River where similar notched cobble net-sinkers were observed.

name: Little Tennessee River
description: River where the Howard Site is located and similar fishing implements were found.

name: Archaic period
description: Archaeological period during which early evidence for fishing and plant food utilization is found in the region.

name: LeCroy
description: Archaeological time period or level within the Middle Archaic when carbonized hickory shell and small slab mortars were found.

name: small slab mortars
description: Stone tools found in LeCroy contexts at Cactus Hill, possibly indicating increasing use of hickory as food source.

name: pitted cobble tools
description: Stone tools found in LeCroy contexts at Cactus Hill, possibly indicating increasing use of hickory as food source.

name: Indian Creek V site
description: Archaeological site suggesting robust Archaic utilization of wild plant foods and possible active environmental manipulation through fire.

name: Virginia
description: State where Middle Archaic subsistence practices evidence remains elusive, leading researchers to look outside the region for analogs.

name: Dust Cave
description: Limestone cave site in Alabama with exceptional organic preservation showing extensive use of nut, seed, and fruit resources beginning in late Paleoindian levels.

name: Alabama
description: State where Dust Cave is located, providing analog data for Middle Archaic subsistence practices.

name: late Paleoindian
description: Archaeological period at Dust Cave showing extensive use of nut, seed, and fruit resources and exploitation of aquatic resources including fish and waterfowl.

name: Paleoindian
description: Archaeological period preceding the Middle Archaic, mentioned in context of subsistence patterns at Dust Cave.

name: Nottoway River
description: River in southeastern Virginia where findings mirror the subsistence trends observed at Dust Cave.

name: Middle Archaic Period
description: A prehistoric cultural period in Virginia characterized by mobile hunter-gatherers with egalitarian social structure and seasonal settlement patterns.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region discussed as having a resource-rich environment that supported Middle Archaic hunter-gatherer populations.

name: Early Archaic Period
description: An earlier prehistoric cultural period referenced in Parker's discussion of settlement and demography.

name: !Kung San Bushmen

name: Kalahari Desert
description: An extremely challenging and unpredictable desert environment where the !Kung San Bushmen live.

name: COVA synthesis
description: A publication that contains Parker's discussion of Early Archaic and Middle Archaic settlement and demography.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A cultural period in North American prehistory characterized by semi-transient hunter-gatherer societies with complex social networks and distinctive projectile point technologies.

name: LeCroy point
description: A type of Middle Archaic projectile point found across wide geographic areas from the southeastern coastal plain through the Appalachians to the middle Ohio Valley and into southern New England.

name: Appalachians
description: Mountain region where LeCroy and similar bifurcate stemmed projectile points are found during the Middle Archaic period.

description: Geographic region where Middle Archaic projectile point types like LeCroy points are found.

name: Late Woodland
description: A later cultural period characterized by raiding and warfare among societies, contrasted with the Middle Archaic period.

name: Dust Cave
description: Archaeological site where Middle Archaic burials show evidence of skeletal injuries and demographic distortions pointing to endemic conflict.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A cultural period characterized by population growth and dynamic changes in settlement patterns, marked by significant increases in projectile point frequencies during its second half.

description: A collection of archaeological sites and artifacts used to calculate time-weighted point counts for population estimates during the Archaic Period.

description: State where Middle Archaic archaeological research is being conducted, with both strengths and weaknesses in existing data.

name: Middle Archaic

name: Early Archaic

name: Virginia
description: The state where Middle Archaic archaeological contexts and sites are being investigated, particularly in the southeastern part.

description: A major river in Virginia where intact floodplain sites containing Middle Archaic materials are likely to be present.

description: A river in Virginia where intact floodplain sites containing Middle Archaic materials are likely to be present.

name: Ridge and Valley

name: Commonwealth of Virginia

name: Virginia Department of Environmental Quality
description: The institutional affiliation of author Christopher Egghart.

name: Christopher Egghart

name: Section 106

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period characterized by pronounced cultural developments including novel technologies, shifts in subsistence and settlement, long distance trade networks, and nascent socio-political complexity.

name: Middle Archaic
description: An earlier cultural period characterized by apparently relatively stable lifeways in contrast to the Late Archaic.

name: Western Hemisphere
description: The geographic region where humans first arrived and where egalitarian order is presumed to have been the norm since first arrival.

name: Chesapeake Bay
