name: Virginia
description: A region with diverse physiographic settings and environmental zones where Late Archaic societies lived and participated in cultural trends to varying degrees.

name: Big Man model
description: A model of social organization postulated for the Late Archaic where certain individuals rose to prominence through force of personality, achievement, and largesse, allowing them to coordinate communal activities.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural and chronological period characterized by profound environmental change and human adaptations, particularly east of the Fall Line.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary in Virginia east of which particularly profound environmental change occurred during the Late Archaic period.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region where mid-Holocene environmental changes shaped the landscape and influenced Late Archaic cultural developments.

name: mid-Holocene
description: A geological time period characterized by warming and drying climate and ongoing sea level rise in what is now Virginia.

name: Chesapeake estuary system
description: A highly productive estuary system whose full development had direct influence on Late Archaic lifeways.

name: Holocene
description: Geological epoch characterized by warming and drying climate conditions in North America.

name: North America
description: Continental region experiencing climate changes that affected forest composition during the Holocene.

name: Archaic period
description: Cultural period during which essentially modern forest conditions were established due to climate changes.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which warm/dry climate conditions may have influenced human adaptations and cultural changes.

name: Custer
description: Researcher who argued that a peak in warm/dry conditions was coeval with the beginning of the Late Archaic period and coined the term xerothermic.

name: xerothermic
description: Term used by Custer to refer to a warm/dry climatic occurrence that played a significant role in cultural changes during the Late Archaic period.

name: Broadspear tradition
description: Cultural tradition that developed during the Late Archaic period, potentially influenced by xerothermic conditions.

name: Curry
description: Researcher who documented aeolian and alluvial soil discontinuities during archaeological investigations.

name: Ebright
description: Researcher who collaborated with Curry on studies of aeolian soils and archaeological contexts.

name: Heite
description: Researcher who studied the occurrence of aeolian soils capping period occupation floors.

name: Blume
description: Researcher who collaborated with Heite on studies of aeolian soils and archaeological contexts.

name: Brooks
description: Researcher who studied aeolian deposition patterns and their relationship to changing hydrological regimes rather than broad climatic conditions.

name: Mouer
description: Researcher who presented aeolian deposition over archaeological surfaces on Coastal Plain James River terraces as a highly localized phenomenon.

name: Stevens
description: Researcher who studied aeolian phenomena in relation to changing hydrological regimes and anthropogenic factors.

name: Blanton
description: Researcher who summarized the debate on the mid-Holocene warm/dry peak or Hypsithermal and endorsed local-scale analysis.

name: Hypsithermal
description: Mid-Holocene climatic period characterized by warm and dry conditions, subject of debate regarding its influence on archaeological patterns.

name: James River
description: Coastal Plain river where aeolian deposition over archaeological surfaces has been documented on terraces.

name: Atlantic Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where rivers responded to mid-Holocene sea level rise by meandering and cutting multiple braided channels.

name: Schuldenrein
description: Researcher who studied how Atlantic Coastal Plain rivers responded to mid-Holocene sea level rise.

name: South Carolina
description: State where Brooks et al. studied aeolian activity in the Wateree River system.

name: Wateree River
description: River in South Carolina where aeolian activity onset and cessation was studied as independent of climate factors.

name: Big Bay
description: Location associated with aeolian activity in the Wateree River system.

name: Savannah River
description: Coastal Plain river where similar environmental transformations occurred around 4000-3000 BP, closing off sediment sources for aeolian transport.

name: Hypsithermal
description: A period of maximum post-glacial warmth and aridity, dated by various studies from approximately 8500-4500 BP or 7500-5000 BP, essentially coeval with the Middle Archaic cultural period.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period that followed the Middle Archaic, characterized by unstable floodplain surfaces and significant climate oscillations, ending around 1200 BC.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period that was essentially coeval with the Hypsithermal period of maximum post-glacial warmth.

name: Early Holocene
description: Early part of the Holocene epoch when climate was strongly influenced by factors like lower sea levels and remnant ice sheets.

name: Mid-Holocene
description: Middle part of the Holocene epoch characterized by general climatic shift to warm/dry conditions.

name: Great Plains
description: Geographic region where climate reconstructions align with the timing of maximum post-glacial warmth and aridity from 7500-5000 BP.

name: Midwest
description: Geographic region where climate reconstructions align with the timing of maximum post-glacial warmth and aridity from 7500-5000 BP.

name: Eastern North America
description: Geographic region for which paleoclimate data and studies have been reviewed to characterize the Hypsithermal period.

name: North America
description: Continental region whose Early to Mid-Holocene climate was influenced by factors like lower sea levels and remnant ice sheets.

name: Laurentide Ice Sheet
description: Remnant ice sheet that was one of the factors strongly influencing Early Holocene to Mid-Holocene climate of North America.

name: North Atlantic
description: Ocean region that received episodic releases of continental glacial melt waters and where climate data from ice cores shows warming at the end of the Late Archaic.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region with floodplain surfaces that showed apparent unstable nature during the Late Archaic period.

name: Greenland
description: Location of ice cores that provide climate data indicating North Atlantic warming at the end of the Late Archaic around 1200 BC.

name: Joyce
description: Researcher who in 1988 presented convincing arguments for the earlier timing of the Hypsithermal (8500 BP to 4500 BP) based on palynological studies.

name: Delcourt
description: Researcher who with Delcourt (1985) dated the period of maximum post-glacial warmth and aridity from 7500 BP-5000 BP.

name: Anderson
description: Researcher who in 2001 characterized the Hypsithermal as essentially coeval with the Middle Archaic cultural period based on paleoclimate data review.

name: Carbone
description: Researcher whose 1976 groundbreaking work defined a general mid-Holocene climatic shift to warm/dry conditions.

name: Klein
description: Researcher who documented the unstable nature of Middle Atlantic floodplain surfaces during the Late Archaic period.

name: Klatka
description: Researcher who with Klein (1991) documented the unstable nature of Middle Atlantic floodplain surfaces during the Late Archaic period.

name: Vento
description: Researcher who in 2008 contributed to documentation of unstable Middle Atlantic floodplain surfaces during the Late Archaic period.

name: Custer
description: Researcher who in 1989 discussed aeolian soil deposition on regional Late Archaic occupation surfaces.

name: Gunn
description: Researcher who in 1997 characterized the climate for the period 4400 to 2600 BP as subject to significant fluctuations on a century scale.

name: Yu
description: Researcher who with Eicher (1998) studied episodic releases of continental glacial melt waters into the North Atlantic.

name: Eicher
description: Researcher who with Yu (1998) studied episodic releases of continental glacial melt waters into the North Atlantic.

name: Hu
description: Researcher who in 1999 studied factors influencing Early Holocene to Mid-Holocene climate of North America.

name: Willemse
description: Researcher who with Tornqvist (1999) analyzed climate data showing dramatic temperature changes at the end of the Late Archaic.

name: Tornqvist
description: Researcher who with Willemse (1999) analyzed climate data showing dramatic temperature changes at the end of the Late Archaic.

name: Blanton
description: A researcher who argues that climate factors alone had relatively little effect on hunter-gatherer adaptations of the mid-Holocene at pan-regional scale.

name: mid-Holocene
description: A chronological period during which hunter-gatherer adaptations were studied in relation to climate factors.

name: Delmarva
description: A region where most archaeological sites with aeolian discontinuities have been noted, with surface soils underlain by fluvial deposited sands and gravels of the Columbia Formation.

name: Columbia Formation
description: Fluvial deposited and reworked sands and gravels that underlie surface soils in the Delmarva region, creating well-drained upland terraces.

name: Groot and Jordan
description: Researchers who documented the Columbia Formation consisting of fluvial deposited and reworked sands and gravels.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: A region characterized by well-developed B horizon soils often underlain by marine clays, with greater moisture retaining characteristics than the Columbia Formation sediments.

name: Chesapeake
description: A geographic reference point, with the Virginia Coastal Plain lying west of it.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period during which aeolian activity occurred in Delaware and forest cover variations happened in Virginia.

name: Delaware
description: A state where Late Archaic aeolian activity has been observed, with associated inferences on local climate.

name: Virginia
description: A state with varied precipitation patterns, particularly in western regions where mountain ridges influence meteorological conditions.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range that significantly influences precipitation patterns, with west-facing slopes averaging 46-58 inches of precipitation annually.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region east of the Blue Ridge that averages between 40 and 45 inches of rain per year.

name: Allegheny highlands
description: A mountainous region that averages between 46 and 58 inches of precipitation annually.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: A mountainous region that averages around 60 inches of precipitation annually, with higher elevation and narrower valley floors.

name: United States Department of Agriculture
description: A government agency that has documented precipitation data for various regions of Virginia.

name: Hayden and Michaels
description: Researchers who documented that mountains of far Southwest Virginia average around 60 inches of precipitation.

name: Abrams
description: A researcher who explained orographic lift as the phenomenon causing increased precipitation over high terrain.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A valley region that experiences rain shadow effect, averaging only around 36 inches of precipitation per year.

name: Rockingham County
description: A county with isolated areas receiving 33 inches or less precipitation annually, near the threshold where forest cover thins and grasslands begin.

name: Southeast Regional Climate Center
description: An organization that documented precipitation data showing some areas in Rockingham County receive 33 inches or less annually.

name: Knox
description: A researcher who identified the precipitation threshold at which forest cover thins and grasslands begin to occur.

name: New River Valley
description: A valley region experiencing rain shadow effect along its middle reaches, though less pronounced than in the Shenandoah Valley.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to Virginia as a political entity, specifically mentioned in the context of its mountainous western regions.

name: mid-Holocene
description: A chronological period characterized by warmth and dryness that influenced human adaptations in Virginia's regions.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period that roughly corresponds with the final development of the greater Chesapeake estuary system and cultural changes associated with new estuarine environments.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major estuary system formed by rising ocean waters backing into the massive ancestral Susquehanna River, reaching its modern configuration by 3000 BP.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region whose inland river systems were stabilized by sea level rise, resulting in highly productive environmental settings.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary west of which areas remained largely unaffected by sea level rise changes.

name: Holocene period
description: A geological time period for which relative sea level curves have been generated for the Delaware Bay region.

name: Delaware Bay
description: A bay for which Holocene period relative sea level curves were generated by Kraft in 1977.

name: Susquehanna River
description: A major river into which rising ocean waters backed to eventually form the Chesapeake Bay, with rising waters pushing well up the river by 4500 years ago.

name: Potomac
description: A major tributary of the Susquehanna River that was affected by rising waters by 4500 years ago.

name: Rappahannock
description: A major tributary of the Susquehanna River that was affected by rising waters by 4500 years ago.

name: James River
description: A major tributary of the Susquehanna River that was embayed as far upstream as the mouth of Baileys Creek by around 5000 BP, with full tidal influence extending to the base of the Falls by the following millennium.

name: Baileys Creek
description: A creek whose mouth in eastern Henrico County marks how far upstream the Lower James River was embayed by around 5000 BP.

name: Henrico County
description: A county in eastern Virginia where the mouth of Baileys Creek is located.

name: Falls
description: A location to which full tidal influence likely extended during the millennium following 5000 BP.

name: Late Archaic
description: A prehistoric cultural period characterized by intensification of resource exploitation and focused orientation to estuarine and freshwater environments.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where intensive food resource exploitation occurred during the Late Archaic period.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region with river floodplains where intensive foraging and wild food harvesting likely occurred during the Late Archaic period.

name: Appalachian highlands
description: A mountainous region where intensive foraging and wild food harvesting likely occurred during the Late Archaic period, possibly with localized forest modification.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A large bay where significant Late Archaic occupation sites are believed to lie on the floor due to rising sea levels.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where rivers were affected by sea level rise and where major drainages east of the Fall Line are located.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period characterized by specific life ways and associated with floodplain occupations and the transformation of wetland environments.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major body of water created as the ultimate result of sea level rise affecting Coastal Plain rivers.

name: Chickahominy River
description: A strongly meandering tributary river channel with an associated swampy floodplain that vividly expresses physical transformation from sea level rise.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary east of which major drainages were affected by sea level rise and meandering river channels developed.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: An area that was transformed from low scarps and stream valleys into a single expansive wetland, experiencing its greatest prehistoric use during an intermediate stage of this transformation in the Late Archaic period.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A region where researchers have studied Holocene floodplain depositional patterns and Late Archaic floodplain surfaces.

name: Holocene
description: A geological time period during which floodplain depositional patterns were dynamic with periods of stability punctuated by active deposition and surface erosion.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period that, along with the Late Archaic, marks a transition time that appears to have been characterized by floodplain stability.

name: Virginia
description: A state where archaeological observations about Late Archaic floodplain surfaces mirror patterns seen across northern Middle Atlantic watersheds.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period in Virginia defined chronologically from 2500 BC to 1000 BC, characterized by increasing cultural diversity and regionalization compared to preceding times.

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region where the Late Archaic period is being studied, referenced as the Commonwealth in the text.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region whose archaeologists use specific prehistoric subdivisions for research framework.

name: Savannah River point tradition
description: Point tradition that represents a logical demarcation for the beginning of the Late Archaic in Virginia.

name: Lamoka
description: Narrow-bladed point type included within the Late Archaic period by archaeologists working in states north of Virginia.

name: Bare Island
description: Narrow-bladed point type with uncertain temporal placement, though most researchers ascribe a Late Archaic association.

name: Poplar Island
description: Narrow-bladed point type that has association with stone vessels in the Northeast.

name: Halifax tradition
description: Point tradition that Dent sees as analogous to forms like Lamoka, Bare Island, and Poplar Island; usually included in the latter portion of Middle Archaic in Virginia.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region where Poplar Island points have association with stone vessels.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period that precedes the Late Archaic, during which the Halifax tradition is usually included in the latter portion.

name: Woodland Period
description: Cultural period that follows the Late Archaic, traditionally defined by the adoption of fired clay vessel manufacture.

name: Orient Fishtail
description: Late period point type that co-occurs with early ceramics, complicating the distinction between Late Archaic and Woodland periods.

name: Small Savannah River
description: Late period point type that co-occurs with early ceramics, clouding the distinction between Late Archaic and Woodland periods.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period that follows the Late Archaic, with cultural traits that appear to span the traditional demarcation between the two periods.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period in Virginia whose onset is defined by the Savannah River tradition.

name: Virginia
description: The state/region where the Savannah River tradition is discussed as a dominant material culture trait.

name: Savannah River tradition
description: A dominant material culture tradition that defines the onset of the Late Archaic in Virginia, characterized by distinctive hafted tools.

name: Savannah River point
description: A distinctive hafted tool featuring a lanceolate blade, well-pronounced shoulders, and a very slightly tapered stem with a concave base.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: A geographic region where the Savannah River point is noted as being unique in morphology, size, and manufacturing technique.

name: Holmes point
description: A narrow blade version of the classic Savannah River point form, found across eastern and northern Virginia.

name: Potomac drainage
description: A drainage area where the narrow blade variant of Savannah River points most commonly occurs.

name: Coastal Plain James River
description: A geographic area where narrow blade Savannah River variants occur in numbers.

name: Carolina Sounds drainages
description: A drainage area where narrow blade Savannah River variants are found.

name: Nottoway River
description: A river where narrow blade and wide blade varieties of Savannah River points are generally not found together in fire-cracked rock features.

name: Inner Piedmont
description: A geographic region in Northern Virginia where the Holmes point is viewed as a late Savannah River manifestation.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A geographic region in Northern Virginia where the Holmes point is viewed as a late Savannah River manifestation.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A region containing Inner Piedmont and Blue Ridge sections where narrow blade points occur.

name: Slade Site
description: An archaeological site where a radiocarbon date of 4070 +/- 80 BP was obtained from carbonized hickory nut associated with narrow blade Savannah River variants.

name: southeastern Virginia
description: A region where stratigraphic evidence suggests narrow-blade Savannah River variants may date slightly earlier than the classic form.

name: Stallings Island
description: An archaeological site in Georgia where a Savannah River variant with wide blade and strongly contracting stem was first described by Claflin in 1931.

name: Georgia
description: The state where Stallings Island is located.

name: Cattle Run
description: A name applied by Geier to better define the strongly contracting stem Savannah River variant.

name: Morrow Mountain tradition
description: A tradition to which the strongly contracting stem Savannah River variant is often erroneously attributed.

name: James River Coastal Plain
description: A geographic area containing single component Late Archaic encampment sites studied to understand Savannah River variants.

name: northern Piedmont
description: A geographic region with single component Late Archaic encampment sites studied to understand Savannah River variants.

name: Nottoway River Fall Line
description: A geographic area where site assemblages were analyzed to understand Savannah River variants as distinct elements.

name: Small Savannah River
description: A projectile point type that is a variant of Savannah River points, characterized by reduced size and a squatter, more squared stem, often made from non-quartzite materials like quartz.

name: Oliver
description: Archaeologist who formally described the Small Savannah River point type in 1981.

name: Gleach
description: Archaeologist who elaborated on the Small Savannah River point type description in 1987.

name: McAvoy and McAvoy
description: Archaeological researchers who placed Small Savannah River points towards the end of the Late Archaic based on stratigraphic positions along the Nottoway drainage.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period in Virginia prehistory characterized by specific projectile point types and cultural developments.

name: Nottoway drainage
description: A river drainage system in Virginia where stratigraphic evidence for Small Savannah River points was recorded.

name: Mouer
description: Archaeologist who reported associations between Small Savannah River points and early ceramic wares in 1991, and studied point types along the Coastal Plain James River.

name: Savannah River
description: A classic Late Archaic projectile point type from which Small Savannah River is considered a variant.

name: Lamoka
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type associated with the northern Middle Atlantic and Northeast material culture sequences.

name: Bare Island
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type associated with the northern Middle Atlantic and Northeast material culture sequences.

name: Poplar Island
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type associated with the northern Middle Atlantic and Northeast material culture sequences.

name: Brewerton
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type associated with the northern Middle Atlantic and Northeast material culture sequences.

name: Susquehanna
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type found mostly within the Potomac drainage and occasionally along the Coastal Plain James River.

name: Perkiomen
description: A Late Archaic projectile point type found mostly within the Potomac drainage, with notable concentrations along western Dismal Swamp margins.

name: Fishtail point
description: A distinctive Late Archaic projectile point type with relatively even distribution across Virginia that may span the traditional Archaic-Woodland break.

name: Dry Brook
description: A projectile point type that appears to be a derivation of the Fishtail point.

name: Potomac drainage
description: A river drainage system where Perkiomen and Susquehanna points are commonly found.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: A geographic region in Virginia where a notable concentration of Perkiomen points has been found along its western margins.

name: Blanton
description: Archaeologist who documented the concentration of Perkiomen points along western Dismal Swamp margins in 2002.

name: Coastal Plain James River
description: A geographic region in Virginia where Susquehanna and Perkiomen point types are occasionally found.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: A region of Virginia where various Late Archaic point types including Susquehanna, Perkiomen, and crude stemmed varieties have been found.

name: Egghart
description: Archaeologist who documented point types in extreme Southwest Virginia in 1991.

name: Jones
description: Archaeologist who documented point types in extreme Southwest Virginia in 2001.

name: McLearen
description: Archaeologist who detailed the occurrence of small, crudely flaked stemmed and side notched points in Southwest Virginia and the Shenandoah Valley.

name: Chapman
description: Archaeologist who defined the Iddins type for the lower Tennessee Valley in 1981.

name: Iddins type
description: A projectile point type defined by Chapman for the lower Tennessee Valley, similar to stemmed varieties found in Southwest Virginia.

name: Tennessee Valley
description: A geographic region where the Iddins projectile point type was defined.

name: Voigt
description: Archaeologist who placed side notched point varieties within the greater Lamoka tradition in 2000.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A geographic region in Virginia where crudely stemmed and side notched points occur in possible Late Archaic contexts.

name: Boyer
description: Archaeologist who documented point types in the Shenandoah Valley in 1982.

name: Piedmont Potomac River
description: A geographic region where crudely stemmed and side notched points occur in possible Late Archaic contexts.

name: Gardner
description: Archaeologist who documented point types along the Piedmont Potomac River in 2000.

name: Slade point
description: A projectile point type formally described by McAvoy and McAvoy in the Carolina Sounds drainage, similar to several other regional types.

name: Carolina Sounds drainage
description: A drainage system where the Slade point type was formally described.

name: Broyle
description: Archaeologist who described the Buffalo Expanding Stem point type for the Appalachian region in 1976.

name: Buffalo Expanding Stem
description: A projectile point type described by Broyle for the Appalachian region, very similar to the Slade point.

name: Appalachian region
description: A geographic region where the Buffalo Expanding Stem point type was described.

name: State Farm type
description: A projectile point type described by Mouer, similar to the Slade point.

name: Lobate Point
description: A projectile point type described by Knepper for Northern Virginia, similar to the Slade point with uncertain temporal placement.

name: Knepper
description: Archaeologist who described the Lobate Point type for Northern Virginia in 1995.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A geographic region where the Lobate Point type was described.

name: Halifax
description: A projectile point type found in stratigraphic contexts below Slade points along the Nottoway River.

name: Egloff
description: Archaeologist who worked with McAvoy to document stratigraphic contexts of Slade points along the Nottoway River in 1990.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A chronological period that may include the temporal placement of the Slade/Lobate Point type.

name: Archaic-Woodland break
description: A traditional chronological boundary that Fishtails and possibly Small Savannah River points may span.

name: Potomac River
description: A major river that serves as a geographic boundary for different archaeological classification schemes.

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region where steatite vessels are associated with the Savannah River tradition as a late manifestation.

name: Savannah River tradition
description: Archaeological tradition most closely associated with steatite vessels in Virginia as a late manifestation.

name: Potomac
description: River that serves as a geographic boundary, north of which steatite is associated with different point types than in Virginia.

name: Perkiomen points
description: Type of projectile points largely associated with steatite in areas north of the Potomac River.

name: Susquehanna
description: Variety of points associated with steatite in areas north of the Potomac River.

name: Fishtail varieties
description: Variety of points associated with steatite in areas north of the Potomac River.

name: steatite vessels
description: Stone vessels most closely associated with the Savannah River tradition in Virginia, obtained and used outside of the Savannah River realm to some degree.

name: Savannah River realm
description: Geographic region associated with the Savannah River tradition, outside of which steatite vessels were also obtained and used in Virginia.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period in Virginia's prehistory during which specific projectile point types were manufactured and used.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 6.1: Point Types Relevant to the Late Archaic

name: Calvert
description: A projectile point type found commonly only in the Potomac drainage during the Early Woodland period.

name: Potomac drainage
description: A geographic region where certain projectile point types like Calvert are commonly found.

name: Early Woodland
description: A chronological period during which various projectile point types were in use.

name: Fishtail
description: A statewide Early Woodland projectile point type found throughout Virginia.

name: Early Woodland Transition
description: A transitional period associated with Piscataway projectile points, found primarily in northern and eastern Virginia.

name: Piscataway
description: A projectile point type found primarily in northern and eastern Virginia during the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period associated with various projectile point types and the introduction of steatite vessels.

name: Perkiomen
description: A statewide but rare projectile point type with clusters in southeastern and northeastern Virginia.

name: Susquehanna
description: A projectile point type found commonly only in the Potomac drainage with isolated occurrences elsewhere.

name: Small Savannah River
description: A statewide projectile point type associated with the Archaic to Woodland transition period.

name: Archaic to Woodland transition
description: A transitional chronological period between the Archaic and Woodland periods.

name: Cattle Run
description: A projectile point type found mostly in southern Virginia, possibly a later variant of Savannah River.

name: Savannah River
description: A statewide projectile point type dominant in all areas except the southwest.

name: Savannah River Narrow
description: A variant of Savannah River points found east of the Blue Ridge, most common in the north.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A geographic feature used as a boundary marker for the distribution of Savannah River Narrow points.

name: Poplar Island
description: A projectile point type found in southeastern Virginia with intermittent occurrence north of the James River.

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia used as a geographic reference for projectile point distributions.

name: Bare Island
description: A projectile point type found in southeastern Virginia with intermittent occurrence north of the James River.

name: Iddins-like
description: A projectile point type found in southwestern Virginia and the Great Valley region.

name: Great Valley
description: A geographic region in Virginia where Iddins-like projectile points are found.

name: Lamoka
description: A statewide projectile point type, with 'Lamoka-like' terminology often applied to similar forms.

name: Slade
description: A projectile point type that clusters in southeastern and northern Piedmont Virginia, associated with Middle Archaic Transition.

name: Lobate
description: A projectile point type that clusters in southeastern and northern Piedmont Virginia, associated with Middle Archaic Transition.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region in Virginia where Slade/Lobate projectile points cluster.

name: Middle Archaic Transition
description: A transitional chronological period associated with Slade/Lobate projectile points.

name: Halifax
description: A statewide projectile point type associated with the late Middle Archaic period.

name: late Middle Archaic
description: A chronological period associated with Halifax and Brewerton Cluster projectile points.

name: Brewerton Cluster
description: A projectile point type found statewide but rare outside northern Virginia, associated with late Middle Archaic period.

name: steatite vessels
description: Heavy and bulky stone vessels likely transported from distant quarry sources by boat, used for food preparation and processing.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: A geographic region where evidence for the use of water craft is generally absent prior to steatite vessel use.

name: grooved axes
description: Groundstone tools that first appear during the Late Archaic period, used possibly for fire-dugout boat building and as grubbing implements.

name: Middle Archaic period
description: A chronological period when chipped stone axes were used, preceding the Late Archaic groundstone axes.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: A chronological period in Virginia prehistory characterized by specific lithic tool traditions and the first appearance of slate gorgets or pendants.

name: slate gorgets
description: Lithic artifacts first appearing in the Late Archaic, often with drilled holes and incised cross hatching, whose function is unknown but may have been fastened to clothing.

name: Early Woodland
description: A chronological period following the Late Archaic in which slate gorgets/pendants equally occur.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A chronological period through which two hole pendants often date, according to Egloff.

name: Late Woodland
description: A chronological period in which slate gorgets can be found and with which one hole pendants seem to have an association.

name: Egloff
description: A researcher who provided personal communication in 2011 about pendant dating patterns.

name: Savannah River tradition
description: A morphological tool tradition that is archaeologically dominant in the Late Archaic period.

name: Perkiomen tradition
description: A morphological tool tradition found in Late Archaic occupations alongside Savannah River components.

name: Gardner
description: A researcher cited for work on Late Archaic material culture categorization published in 2000.

name: Chesapeake region
description: A physiographic region where Dent's narrow blade-broad blade distinction was specifically tailored and applied.

name: Dent
description: A researcher who developed the narrow blade tradition-broad blade tradition dichotomy in 1995.

name: Blanton
description: A researcher who in 2003 pointed out limitations of Dent's blade distinction approach outside the Chesapeake region.

name: Northeast
description: A physiographic/cultural realm that encompasses the uppermost reaches of the Chesapeake region.

name: Virginia
description: The state/commonwealth for which researchers are developing material culture frameworks and sequences.

name: Broadspears
description: Projectile points that include narrow-blade variants, creating confusion in material culture classification.

name: Woodland era
description: A cultural period mentioned in contrast to the Archaic period, characterized by ceramics as primary material culture markers.

name: Archaic period
description: A cultural period characterized by projectile point styles rather than ceramics as primary material culture markers.

name: Late Archaic
description: A subdivision of the Archaic period characterized by accelerated regionalization and increasing cultural diversity expressed through material culture variations.

name: Custer (1989)
description: Researcher who formulated a non-traditional classification scheme for projectile points that rejected the Coe Axiom, arguing that morphological variability in projectile point form is not chronologically specific after 5000 B.C.

name: Coe Axiom
description: Archaeological concept that limited morphological variability in projectile point form is chronologically specific.

name: Broyles (1971)
description: Researcher who presented Archaic point sequences in West Virginia that are considered valid according to Custer.

name: West Virginia
description: State where Broyles conducted research on Archaic point sequences.

name: Chapman (1977)
description: Researcher who worked on point sequences along the Tennessee River.

name: Tennessee River
description: River along which Chapman conducted archaeological research on point sequences.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period during which projectile point morphologies became temporally significant according to some researchers.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Cultural period when the manufacture of contracting stemmed points began.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period that extends the timeline of contracting stemmed point manufacture.

name: Morrow Mountain
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point in the long sequence of Middle Atlantic point styles.

name: Cattle Run
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point in the Middle Atlantic sequence.

name: Savannah River Variant
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point in the Middle Atlantic sequence.

name: Poplar Island
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point in the Middle Atlantic sequence.

name: Piscataway
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point that has temporal disagreement among researchers and may have cultural/temporal relationship with Rossville points.

name: Rossville
description: Type of contracting stemmed projectile point that may have cultural/temporal relationship with Piscataway points, but separated from Morrow Mountain by several millennia.

name: Chesapeake Diamond
description: Temporally ill-defined projectile point formally described by Hranicky and Painter (1989), with disputed temporal affiliation ranging from Middle Archaic to Late Archaic.

name: Adena
description: Type of projectile point that occasionally occurs in the Middle Atlantic region.

name: Koens-Crispin
description: Type of projectile point characteristic of the Northeast that may have cultural/temporal relationship with Savannah River points.

name: Lehigh
description: Type of projectile point characteristic of the Northeast region.

name: Snook Kill
description: Type of projectile point characteristic of the Northeast region.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region characterized by Koens-Crispin, Lehigh, and Snook Kill projectile point types.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where researchers are confronted with a confusing array of contracting stemmed points.

name: Hranicky and Painter (1989)
description: Researchers who formally described the Chesapeake Diamond point and postulated a general Archaic temporal affiliation for it.

name: Egloff
description: Researcher who prefers to place Chesapeake Diamond points in the late Middle Archaic to Late Archaic period and notes their predilection towards rhyolite and quartzite manufacture.

name: Dent 1995
description: A researcher who has ascribed Late Archaic dates for certain projectile point types.

name: Grow and Sharpe 2010
description: Researchers who have ascribed Late Archaic dates for certain projectile point types.

name: Piscataway
description: A projectile point type with clear Early Woodland contextual associations in Virginia.

name: Virginia
description: A state where Piscataway projectile points have clear Early Woodland contextual associations.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period during which Piscataway points have clear contextual associations in Virginia.

name: Egloff
description: A researcher who views the contracting stem style of point manufacture as related to hafting approach and possibly reflective of bone handles/foreshafts use.

name: Morrow Mountain
description: A projectile point type representing part of the extremely long-lived, contracting stem style of point manufacture.

name: Chesapeake Diamond
description: A projectile point type representing part of the extremely long-lived, contracting stem style of point manufacture.

name: Savannah River Variant
description: A projectile point type representing part of the extremely long-lived, contracting stem style of point manufacture.

name: Rossville
description: A projectile point type representing part of the extremely long-lived, contracting stem style of point manufacture.

name: atlatl
description: A weapon technology that appears to have been in use from Paleoindian times through at least the first half of the Woodland period.

name: Paleoindian
description: An early cultural period when the atlatl weapon technology was in use.

name: Woodland
description: A cultural period during which the atlatl was used at least through the first half of the period.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period that is part of the material culture sequences being studied and about which much uncertainty remains.

name: Middle Archaic
description: A cultural period that is part of the stemmed projectile point traditions being studied.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period that is part of the stemmed projectile point traditions being studied.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: Archaeological period characterized by distinctive material culture and behavioral patterns that was first documented by pioneering regional archaeologists in the mid-20th century.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Joffre L. Coe
description: Pioneering regional archaeologist who documented basic manifestations of the Late Archaic period in 1964.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: W. Fred Kinsey III
description: Pioneering regional archaeologist who contributed to documenting Late Archaic manifestations in 1959.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: William A. Ritchie
description: Pioneering regional archaeologist who documented Late Archaic manifestations in 1965.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: John Witthoft
description: Pioneering regional archaeologist who contributed to documenting Late Archaic manifestations in 1953.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: William Henry Holmes
description: Late 19th century archaeologist who investigated the Late Archaic Piney Branch Quarry site in Washington DC in 1890.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Gerard Fowke
description: Late 19th century archaeologist who conducted surveys in the James and Potomac drainages in 1894.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Piney Branch Quarry Site
aliases: Late Archaic Piney Branch Quarry site
description: Late Archaic archaeological site in Washington DC investigated by Holmes in 1890, representing one of the earliest formal archaeological endeavors in the United States.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: James River
description: River drainage system where Gerard Fowke conducted archaeological surveys in 1894, including areas below Richmond.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Potomac River
description: River drainage system where Gerard Fowke conducted archaeological surveys in 1894.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Washington DC
description: Location of the Piney Branch Quarry site investigated by Holmes in 1890.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: National Historic Preservation Act
description: Federal legislation that included Section 106 mandates, which significantly increased archaeological data pertaining to the Late Archaic period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Archaeological Society of Virginia
description: Avocational archaeological organization that conducted excavations at Daugherty's Cave and published ASV Quarterly Bulletin articles on Late Archaic studies.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Archaeological site in Southwest Virginia excavated by the Archaeological Society of Virginia, documented by Benthall in 1975.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Deep Bottom Site Complex
description: Archaeological site complex located along the tidal James River below Richmond, investigated by multiple researchers including Buchannan and Egghart.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Regional area where Daugherty's Cave was located and where Holland's 1970 survey documented numerous Archaic period sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Richmond
description: City near which the Deep Bottom Site Complex is located along the tidal James River.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Joseph T. Benthall
description: Archaeologist who documented the Archaeological Society of Virginia excavations at Daugherty's Cave in 1975.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Robert Buchannan
description: Archaeologist who conducted investigations at the Deep Bottom Site Complex, publishing work in 1969 and 1974.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Carole L. Egghart
description: Archaeologist who conducted investigations at the Deep Bottom Site Complex, publishing work in 2014.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Ben C. McCary
description: Archaeologist who produced numerous ASV Quarterly Bulletin articles on Late Archaic studies from 1972-1977, including work on the Perkiomen tradition.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Perkiomen Tradition
description: Late Archaic cultural manifestation identified by McCary along the western margins of the Dismal Swamp in 1972.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Geographic area where McCary identified the presence of the Perkiomen tradition and other Late Archaic manifestations along its western margins.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Late Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period that followed the Late Archaic, characterized by ceramic bearing sites that attracted avocational community attention.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Contact Period
description: Archaeological period characterized by ceramic bearing sites that attracted attention from the avocational archaeological community.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Luckenbach Study
description: Groundbreaking 1975 study on the movement of Piedmont steatite vessels utilizing neutron activation in trace element analysis.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Piedmont Steatite Vessels
description: Archaeological artifacts studied by Luckenbach et al. in 1975 using neutron activation analysis to understand their movement patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: C. G. Holland
description: Archaeologist who conducted a 1970 survey of Southwest Virginia that documented numerous Archaic period site locations.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Archaic Period
description: Archaeological period that includes the Late Archaic, with numerous site locations documented in Southwest Virginia by Holland's 1970 survey.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Section 106
description: Federal mandate part of the National Historic Preservation Act that exponentially increased archaeological data pertaining to the Late Archaic period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Blue Ridge Mountains
description: Mountain range representing upland areas across Virginia where Cultural Resource Management data shed light on Late Archaic use.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Allegheny Mountains
description: Highland region where Cultural Resource Management data provided information on Late Archaic upland area use.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Michael Barber
description: Archaeologist who conducted wide ranging surveys on National Forest Service lands relevant to Late Archaic research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: George Tolley
description: Archaeologist who conducted wide ranging surveys on National Forest Service lands relevant to Late Archaic research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Paul Inashima
description: Archaeologist who conducted efforts in the Shenandoah National Park relevant to Late Archaic research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Shenandoah National Park
description: National park where Paul Inashima conducted archaeological efforts relevant to Late Archaic research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: William Gardner
description: Archaeologist associated with the Thunderbird organization who provided new data from the Shenandoah Valley and surrounding areas.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Thunderbird Organization
description: Archaeological organization led by William Gardner that provided new Late Archaic data from the Shenandoah Valley and surrounding areas.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region where the Thunderbird organization provided new Late Archaic archaeological data.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: Virginia
description: State where Late Archaic archaeological research has been conducted, including upland areas and various regions documented through Cultural Resource Management projects.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: National Forest Service
description: Federal agency on whose lands Michael Barber and George Tolley conducted wide ranging archaeological surveys relevant to Late Archaic research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part0

name: James Madison University
description: University that housed one of the archaeological research centers contributing to Late Archaic research in Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Virginia Commonwealth University
description: University that housed one of the archaeological research centers contributing to Late Archaic research in Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: College of William and Mary
description: University that housed one of the archaeological research centers contributing to Late Archaic research in Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Fairfax County Heritage Resource Branch
description: Organization that made significant contributions to the development of public archaeology in Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Virginia Landmarks Commission
description: State organization (later renamed Department of Historic Resources) whose staff made notable contributions to understanding the Late Archaic period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Potter
description: Researcher who conducted dissertation excavations (1982) along the lower Potomac providing detailed views of Late Archaic settlement and subsistence trends.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Wasselkov
description: Researcher who conducted dissertation excavations (1982) along the lower Potomac providing detailed views of Late Archaic settlement and subsistence trends.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: lower Potomac
description: Geographic region along the Potomac River where Potter and Wasselkov conducted excavations revealing Late Archaic settlement and subsistence patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Large bay region where detailed Late Archaic settlement and subsistence trends were documented through archaeological research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Council of Virginia Archaeologists
description: Archaeological organization that published the important 1991 synthesis 'Late Archaic and Early Woodland Research in Virginia' edited by Reinhart and Hodges.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Late Archaic and Early Woodland Research in Virginia: A Synthesis
description: 1991 publication edited by Reinhart and Hodges that synthesized Late Archaic research and made fragmented archaeological data more accessible.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Reinhart
description: Co-editor of the 1991 COVA publication 'Late Archaic and Early Woodland Research in Virginia: A Synthesis'.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Hodges
description: Co-editor of the 1991 COVA publication who framed Late Archaic issues within broader regional and extra-regional context.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: McLearen
description: Researcher who provided a succinct review of Late Archaic material culture in the 1991 COVA volume and conducted research in Southwest Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Mouer
description: Researcher who discussed the formative trends that define the Late Archaic period in the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Klein
description: Researcher who presented demographic analyses of the Late Archaic period in the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Klatka
description: Researcher who presented demographic analyses of the Late Archaic period in the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Stevens
description: Researcher who elucidated fresh perspectives on paleoecology and cultural ecology in the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Barber
description: Researcher who discussed subsistence patterns and settlement implications of the Late Archaic period in the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Chesapeake Prehistory
description: Synthetic work published by Dent in 1995 that augmented the studies from the 1991 COVA volume.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Dent
description: Author who published 'Chesapeake Prehistory' in 1995, a synthetic work on regional archaeology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Late Archaic: An Updated Overview
description: 2003 publication by Blanton intended to update research first presented in the 1991 COVA volume, providing fresh synthesis of Late Archaic archaeological data.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Blanton
description: Author who published 'Late Archaic: An Updated Overview' in 2003, providing fresh synthesis of archaeological data and perspectives on Late Archaic environmental and cultural dynamics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: southeastern Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region used by Blanton as a case example for articulating perspectives on Late Archaic environmental conditions and cultural dynamics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Virginia Department of Transportation
description: State agency that sponsored at least nine Phase III Data Recovery projects on sites with major Late Archaic components from 1990-2000.

name: Coastal Plain Appomattox
description: Geographic region where Virginia Department of Transportation sponsored Phase III Data Recovery projects on Late Archaic sites (Stuck et al. 1997).

name: Piedmont James River

name: north-central Piedmont uplands
description: Geographic region where Virginia Department of Transportation sponsored Phase III Data Recovery projects on Late Archaic sites (Jones 2000).
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Southwest Virginia

name: Stuck
description: Lead researcher on 1997 Phase III Data Recovery project along the Coastal Plain Appomattox with major Late Archaic components.

name: Fiedel
description: Co-researcher on 1997 Phase III Data Recovery project along the Piedmont James River with major Late Archaic components.

name: Baliki
description: Co-researcher on 1997 Phase III Data Recovery project along the Piedmont James River with major Late Archaic components.

name: Pullins

name: Monroe

source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Jones
description: Researcher who conducted 2000 Phase III Data Recovery project in the north-central Piedmont uplands with major Late Archaic components.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Egghart
description: Researcher who conducted 1991 Phase III Data Recovery project in Southwest Virginia with major Late Archaic components.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Voigt
description: Researcher who conducted 2000 Phase III Data Recovery project in Southwest Virginia with major Late Archaic components.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Late ArchaicArchaeological Research To Date_part1

name: Daugherty's Cave

name: Archaeological Society of Virginia
description: Organization that conducted archaeological work at Daugherty's Cave, published by Benthall in 1990.

name: Benthall

name: McAvoy

name: Slade Site

name: Nottoway River

name: Late Archaic

name: Northern Virginia

name: Tidewater

name: Jefferson National Forest
description: Western mountainous area of the Commonwealth that has witnessed significant archaeological work.

name: George Washington National Forest
description: Western mountainous area of the Commonwealth that has witnessed significant archaeological work.

name: Commonwealth

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period that is the focus of the archaeological research described, characterized by sites with limitations including paucity of intact deposits and poor organic preservation.

name: Archaic
