name: Late Archaic
description: A prehistoric time period in Virginia archaeology that is the focus of future research needs, characterized by specific material culture, settlement patterns, and subsistence practices.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to the Commonwealth of Virginia, where Late Archaic sites are found across all regions and need systematic excavation.

name: Virginia
description: The state where Late Archaic research is being conducted and future research directions are being planned.

name: Savannah River
description: Signature archaeological remains associated with a dominant Late Archaic culture that originated in the Southeast and spread north into Virginia and beyond.

name: Virginia
description: State where Savannah River culture spread and Late Archaic complexes are found in archaeological record.

name: Southeast
description: Region of origin for the Savannah River culture that spread north into Virginia.

name: James River Crescent
description: Geographic region stretching from the salt tidal zone to the high ridges of the western headwaters where Savannah River culture flourished.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period associated with steatite vessel manufacture and use, and various cultural complexes.

name: steatite vessels
description: Stone vessels manufactured and used during the Late Archaic period, associated with changing subsistence ways and social complexity.

name: Potomac drainage
description: Geographic region northwards where Late Archaic complexes like Lamoka, Poplar Island, and others are typical.

name: Lamoka
description: Late Archaic complex typical of the Potomac drainage region.

name: Poplar Island
description: Late Archaic complex typical of the Potomac drainage region.

name: Bare Island
description: Late Archaic complex typical of the Potomac drainage region.

name: Perkiomen
description: Late Archaic complex typical of the Potomac drainage region.

name: Susquehanna
description: Late Archaic complex typical of the Potomac drainage region.

name: Northeast
description: Region where elaborate Late Archaic mortuary practices are documented.

name: Christopher Egghart
description: Author affiliated with Virginia Department of Environmental Quality.

name: Early Woodland
description: A distinct cultural period in Virginia prehistory characterized by the adoption of ceramics, pronounced settlement shifts, and major changes in lithic tool manufacture and use.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region and state where Early Woodland archaeology is being studied, containing various physiographic provinces.

name: Archaic
description: The cultural period preceding the Early Woodland, characterized by hunter-gatherer lifeways and the use of steatite vessels.

name: Late Archaic
description: The later portion of the Archaic period that shows some incipient social stratification and is linked to the Early Woodland through material culture traits.

name: Woodland
description: A broader cultural period that includes the Early Woodland and is traditionally demarcated by the appearance of ceramics in the archaeological record.

name: ceramics
description: Pottery that marks the hallmark of the Early Woodland era, functioning similarly to steatite vessels for food preparation and storage, and as socially charged markers.

name: steatite vessels
description: Stone vessels from the preceding Archaic era that functioned similarly to early ceramics in food preparation and storage.

name: carved bowls
description: Late Archaic stone vessels that show morphological similarity to early ceramics, underscoring the transitional nature of period material culture.

name: Broadspear-derived projectile point forms
description: Stone projectile points that continued to be used during the Early Woodland, linking it to the preceding Archaic period.

name: ground stone implements
description: Stone tools that were ground rather than chipped, continuing to be produced and used during the Early Woodland period.

name: slate accoutrements
description: Items made from slate that continued to be produced and used during the Early Woodland, linking it to the Archaic period.

name: COVA synthesis volume
description: An earlier archaeological publication that this chapter builds upon by reviewing investigations carried out since its publication.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by ameliorating climate compared to the more dynamic conditions of the Late Archaic.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period portrayed as having relative warmth but with significant fluctuations in both temperature and precipitation patterns.

name: Subatlantic phase
description: A climatic phase characterized by general cooling with more regular precipitation patterns.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary in Virginia, east of which the Chesapeake Bay was a significant environmental factor.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A significant environmental factor for areas east of the Fall Line that had largely reached its modern configuration during the preceding millennia.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A region where pollen profiles indicate establishment of essentially modern forest communities before the Early Woodland period.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A geographic feature in Virginia, east of which pine species would have had greater occurrence during warm/dry episodes.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A normally dryer locale that may have hosted broken forest and patchy grassland during severe warm/dry episodes.

name: Nottoway River
description: A river in southeast Virginia where quality dendrochronological data has been obtained from bald cypress.

name: Ontario
description: A location where dendrochronological data has been obtained from white cedars.

name: Northern Europe
description: A region where comprehensive dendrochronological sequences including Irish oak have been established.

name: Greenland
description: Location of ice cores that have provided expanding data about North Atlantic climate.

name: North Atlantic
description: A region whose climate data from ice cores shows it was warmer around cal 1200 BC than any time since cal 7500 BC.

name: Southwest United States
description: A region where dendrochronological studies were once largely limited before expanding to East Coast locations.

name: East Coast
description: A region where recent studies have established fairly robust dendrochronological sequences.

name: cal 1200 BC
description: A time period when North Atlantic climate was warmer than any time since cal 7500 BC according to ice core data.

name: cal 7500 BC
description: A reference point for comparing North Atlantic climate warmth, used in ice core data analysis.

name: ca. 1000 cal BC
description: A time period when North Atlantic climate experienced a sudden drop of two degrees C according to ice core data.

name: Greenland
description: Geographic location referenced for ice core data that provides evidence of pronounced cooling events during the Early Woodland period.

name: Europe
description: Geographic region referenced for tree ring data that provides evidence of cooling events around 850 BC and 1159 BC.

name: Bond Events
description: Periodic Holocene cooling episodes of the North Atlantic climate that recurred on a 1500 +/- 500-year interval throughout much of the Holocene.

name: North Atlantic
description: Ocean region where episodic cool downs are signaled by the presence of ice rafted sediment/debris on the sea floor.

name: Holocene
description: Geological epoch during which Bond Events recurred on regular intervals and radiocarbon curve patterns were observed.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period that experienced significant cooling around 1000 cal BC and multiple pronounced cooling episodes, with stable floodplain conditions between 3000 BP and 2000 BP.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period preceding the Early Woodland that was more dynamic, with alluviation dominant along larger drainages and some instability evident on smaller tributaries.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period that followed the Early Woodland, mentioned in context of floodplain deposition patterns at archaeological sites.

name: Grand Solar Minimum
description: Periodic decrease in solar output around 800 BC that resulted in global cooling and had pronounced effects on climate through atmospheric circulation pattern changes.

name: Potomac
description: River drainage system in Virginia where stable Early Woodland archaeological surfaces have been observed.

name: Virginia
description: State where stable Early Woodland archaeological surfaces have been observed at multiple locations.

name: 44WR0232
description: Archaeological site along the South Fork of the Shenandoah in Front Royal that evidenced Early Woodland floodplain stability with a clear, stable occupation floor.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah
description: River location where archaeological site 44WR0232 demonstrates Early Woodland floodplain stability and occupation patterns.

name: Front Royal
description: Town location in Virginia where archaeological site 44WR0232 is situated along the South Fork of the Shenandoah.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period that experienced somewhat cooler and moister climate than the preceding Late Archaic, with reduced pine in many Virginia locations and establishment of climax forest conditions.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period that preceded the Early Woodland, during which grasslands, broken forest, or fire-maintained communities existed.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major estuarine basin in eastern Virginia that established fully modern estuarine conditions during the Early Woodland and achieved its modern configuration by the Late Archaic.

name: Virginia
description: A region with diverse physical areas that likely exerted significant influence on prehistoric settlement and economic life ways.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physical region of Virginia that represents a particularly salient divide from the Piedmont with respect to Early Woodland settlement.

name: Piedmont
description: A physical region of Virginia characterized by expansive floodplains in major river valleys, representing a divide from the Coastal Plain.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic feature of particular importance due to its central location with respect to diverse physical and environmental settings and as a prime location to exploit anadromous fish.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain range that represents a formidable demarcation, relatively low in far northern Virginia but rising in elevation further south to form an impressive massif separating the Piedmont from the Ridge and Valley.

name: Potomac River
description: One of the major rivers in Virginia that traverses the Blue Ridge province and provides a direct route into the northern Shenandoah Valley and the Ridge and Valley, helping to channel human movement.

name: James River
description: One of the major rivers in Virginia that traverses the Blue Ridge province and can be thought of as dividing the northern and southern Blue Ridge subregions, with its break through the Blue Ridge having a significant role in channeling settlement.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A valley region accessible via the Potomac River route through the Blue Ridge in northern Virginia.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A physiographic region accessible via the Potomac River route and separated from the Piedmont by the Blue Ridge massif.

name: New River Valley
description: A valley that is part of the greater Ohio drainage, characterized by open character, ample floodplains, fertile limestone soils, and diverse resource base that supported intensive Late Woodland settlement.

name: Ohio drainage
description: A major river drainage system that includes the New River Valley and much of the Virginia Appalachian Plateau.

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period during which intensive settlement occurred in the New River Valley environs.

name: Virginia Appalachian Plateau
description: A physiographic region within the greater Ohio River watershed, characterized by rugged uplands and deeply incised valleys that are unique in the Commonwealth.

name: Tennessee River drainage
description: A river system in far southwest Virginia that likely remained under cultural influence of the interior Southeast due to its remote location and difficult access from the east.

name: Piedmont
description: The largest physiographic province in Virginia, divided into Northern and Southern sub-areas demarcated by the James River.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A physiographic province in Virginia that, like the Piedmont, can be divided into Northern and Southern sub-areas.

name: Northern Piedmont
description: The northern section of the Piedmont province, cross-cut by three principal Chesapeake Bay tributaries.

name: James River
description: A major river that demarcates the Northern and Southern sub-areas of the Piedmont and Ridge and Valley provinces, and is one of the principal Chesapeake Bay tributaries.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major water body whose tributaries include the Potomac, Rappahannock, and James rivers.

name: Potomac
description: One of the three principal Chesapeake Bay tributaries that cross-cut the Northern Piedmont.

name: Rappahannock
description: One of the three principal Chesapeake Bay tributaries that cross-cut the Northern Piedmont.

name: Southern Piedmont
description: The southern section of the Piedmont province where the Roanoke River is the largest drainage.

name: Roanoke River
description: The largest drainage in the Southern Piedmont, part of the greater Carolina Sounds watershed that connected the Southern Piedmont to the greater Southeastern cultural realm.

name: Carolina Sounds watershed
description: A watershed system that includes the Roanoke River and connected the Southern Piedmont to the greater Southeastern cultural realm.

name: Dan River
description: The largest tributary of the Roanoke River, connecting the Southern Piedmont to the greater Southeastern cultural realm.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period whose settlement patterns and site preservation are discussed in relation to various physiographic settings in Virginia.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic region where significant portions of prime Early Woodland settlement locations have been lost to rising sea levels and shoreline regression.

name: Middle and Inner Coastal Plain
description: Regions where tidal inundation of major floodplains may have directly influenced Early Woodland settlement patterns.

name: Savannah River tradition
description: Archaeological tradition from which Early Woodland projectile point types appear to be derived, with a reduction in size evident over time.

name: Small Savannah River
description: Projectile point type that spans the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition, appearing to be a scaled down version of the Late Archaic Broadspear with a squatter, more squared stem.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period preceding the Early Woodland, associated with the Broadspear point type and ending around the time Small Savannah River points appear.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period characterized by specific projectile point types and the introduction of ceramic wares, following the Late Archaic period.

name: Calvert
description: Projectile point type derived from Savannah River tradition, typically short, thick in cross section, with a pronounced, fully squared stem and strong preference for quartz material.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region where Gypsy Stemmed projectile points are mostly found.

name: Gypsy Stemmed
description: Projectile point type found mostly in Southwest Virginia that appears derived from Savannah River tradition.

name: Broadspear
description: Late Archaic projectile point type that Small Savannah River points appear to be a scaled down version of.

name: Nottoway drainage
description: River drainage system where stratigraphic positions of Small Savannah River points were recorded, placing them towards the end of the Late Archaic.

name: James River Piedmont
description: Geographic region where Small Savannah River points have been found in association with early ceramic wares.

name: Accokeek Creek Site
description: Archaeological site where the Calvert point was first described by Stephenson and Ferguson in 1963.

name: White Oak Point Site
description: Archaeological site along the tidal Potomac where firmly dated Calvert point contexts were found, with three overlapping dates averaging around 3050 BP.

name: tidal Potomac
description: Geographic location along the Potomac River where the White Oak Point Site is situated.

name: Small Stemmed
description: Provisional designation applied to small, stemmed projectile points that do not readily conform to formally described types, occurring widely in Early Woodland contexts across Virginia.

name: Clark's Branch Site
description: Archaeological site in the Northern Virginia Piedmont where Gypsy Stemmed points were recovered from dated Early Woodland contexts together with Selden Island ceramics.

name: Northern Virginia Piedmont
description: Geographic region where the Clark's Branch Site is located.

name: Selden Island ceramics
description: Early Woodland ceramic type found together with Gypsy Stemmed points at the Clark's Branch Site.

name: Susquehanna points
description: Projectile point type traditionally dated to the latter portion of the Late Archaic but also found in Early Woodland contexts, suggesting continued use into the ceramic era.

name: Transitional Period
description: Alternative name used in the Northeast for the era when Susquehanna points were traditionally dated, corresponding to the latter portion of the Late Archaic.

name: Terminal Archaic
description: Alternative name used in the Northeast for the era when Susquehanna points were traditionally dated, corresponding to the latter portion of the Late Archaic.

name: North Fork of the Shenandoah
description: River location where Susquehanna points have been recovered from sealed, dated Early Woodland contexts.

name: South Fork
description: River fork in Front Royal where Susquehanna points have been recovered from sealed, dated Early Woodland contexts.

name: Front Royal
description: Location where Susquehanna points have been found in Early Woodland contexts along the South Fork.

name: Virginia
description: State where small, stemmed points occur widely in Early Woodland contexts and where various projectile point types have been studied.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region where the Transitional Period or Terminal Archaic era has been defined in relation to Susquehanna points.

name: Fishtail
description: A projectile point type with wide but relatively uneven distribution across Virginia, appearing to be rooted in the Late Archaic and associated with steatite vessels.

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region where various Early Woodland projectile points have been found and studied.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period associated with various projectile point types including Fishtail, Piscataway, and others.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period that appears to be the root origin of several Early Woodland point types including Fishtail.

name: Ritchie
description: Researcher who originally referred to the Fishtail point type as Orient Fishtail in 1965 and 1971.

name: Dry Brook
description: A projectile point type very similar to Fishtail, described by Kinsey in 1972, which appears to intergrade with Fishtail.

name: Kinsey
description: Researcher who described the Dry Brook point type in 1972.

name: Susquehanna
description: A projectile point type that shows intergrade characteristics with the Fishtail type.

name: Piscataway
description: Perhaps the most widely distributed projectile point associated with the Early Woodland in Virginia, foliate to lozenge-shaped with possible Late Archaic origins.

name: Stephenson
description: Researcher who, along with Ferguson, first described the Piscataway point type in 1963 at the Accokeek Creek Site.

name: Ferguson
description: Researcher who, along with Stephenson, first described the Piscataway point type in 1963 at the Accokeek Creek Site.

name: Accokeek Creek Site
description: Archaeological site where the Piscataway point type was first described by Stephenson and Ferguson in 1963.

name: Dent
description: Researcher who suggested Late Archaic origins for the Piscataway point type in 1995.

name: Luckenbach
description: Researcher who suggested Late Archaic origins for the Piscataway point type in 2010.

name: Teardrop
description: A projectile point type very similar in form to Piscataway but has not been radiocarbon dated in Virginia, though dated to Early Woodland in the Northeast.

name: Mounier
description: Researcher who, along with Martin, described the Teardrop point type in 1994.

name: Martin
description: Researcher who, along with Mounier, described the Teardrop point type in 1994.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region where radiocarbon samples have dated Teardrop points to the Early Woodland period.

name: Petraglia
description: Researcher involved in radiocarbon dating studies of Teardrop points in the Northeast in 1998.

name: Will's Cove
description: An Early Woodland point type with elongated, slightly excurvate blade, pronounced shoulders and a short, straight stem, originally described by Bottoms in 1979.

name: Broadspear tradition
description: A projectile point tradition to which Will's Cove appears to have no connection.

name: Bottoms
description: Researcher who originally described the Will's Cove point type in 1979.

name: Egloff
description: Researcher who elaborated on Will's Cove points in 2016 and has provided various point type classifications.

name: Poplar Island
description: A Late Archaic projectile point tradition that may have a genetic connection to Will's Cove points.

name: Bare Island
description: A Late Archaic projectile point tradition that may have a genetic connection to Will's Cove points.

name: Vernon
description: A projectile point type possibly of Early Woodland age, first described by Stephenson and Ferguson in 1963, though some researchers place it in late Middle Archaic.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period to which some researchers assign the Vernon point type, citing similarity to Halifax points.

name: Halifax
description: A projectile point type similar to Vernon, associated with late Middle Archaic period.

name: Nash
description: Researcher who, along with Egloff, ascribed an Early Woodland association to Vernon points in 2009.

name: Ogle Projectile Point collection
description: A collection of projectile points from Sussex County sites used for comparative analysis of point type distributions.

name: Sussex County
description: Geographic area in Virginia where the Ogle Projectile Point collection sites are located.

name: Egghart
description: Researcher who, along with Manson, analyzed the Ogle Projectile Point collection in 2016.

name: Manson
description: Researcher who, along with Egghart, analyzed the Ogle Projectile Point collection in 2016.

name: Badin
description: Large, crude triangle projectile points long regarded as dating to the Early Woodland, described by Coe in 1964 from the North Carolina Piedmont.

name: Coe
description: Researcher who described the Badin point type in 1964 and worked at the Doerschuk Site.

name: North Carolina Piedmont
description: Geographic region where the Badin point type was originally found and described.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period to which Egloff assigns Badin points based on stratigraphic contexts at the Doerschuk Site.

name: Doerschuk Site
description: Archaeological site where stratigraphic contexts helped inform the dating of Badin points to early Middle Woodland.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period mentioned in relation to triangular projectile points that Badin-like points might represent preforms for.

name: Archaic
description: Archaeological period referenced in comparative studies of projectile point distributions in the Ogle Collection.

name: Bushnell Plain
description: May be the earliest ceramic in Virginia, characterized by coil construction with smoothed surfaces and schist particle temper with grog, fiber, and sometimes steatite.

name: David Bushnell
description: Person who identified Bushnell Plain ceramic type during pioneering excavations in Caroline County.

name: Caroline County
description: Location where David Bushnell conducted pioneering excavations that identified Bushnell Plain ceramics.

name: Marcey Creek
description: Ceramic type defined by crushed steatite temper comprising up to 50% of paste, with widespread occurrence from southeastern Virginia across the Coastal Plain.

name: Marcey Creek Site
description: Archaeological site along the Potomac River Falls near Washington, D.C. where the Marcey Creek ceramic type was originally defined.

name: Potomac River Falls
description: Location near Washington, D.C. where the Marcey Creek Site is situated.

name: Washington, D.C.
description: City near which the Marcey Creek Site is located along the Potomac River Falls.

name: Potomac drainage
description: Geographic region where Marcey Creek ceramics have widespread occurrence, extending inland from the Coastal Plain.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region across which Marcey Creek ceramics have widespread occurrence.

name: southeastern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Marcey Creek ceramics have widespread occurrence.

name: Selden Island
description: Ceramic type similar to Marcey Creek with crushed steatite temper and cord marked surface treatment, distinguished by always being coil constructed and usually conical form.

name: Ware Plain
description: Sand and grit tempered variant of Marcey Creek that lacks steatite as a tempering agent, first described at the Ware Creek Site.

name: Catherine McCane
description: Person who first described the Ware Plain ceramic type in 1950 at the Ware Creek Site in New Jersey.

name: Ware Creek Site
description: Archaeological site in New Jersey where Catherine McCane first described the Ware Plain ceramic type.

name: New Jersey
description: State where the Ware Creek Site is located.

name: Croaker Landing
description: Ceramic ware tempered with grog (previously fired clay) comprising up to 50% of the paste, with smoothed interior surfaces.

name: Croaker Landing Site
description: Archaeological site along the York River where the Croaker Landing ceramic ware was first described.

name: York River
description: River along which the Croaker Landing Site is located.

name: Teardrop
description: Early Woodland point type found east of the Blue Ridge, transitioning from Early to Middle Woodland periods.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountain range used as a geographic reference for artifact distributions in Virginia.

name: Small Stemmed
description: Point type with statewide distribution during Early Woodland period.

name: Gypsy Stemmed
description: Early Woodland point type found in far south and southwest Virginia.

name: Badin
description: Early Woodland point type distributed in south and east Virginia.

name: Will's Cove
description: Early Woodland point type found in the Coastal Plain of Virginia.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region of Virginia where various archaeological artifacts are found.

name: Calvert
description: Early Woodland point type distributed in north and east Virginia.

name: Small Savannah River
description: Point type with statewide distribution spanning Late Archaic into Early Woodland periods.

name: Piscataway
description: Point type found statewide except southwest Virginia, dating to Late Archaic and Early Woodland periods.

name: Fishtail Intermittent
description: Point type with statewide distribution spanning Late Archaic into Early Woodland periods.

name: Susquehanna Intermittent
description: Point type with statewide distribution spanning Late Archaic into Early Woodland periods.

name: Vernon
description: Point type found in north and east Virginia, possibly dating to Middle Archaic or Early Woodland periods.

name: Currituck
description: Ceramic type characterized by flat-bottomed jar or beaker shape, first reported at the Currituck Site in northeast North Carolina and found in Virginia as far north as the York River.

name: Currituck Site
description: Archaeological site in northeast North Carolina where Currituck ceramics were first reported by Painter in 1977.

name: York River
description: River in Virginia representing the northern extent of Currituck ceramic distribution.

name: Accokeek
description: The most common Early Woodland ceramic in Virginia, defined at the Accokeek Creek Site along the tidal Potomac in Prince Georges County, Maryland.

name: Accokeek Creek Site
description: Archaeological site along the tidal Potomac in Prince Georges County, Maryland where Accokeek ceramics were defined by Stephenson and Ferguson in 1963.

name: Potomac
description: River where the Accokeek Creek Site is located in Maryland.

name: Prince Georges County
description: County in Maryland where the Accokeek Creek Site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where the Accokeek Creek Site is located.

name: James River Piedmont
description: Geographic region in Virginia where the Elk Island variant of Accokeek ceramics was described.

name: Elk Island
description: Regional variant of Accokeek ceramics found in the James River Piedmont, characterized by flattened bases and occasional lug handles.

name: Stony Creek
description: Ceramic ware similar to Elk Island, found primarily in far southeastern Virginia and described by Evans in 1955.

name: Marcey Creek
description: Early ceramic tradition to which various additional early ceramic wares in Virginia appear related.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Marcey Creek-like ceramics with crushed rock temper have been observed.

name: Delmarva
description: Peninsula region where Experimental Phase ceramics and Dames Quarter ceramics are found.

name: Experimental Phase
description: Term coined by Wise in 1975 to describe early ceramic production in the Delmarva region characterized by variety of tempering agents and surface treatments.

name: Dames Quarter
description: Ceramic type found in northern and central Delmarva region, similar to Marcey Creek but using crushed hornblende or gneiss as tempering agent.

name: Virginia Eastern Shore
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Dames Quarter ceramics may extend from the Delmarva Peninsula.

name: Fall Zone
description: Geographic zone along the James River and Potomac where early ceramic finds are concentrated.

name: James River
description: Major river in Virginia where early ceramic finds are centered along its Fall Zone and Piedmont regions.

name: northern Virginia
description: Geographic region of Virginia with significant occurrences of early ceramic archaeological components.

name: outer Piedmont
description: Geographic region in northern Virginia with significant early ceramic archaeological components.

name: Shenandoah River
description: River system in Virginia where the lower reaches have significant early ceramic archaeological components.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region of Virginia where ceramics appear around 500 BC with Swannanoa ware.

name: Swannanoa
description: Crushed quartz/sand tempered ceramic ware that appears in Southwest Virginia around 500 BC.

name: Roanoke drainage
description: River drainage system in Virginia where Hyco Plain and Hyco Cord Marked ceramics are found.

name: Hyco Plain
description: Sand tempered ceramic ware found in the Roanoke drainage, first described by Miller in 1962.

name: Hyco Cord Marked
description: Sand tempered ceramic ware found in the Roanoke drainage, first described by Miller in 1962.

name: Early Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period during which various point types and ceramic traditions developed in Virginia.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period that succeeded the Early Woodland, referenced in relation to Teardrop point types.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period preceding Early Woodland, during which some point types continued to be used.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period possibly associated with Vernon point types.

name: Virginia
description: State where the archaeological point types and ceramic traditions described in the text are found.

name: Elk Island
aliases: Elk Island Coastal Plain and Southern Piedmont
description: A cultural tradition or ceramic variant associated with the Coastal Plain and Southern Piedmont regions, related to the greater Accokeek tradition.

name: Accokeek Variant
description: A ceramic variant found in various regions including Coastal Plain, Piedmont, and Potomac drainage areas, part of the greater Accokeek/Elk Island tradition.

name: Hyco
description: A location in the Southern Piedmont region associated with the Roanoke Drainage.

name: Southern Piedmont
description: A geographic region where various archaeological variants and traditions are found.

name: Roanoke Drainage
description: A drainage system or watershed area in the Southern Piedmont region.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where multiple ceramic traditions and archaeological variants are found.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region associated with various ceramic traditions and drainage systems.

name: Potomac drainage
description: A drainage system or watershed area where multiple ceramic variants are found.

name: Currituck
description: A ceramic variant found in the Far Southeast region.

name: Far Southeast
description: A geographic region associated with the Currituck ceramic variant.

name: Flat Bottom Beaker
description: A type of ceramic vessel or pottery form.

name: Croaker Landing
description: A ceramic variant found only in the Southeast region.

name: Southeast
description: A geographic region where the Croaker Landing ceramic variant is found.

name: Swannanoa
description: The oldest ceramic ware found in the Far Southwest region.

name: Far Southwest
description: A geographic region where the oldest Swannanoa ceramic ware is found.

name: Dames Quarter
description: A ceramic variant possibly found on the Eastern Shore.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A geographic region where the Dames Quarter ceramic variant may be found.

name: Ware Plain
description: A ceramic variant found in the Coastal Plain and Potomac drainage areas.

name: Selden Island
description: A ceramic variant found in the Coastal Plain and Potomac drainage areas.

name: Marcey Creek
description: A ceramic variant found in Coastal Plain, Piedmont, and Potomac drainage areas.

name: Bushnell Plain
description: A ceramic variant found in the Coastal Plain and Outer Piedmont regions.

name: Outer Piedmont
description: A geographic subregion of the Piedmont where the Bushnell Plain ceramic variant is found.

name: Early Woodland
description: A chronological period characterized by marked changes in lithic preferences and tool production.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region where mid-grade chert is commonly found in nodule form.

name: McLearen
description: A researcher who noted lithic utilization patterns across the Coastal Plain and Piedmont in 1991.

name: Broadspear
description: A type of projectile point form that was phased out early in the Early Woodland period.

name: Late Archaic
description: A chronological period during which distinctive ground stone axes first appeared.

name: Mouer
description: A researcher who noted in 1990 that ground stone tools resembling axes were likely used as grubbing implements.

name: James River Survey
description: A large scale archaeological salvage effort undertaken by Dan Mouer and staff of Virginia Commonwealth University in 1980 following severe flooding that scoured areas of the James River floodplain.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Dan Mouer
description: Lead archaeologist who conducted the James River Survey and authored multiple publications on Early Woodland occupation along the Piedmont James River.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Virginia Commonwealth University
description: Institution that conducted the James River Survey archaeological investigation in 1980.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: James River
description: River in Virginia where extensive evidence of Early Woodland occupation was revealed through archaeological investigation.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Richmond
description: City in Virginia, above which the James River floodplain was investigated during the James River Survey.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period characterized by dense deposits of fire-cracked rock, Accokeek and Elk Island ceramics, and associated projectile point types.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Accokeek
description: Type of Early Woodland ceramic found at sites along the James River and other locations in Virginia.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Elk Island
description: Type of Early Woodland ceramic found at sites along the James River and in other archaeological contexts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Small Savannah River
description: Type of projectile point associated with Early Woodland occupation along the James River.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Calvert
description: Type of projectile point associated with Early Woodland components and found in various Virginia archaeological contexts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Piscataway
description: Type of projectile point associated with Early Woodland occupation, occurring with lesser frequency than Vernon and Calvert points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Fishtails
description: Type of projectile point possibly associated with Early Woodland occupation, occurring with lesser frequency than other types.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Stoneman West
description: Archaeological site along the James River that was intensively investigated, revealing numerous pits, a large midden, and Early Woodland artifacts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Nash
description: Researcher who authored a 2009 dissertation focusing on modeling prehistoric settlement in Madison County.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Madison County
description: County in Virginia that crosscuts the Piedmont and Blue Ridge provinces, studied for prehistoric settlement patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountain region in Virginia with differing elevation and environmental characteristics from the Piedmont.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Piedmont
description: Physiographic province in Virginia where Early Woodland settlement was centered, particularly in the Inner Piedmont area.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Mesozoic Basin
description: Geological region within Madison County study area with distinct environmental characteristics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Rappahannock
description: River system whose named headwater forks drain the Madison County study area.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Vernon
description: Type of projectile point that, along with Calvert points, signaled Early Woodland components in Madison County.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period preceding the Early Woodland, during which base camps were established that continued to be utilized in the Early Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Klein
description: Researcher who, along with Klatka, observed a significant increase in floodplain versus upland settlement around 1000 BC in the central Piedmont.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Klatka
description: Researcher who, along with Klein, observed a significant increase in floodplain versus upland settlement around 1000 BC in the central Piedmont.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Arnold Valley Survey
description: Archaeological survey conducted by Barber in 2010 examining 152 sites in Arnold Valley along the Blue Ridge slope near the James River.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Barber
description: Researcher who conducted the Arnold Valley Survey in 2010, identifying and reviewing 152 sites in the topographically diverse valley.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Arnold Valley
description: Topographically diverse valley situated along the Blue Ridge slope near the point where the James River enters the Piedmont, extensively settled throughout prehistory but lacking Early Woodland components.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Major rock shelter site in far southwest Virginia that was abandoned between the Late Archaic and Middle Woodland periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Benthall
description: Researcher who noted the abandonment of Daugherty's Cave between the Late Archaic and Middle Woodland periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period following the Early Woodland, during which Daugherty's Cave was reoccupied after being abandoned.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Robert Ogle Collection
description: Archaeological collection compiled from surface sites along the Nottoway River Fall Zone in Sussex County, with carefully recorded locations.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Robert Ogle
description: Collector who carefully recorded the locations of projectile points he found along the Nottoway River Fall Zone in Sussex County.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Egghart
description: Researcher who, along with Manson, analyzed the Ogle Collection in 2016 to understand shifts in settlement preference through time.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Manson
description: Researcher who, along with Egghart, analyzed the Ogle Collection in 2016 to understand shifts in settlement preference through time.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Nottoway River Fall Zone
description: Geographic area in Sussex County, Virginia where Robert Ogle collected artifacts from surface sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Sussex County
description: County in Virginia where the Ogle Collection was compiled and where Blackwater Swamp margins show Early Woodland occupation.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Blackwater Swamp
description: Wetland area in Sussex County whose margins were favored for Early Woodland settlement.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: Chickahominy Swamp
description: Wetland area near Richmond where terraces show evidence of Early Woodland occupation, similar to other wetland settings.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Summary of Early Woodland Research to Date

name: 522 Bridge Site
description: Early Woodland archaeological site located along the North Fork of the Shenandoah River just upstream of its confluence with the South Fork, featuring extensive house outlines with central hearths and storage facilities.

name: 44WR0232
description: Early Woodland archaeological site situated on South Fork of the Shenandoah near its confluence with the North Fork, featuring buried occupation surfaces separated by sterile alluvium.

name: 44WR0446
description: Early Woodland archaeological site situated on South Fork of the Shenandoah near its confluence with the North Fork, associated with site 44WR0232.

name: Enfield Plantation
description: Early Woodland archaeological site located on the Mattaponi River, excavated and reported as part of VDOT-sponsored investigations.

name: Wheeler Site
description: Early Woodland archaeological site located in far Southwest Virginia, excavated and reported as part of VDOT-sponsored investigations.

name: Shenandoah Forks
description: Geographic location where sites 44WR329 and 44WR0232/44WR0446 are situated, in the Front Royal area.

name: Front Royal
description: Location where Early Woodland sites 44WR0232/44WR0446 are situated along the Shenandoah Forks.

name: Mattaponi River
description: River where the Enfield Plantation Early Woodland site is located.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region where the Wheeler Site 44LE33 is located.

name: North Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: River along which the 522 Bridge Site was located, just upstream of its confluence with the South Fork.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah
description: Larger river near which sites 44WR0232 and 44WR0446 were situated, near its confluence with the North Fork.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which the excavated sites were occupied, spanning approximately 450 radiocarbon years based on AMS dates.

name: Accokeek
description: Predominant ceramic type found at both the 522 Bridge Site and sites 44WR0232/44WR0446 during Early Woodland occupation.

name: Marcey Creek
description: Ceramic type occurring in limited numbers at the 522 Bridge Site and as a minority type at sites 44WR0232/44WR0446.

name: Ware Plain
description: Ceramic type occurring in limited numbers at the 522 Bridge Site.

name: Selden Island
description: Ceramic type occurring as a minority type at sites 44WR0232/44WR0446.

name: Piscataway
description: Main projectile point type associated with Early Woodland occupation at the 522 Bridge Site and sites 44WR0232/44WR0446.

name: Susquehanna
description: Projectile point type found at the 522 Bridge Site, with only one specimen documented.

name: Ferry Farm
description: Archaeological site along the Rappahannock River in Fredericksburg where intensive investigations were conducted by The George Washington Foundation to locate and study George Washington's boyhood home.

name: The George Washington Foundation
description: Organization that conducted intensive archaeological investigations at Ferry Farm.

name: Rappahannock River
description: River along which Ferry Farm archaeological site is located.

name: Fredericksburg
description: Location where Ferry Farm archaeological site is situated along the Rappahannock River.

name: George Washington
description: Historical figure whose boyhood home was the focus of archaeological investigations at Ferry Farm.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period represented by archaeological components and occupations found at multiple sites including Ferry Farm, Croaker Landing, Enfield Plantation, and Wheeler Site.

name: Accokeek
description: Ceramic type found at Ferry Farm that showed spatial correlation with various projectile point types, with 105 sherds found in an Early Woodland pit feature.

name: Piscataway
description: Projectile point type that exhibited the strongest spatial association with Accokeek ceramics at Ferry Farm, with 20 of 21 points found in units containing Accokeek.

name: Small Savannah River
description: Projectile point type found at Ferry Farm that showed less distinct spatial correlation with Accokeek ceramics.

name: Susquehanna-like points
description: Projectile point type that aligned spatially with Accokeek ceramics at Ferry Farm, with seven of eight recovered points from units containing Accokeek.

name: Croaker Landing
description: Small shell midden complex located along the south bank of the tidal York River that served as the type site for Croaker Landing ware and provided excellent subsistence data.

name: York River
description: Tidal river along whose south bank the Croaker Landing Site shell midden complex was located.

name: Croaker Landing ware
description: Ceramic type for which the Croaker Landing Site served as the type site.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where Croaker Landing Site helped refine material culture sequences and where Enfield Plantation represents the only fully investigated Early Woodland site.

name: Enfield Plantation Site
description: Archaeological site situated along the Mattaponi River in King William County, representing the only fully investigated Early Woodland site in the Virginia Coastal Plain.

name: Mattaponi River
description: River along which the Enfield Plantation Site is situated.

name: King William County
description: County where the Enfield Plantation Site is located along the Mattaponi River.

name: Wheeler Site
description: Archaeological site located on a high terrace overlooking Indian Creek, representing the only fully investigated Early Woodland occupation in Southwest Virginia.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region where the Wheeler Site represents the only fully investigated Early Woodland occupation.

name: Indian Creek
description: Creek that the Wheeler Site overlooks from a high terrace, described as a tributary of the Powell River.

name: Powell River
description: River of which Indian Creek is a tributary, near the Wheeler Site.

name: Swannanoa ceramics
description: Ceramic type found in several pits at the Wheeler Site.

name: Gypsy Stemmed
description: Projectile point type that points found at Wheeler Site were similar to.

name: Early Woodland Period
description: A chronological period in Virginia archaeology that transitioned gradually from the Archaic period, characterized by the adoption of pottery and changes in material culture.

name: Archaic Period
description: The archaeological period preceding the Woodland in Virginia, characterized by specific material culture traditions including carved steatite vessels.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: The final phase of the Archaic period that transitioned into the Early Woodland period.

name: Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period in Virginia characterized by specific material culture changes from the preceding Archaic period.

name: Savannah River tradition
description: An archaeological tradition from which multiple Early Woodland projectile points appear to be derived.

name: Small Savannah River
description: A projectile point type that spans the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition.

name: Fishtail
description: A projectile point type that spans the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition.

name: Calvert
description: A projectile point type that spans the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition.

name: Susquehanna
description: A distinctive projectile point type traditionally considered Late Archaic but with increasing evidence of use extending into the Early Woodland era.

name: Marcey Creek vessels
description: Ceramic vessels from the Early Woodland that retain flat bottom, rectilinear shape of earlier carved steatite vessels.

name: Piscataway
description: A type of projectile point found in an apparent cache at site 44WR0232/44WR0446.

name: Accokeek ceramics
description: Ceramic artifacts associated with Early Woodland contexts at site 44WR0232/44WR0446.

name: 522 Bridge Site
description: An archaeological site where Susquehanna points were recovered from tightly dated Early Woodland contexts.

name: 44WR0232/44WR0446
description: An archaeological site in Front Royal where an apparent point cache was uncovered in association with Accokeek ceramics in Early Woodland context.

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region being studied for Early Woodland archaeology and material culture sequences.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia where quartz use became prevalent during the Early Woodland period.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region in Virginia where quartz use became prevalent during the Early Woodland period.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region in Virginia where chert and other locally available stone found favor during the Early Woodland period.

name: Front Royal
description: Location of archaeological sites 522 Bridge Site and 44WR0232/44WR0446 where Early Woodland Susquehanna points were found.

name: New York
description: State where some of the Early Woodland radiocarbon returns for Susquehanna points were obtained.

name: Connecticut
description: State where some of the Early Woodland radiocarbon returns for Susquehanna points were obtained.

name: Piscataway point
description: A projectile point type that is the most common Early Woodland projectile point in Virginia, though also found in Late Archaic contexts. Characterized by small size, contracting stem, and often made of quartz.

name: 522 Bridge Site
description: Archaeological site where Piscataway points were recovered from tightly dated Early Woodland contexts.

name: 44WR0232/44WR0446
description: Archaeological sites along the Shenandoah Forks in Front Royal where Piscataway points were recovered from tightly dated Early Woodland contexts.

name: Shenandoah Forks
description: Geographic location in Front Royal where archaeological sites 44WR0232/44WR0446 are located.

name: Front Royal
description: Location where archaeological sites containing Piscataway points are situated along the Shenandoah Forks.

name: Pig Point Site
description: A stratified archaeological site in Anne Arundel County, Maryland, where Piscataway points occurred predominantly in Late Archaic contexts.

name: Anne Arundel County
description: County in Maryland where the Pig Point Site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where the Pig Point Site is located in Anne Arundel County.

name: Accokeek levels
description: Archaeological levels at the Pig Point Site where a few Piscataway points were found in the lower portions.

name: Magnolia Site
description: Archaeological site near the Dismal Swamp where Piscataway points were found, though not recovered from dated contexts.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Geographic area near which the Magnolia Site is located.

name: Teardrop points
description: A formally described projectile point type that is precisely made with a distinctive form, sometimes confused with Piscataway points due to morphological similarity.

name: Accokeek ceramics
description: Ceramic type associated with Teardrop points in Virginia and found in association with Piscataway points on Piedmont James River floodplain sites.

name: New Jersey
description: State where Teardrop points have been firmly dated to the Early Woodland period.

name: Delaware
description: State where Teardrop points have been firmly dated to the Early Woodland period.

name: Calvert
description: A projectile point type that, like Piscataway, was first described by Stephenson and Ferguson at the Accokeek Creek Site.

name: Accokeek Creek Site
description: Archaeological site where both Piscataway and Calvert point types were first described by Stephenson and Ferguson.

name: James River Survey
description: Archaeological survey project that provided findings used by McLearen to elaborate on the Piscataway type.

name: James River
description: River system where Piscataway points were found in association with Accokeek ceramics on Piedmont floodplain sites.

name: Early Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period during which Piscataway points are most commonly found in Virginia, and when Teardrop points are firmly dated in New Jersey and Delaware.

name: Late Archaic Period
description: Archaeological period during which Piscataway points predominantly occurred at the Pig Point Site and are considered by some researchers to originate.

name: Late Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period originally proposed by investigators for Piscataway point association, perhaps due to the point's small size and quartz manufacture.

name: Woodland era
description: Archaeological period during which Piscataway points continued to be produced and used, following their Late Archaic origins.

name: Small Savannah River Points
description: Projectile points that span the Late Archaic to Early Woodland transition period in Virginia archaeology.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period preceding the Early Woodland period.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period that follows the Late Archaic period in Virginia prehistory.

name: Pony Pasture Site
description: Archaeological site along the Chickahominy River east of Richmond where Small Savannah River points were found.

name: Chickahominy River
description: River east of Richmond where the Pony Pasture Site is located.

name: Richmond
description: City in Virginia, used as a geographic reference point for the Chickahominy River area.

name: Cattle Run Savannah River
description: A variant of Savannah River projectile point found fractured and fire-reddened within a hearth feature.

name: Clark's Branch Site
description: Archaeological site in Fairfax County where Small Savannah River points were found in association with Selden Island ceramics.

name: Fairfax County
description: County in Virginia where the Clark's Branch Site is located.

name: Selden Island ceramics
description: Early ceramic type found in spatial association with Small Savannah River points at the Clark's Branch Site.

name: Otarre type
description: Stemmed projectile point type classified as related to Savannah River points.

name: Calvert type
description: Local projectile point type made of quartz, similar in morphology to the Otarre type.

name: Potomac
description: River system where radiocarbon dates for Calvert and Bushnell Plain ceramics were obtained.

name: Bushnell Plain ceramics
description: Early ceramic type dated along the tidal Potomac by Waselkov.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region of Virginia where the Wheeler Site is located.

name: Wheeler Site
description: Archaeological site in Lee County that provided the only Early Woodland dates from Southwest Virginia.

name: Lee County
description: County in Southwest Virginia where the Wheeler Site is located.

name: Swannanoa pottery
description: Early Woodland ceramic type found in small pits at the Wheeler Site.

name: Gypsy Stemmed Point
description: Projectile point type found associated with Swannanoa pottery at the Wheeler Site.

name: Shenandoah Forks
description: Confluence area that appears to be a focal point of Early Woodland settlement.

name: Front Royal
description: Location where three intensively investigated Early Woodland sites are located.

name: 522 Bridge Site
description: Archaeological site in Front Royal where pit features associated with structure patterns yielded Early Woodland radiocarbon dates.

name: Commonwealth
description: Reference to Virginia as a political entity in the context of archaeological sites.

name: 44WR0232
description: Archaeological site where eleven AMS radiocarbon dates were obtained.

name: 44WR0446
description: Archaeological site where eleven AMS radiocarbon dates were obtained.

name: Accokeek
description: Dominant ceramic type in the Early Woodland assemblages at the North Fork and South Fork sites.

name: North Fork
description: Archaeological site where Accokeek ceramics dominated the assemblage.

name: South Fork
description: Archaeological site where Accokeek ceramics dominated the assemblage.

name: Marcey Creek
description: Antecedent ceramic type that co-occurs as a minority type with other ceramics during Early Woodland period.

name: Ware Plain
description: Antecedent ceramic type that co-occurs as a minority type with other ceramics during Early Woodland period.

name: Piscataway
description: Projectile point type that comprises the bulk of points at Early Woodland sites in the Shenandoah area.

name: Susquehanna
description: Projectile point type found in firm Early Woodland context at both North Fork and South Fork sites.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period during which peoples gravitated to resource-rich river floodplain and estuarine settings while intensively utilizing the full range of physiographic and environmental zones.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by more selective, floodplain-oriented settlement patterns and rare site occurrences in many Coastal Plain areas.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where Early Woodland components appear to be rare.

name: Lower James River
description: A river area noted for its lack of recognizable Early Woodland components, discussed by multiple researchers.

name: James River
description: A major river whose floodplains west of the Fall line were relatively intensively settled during the Early Woodland period.

name: Fall line
description: A geographic boundary, west of which the James River floodplains were relatively intensively settled.

name: Virginia Piedmont
description: A geographic region where a shift to floodplain settlement was noted during this period.

name: Potomac drainage
description: A drainage basin mentioned in the context of settlement patterns.

name: Early Woodland Period
description: Archaeological time period in Virginia associated with various radiocarbon dates ranging from approximately 3260 to 2663 BP, characterized by specific ceramic and lithic traditions.

name: Early Woodland Period
description: Cultural period characterized by ceramic production, decreased bifacial reduction, and strategic site locations near productive environments.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by relatively mobile foraging peoples in Virginia who exploited wild food resources and adopted ceramic technology.

name: Virginia

name: Shenandoah Forks confluence area

description: Pottery technology adopted during the Early Woodland period with both utilitarian and social functions.

name: stone bowls
description: Durable vessel technology that predates the Early Woodland period.

name: steatite vessels
description: Utilitarian vessels procured over very large distances during the Late Archaic, likely representing prestige items with social meaning.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by diminished long-distance trade networks, possible population stabilization or decline, and the introduction of ceramic technology with continued use of steatite.

name: steatite
description: A stone material used for carving bowls and later as temper in ceramic manufacture, valued for its thermal properties and cultural significance.

description: A geographic region that maintained trade relations with coastal groups during the Early Woodland period, providing stone materials like hornblende and gneiss.

name: gneiss
description: A Piedmont stone used as temper in ceramic manufacture during the Early Woodland period.

name: pendants
description: Ornamental objects made from slate during the Early Woodland period.

name: ceramic vessels
description: Durable vessel technology introduced during the Early Woodland period, often tempered with steatite and associated with female manufacture in Woodland societies.

name: steatite bowls
description: Carved stone vessels that preceded ceramic technology, sometimes recycled for use as temper in ceramic manufacture.

name: Woodland societies
description: Cultural groups during the Woodland period, characterized by specific gender roles in pottery manufacture and plant food procurement.

name: Sassaman
description: An archaeologist who in 1993 posited that steatite bowl manufacture was part of the male lithic procurement regime.

name: Native Americans
description: Indigenous peoples of North America who made extensive use of non-durable containers and possessed detailed knowledge of the natural world throughout prehistory.

name: North America
description: Continent containing the Eastern Woodlands region where pottery development is being analyzed.

name: steatite vessels
description: Durable containers made from steatite stone that were adopted before ceramic vessels by Native Americans.

name: Early Woodland Period
