source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.1. Middle Woodland Components byJurisdiction Recorded with VDHR.

name: Virginia
description: State where Middle Woodland archaeological components have been recorded and documented.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.1. Middle Woodland Components byJurisdiction Recorded with VDHR.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region referenced in archaeological chronologies for Middle Woodland periods.

name: Middle Atlantic Middle Woodland I
description: Archaeological period from 2500-1800 BP and 1800-1100 BP according to Gardner's 1982 chronology.

name: Gardner
description: Archaeologist who established Middle Atlantic chronology in 1982.

name: Southwest Virginia
description: Geographic region of Virginia with distinct Middle/Late Woodland period chronology.

name: Southwest Virginia Middle/Late Woodland
aliases: Middle and Late Woodland of far southwestern Virginia
description: Archaeological period from 1700-1000 BP associated with Upper Tennessee River drainage cultural developments.

name: Reid
description: Archaeologist who classified the Middle and Late Woodland periods for southwestern Virginia in 1997.

name: Southside Virginia
description: Geographic region of Virginia with Early/Middle Woodland period from 2200-1100 BP.

name: Southside Virginia Early/Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period from 2200-1100 BP in Southside Virginia region.

name: McLearen
description: Archaeologist who studied Southside Virginia Woodland periods and suggested reexamination of Middle Woodland chronology for southwestern Virginia.

name: Hodges
description: Archaeologist referenced in 1998 regarding Southside Virginia Woodland periods.

name: Delmarva
description: Peninsula region where Custer proposed a Woodland I designation covering 5000-1000 BP.

name: Delmarva Woodland I
description: Archaeological period from 5000-1000 BP proposed by Custer, combining Late Archaic, Early Woodland, and Middle Woodland periods.

name: Custer
description: Archaeologist who proposed the Woodland I designation for the Delmarva region in 1989.

name: Upper Tennessee River drainage
description: Geographic region associated with cultural developments during the Middle and Late Woodland periods in southwestern Virginia.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period generally applicable to much of Virginia, though with regional variations.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period that Reid combined with Middle Woodland for southwestern Virginia due to cultural continuity.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period that Custer included in his Woodland I designation for the Delmarva.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period that Custer included in his Woodland I designation for the Delmarva.

name: Mississippian
description: Archaeological period that influenced later developments, mentioned in context of preceding Middle/Late Woodland phenomena.

name: Blanton
description: Archaeologist who conducted Virginia Delmarva studies in 1994 with Margolin, documenting threats to archaeological resources.

name: Margolin
description: Archaeologist who conducted Virginia Delmarva studies in 1994 with Blanton, documenting threats to archaeological resources.

name: Lowery
description: Archaeologist who conducted Virginia Delmarva studies in 2001, documenting threats from ocean transgression and shoreline erosion.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: An archaeological period that transitioned to Middle Woodland II around 1800 BP, characterized by specific cultural patterns in Virginia.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: An archaeological period beginning around 1800 BP, marked by greater cultural segmentation of the landscape and localized ceramic styles.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia that experienced population growth and increased sedentism during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region in Virginia that witnessed hypothesized population decline during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region north of the James River that experienced population decline and/or regional abandonment during the Middle Woodland period.

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia that serves as a geographical boundary in discussions of Middle Woodland cultural patterns.

name: Potomac watershed
description: The drainage area of the Potomac River that witnessed hypothesized population decline during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Southside
description: A region in Virginia where McClearen questioned the Middle Woodland I and II distinction, suggesting continuity of low residential mobility.

name: North Carolina
description: Adjacent state to Virginia whose northern coastal region shows similar Middle Woodland patterns to Virginia's Southside.

name: Early Woodland
description: An archaeological period that preceded the Middle Woodland, showing continuity with Middle Woodland in the Southside region.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A major archaeological period in Virginia characterized by cultural diversity and varying lifeways across different regions.

name: Late Woodland
description: An archaeological period that followed the Middle Woodland, included in Klein's ceramic analysis study.

name: Virginia
description: The state that is the focus of this Middle Woodland archaeological research, potentially containing several distinct 'Middle Woodlands'.

name: Roanoke River Valley
description: One of three river valleys included in Klein's ceramic analysis study of Middle and Late Woodland sites.

name: Potomac River Valley
description: One of three river valleys included in Klein's ceramic analysis study of Middle and Late Woodland sites.

name: James River Valley
description: One of three river valleys included in Klein's ceramic analysis study of Middle and Late Woodland sites.

name: ceramic engineering model
description: A model that hypothesizes continuous adjustment in cooking technology as a response to subsistence changes, providing means for assigning absolute calendrical dates to ceramic assemblages.

name: Klein
description: A researcher who developed an approach providing archaeologists means for assigning absolute calendrical dates to ceramic assemblages and demonstrating subtle shifts within previously defined types.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region where silicate and limestone-tempered ceramics have been found, located between the Potomac and Roanoke Rivers.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region where silicate and limestone-tempered ceramics have been found, located between the Potomac and Roanoke Rivers.

name: Potomac River
description: A river that forms one of the boundaries of the region where ceramic studies were conducted in the Piedmont and Ridge and Valley.

name: Roanoke River
description: A river that forms one of the boundaries of the region where ceramic studies were conducted in the Piedmont and Ridge and Valley.

name: absolute seriation approach
description: An archaeological method developed to reconcile seriation methods with probable patterns of change in artifacts, providing basis for distilling discrete groups of ceramic attributes into temporal types.

name: Middle Woodland ceramic traditions
description: A ceramic tradition period for which new observations concerning development have resulted from applications of the ceramic engineering model technique.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region referenced in the archaeological context of ceramic studies.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region of Virginia where Middle Woodland diagnostic artifacts have been found and studied.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A chronological period in Virginia archaeology characterized by specific diagnostic artifacts.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A major watershed in the Coastal Plain region north of and including the James River.

name: James River
description: A major river serving as a watershed divide in the Coastal Plain, with distinct cultural areas north and south of it.

name: Albemarle Sound
description: A watershed south of the James River in the Coastal Plain region.

name: York River
description: A river in the interior Coastal Plain region where Prince George ceramics were found.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic province in Virginia with the most complex Middle Woodland culture history, divided into multiple culture areas.

name: Western Shore
description: A region where Popes Creek ceramic wares were found, with similar contemporaneous ceramics found on the Eastern Shore.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A region with sand and grit-tempered ceramics similar to and contemporaneous with Popes Creek wares.

name: Inner Coastal Plain Potomac Valley
description: A region where archaeological sites like Falcon's Landing and Accotink Meander were studied for ceramic analysis.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A broader regional area where Accokeek tradition ceramics have been identified in Middle Woodland contexts.

name: Maryland
description: A state with Coastal Plain and Piedmont regions containing well-documented Accokeek-like ceramic assemblages.

name: Virginia
description: A state with Coastal Plain and Piedmont regions containing well-documented Accokeek-like ceramic assemblages and complex Middle Woodland culture history.

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic region containing well-documented Accokeek-like ceramic assemblages.

name: Falcon's Landing Site
description: An archaeological site in the Inner Coastal Plain Potomac Valley where sand and grit-tempered sherds were analyzed using absolute seriation method.

name: Accotink Meander Site
description: An archaeological site in the Inner Coastal Plain Potomac Valley where sand and grit-tempered sherds were analyzed for temporal variation.

name: Abbott Zoned-Incised
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Mockley
description: A ceramic type that co-occurred with later variety Accokeek-like ceramics in Middle Woodland contexts.

name: Hercules
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Mount Pleasant
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Nomini
description: A Middle Woodland ceramic or projectile point type mentioned in both Middle Woodland I and II contexts.

name: Varina
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Chesterfield
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Yadkin
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Levanna
description: A Middle Woodland II ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Jacks Reef Corner Notched
description: A Middle Woodland II projectile point type with corner notched design.

name: Fox Creek/Selby Bay
description: A Middle Woodland I ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Prince George
description: A pebble-tempered ceramic ware found in the James and York River interior during Middle Woodland I.

name: Stony Creek
description: A net-impressed sand- and grit-tempered ceramic ware found south of the James River during Middle Woodland I.

name: Pope's Creek
description: A net-impressed sand- and grit-tempered ceramic ware found in the Chesapeake region during Middle Woodland I.

name: Accokeek
description: A cord-marked ceramic tradition with three temporal varieties spanning from 3100-1800 BP, identified as an inspectional type in Middle Woodland contexts.

name: Potts
description: A Middle Woodland I ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Rossville/Piscataway
description: A Middle Woodland I ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Vernon
description: A Middle Woodland I ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Calvert
description: A Middle Woodland I ceramic or projectile point type.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: An early phase of the Middle Woodland period with four defined culture areas in the Virginia Coastal Plain, characterized by net-impressed sand- and grit-tempered wares.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A later phase of the Middle Woodland period with five defined culture areas in the Virginia Coastal Plain.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period with ceramic types that show temporal overlap with Middle Woodland markers.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period with complex culture history in Virginia's Coastal Plain, showing temporal overlap with some Early Woodland ceramic types.

name: Blanton
description: A researcher (1992) who separated the Coastal Plain into sub-areas and defined culture areas for Middle Woodland periods.

name: Dent
description: A researcher (1995) who provided a list of radiocarbon dates associated with Middle Woodland ceramics.

name: Klein
description: A researcher who, with Stevens (1996), analyzed ceramic sherds using absolute seriation method and studied Accokeek tradition temporal variation.

name: Stevens
description: A researcher who, with Klein (1996), analyzed ceramic sherds and studied the temporal span of the Accokeek tradition.

name: Barse
description: A researcher (2002) who proposed a Middle Woodland I association for Accokeek ceramics based on work in the Potomac Valley.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A specific chronological period within the Middle Woodland when Mockleyware emerged as a circum-Chesapeake phenomenon.

name: Mockleyware
description: Shell-tempered ceramic ware that emerged during Middle Woodland II as a circum-Chesapeake phenomenon, distributed from Delaware south to Virginia.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: Geographic region around which Mockleyware emerged as a circum-Chesapeake phenomenon.

name: Hercules ceramics
description: Sand-and grit-tempered ceramics that blanketed the region south of the James River during Middle Woodland II.

name: Mount Pleasant ceramics
description: Sand-and grit-tempered ceramics similar to Hercules that blanketed the region south of the James River during Middle Woodland II.

name: James River
description: River that served as a geographic boundary, with sand-and grit-tempered ceramics found in the region south of it.

name: Nomini ware
description: One of the localized, non-shell tempered ceramic traditions developed during Middle Woodland II.

name: Varina ware
description: One of the localized, non-shell tempered ceramic traditions developed during Middle Woodland II.

name: Chesterfield ware
description: One of the localized, non-shell tempered ceramic traditions developed during Middle Woodland II.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where distinct ceramic traditions co-occurred during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Great Neck Site
description: Archaeological site where sand-tempered (Mt. Pleasant) and shell-tempered (Mockley) wares co-occurred in Middle Woodland contexts.

name: Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics
description: Ceramics recovered from six sites in the Virginia Coastal Plain indicating possible interaction with more northerly groups during late Middle Woodland II.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics were recovered from six sites.

name: Hatch Site
description: Archaeological site where Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics were excavated from dated contexts, with recent work proposing a Late Woodland association.

name: Late Woodland
description: Chronological period with which recent work proposes the Hatch Site's Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics are associated.

name: Maycock's Point Site
description: Archaeological site where Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics were excavated from dated contexts.

name: Bartlett Site
description: Archaeological site where Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics were excavated from dated contexts.

name: Trenton Complex
description: Archaeological complex at Abbot Farm, New Jersey, with which the Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics are most closely associated.

name: Abbot Farm
description: Archaeological site in New Jersey associated with the Trenton Complex.

name: New Jersey
description: State where Abbot Farm is located and where Mockley variants stretched into the Lower Delaware Valley.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Broad chronological period during which Mockley ceramic ware was documented by Middle Atlantic archaeologists.

name: Delaware
description: Northern extent of the documented distribution of Mockley ceramic ware from Delaware south to Virginia.

name: Virginia
description: Southern extent of the documented distribution of Mockley ceramic ware and location where Abbott Zoned-Incised ceramics were found.

name: Lower Delaware Valley
description: Geographic region in New Jersey where Mockley variants stretched.

name: Algonquian
description: Cultural group whose possible migration during Middle Woodland II is represented by the Mockley ceramic tradition.

name: William and Mary
description: College mentioned in the context of a senior research project studying Mockley vessels.

name: Lane
description: Researcher who conducted a petrographic study of Mockley vessels in 1999 as a senior research project.

name: Mockley
description: A type of ceramic ware dating to the Middle Woodland period, manufactured locally using kaolinite-rich clay.

name: Jenkins Neck Site
description: Multi-component archaeological site in Gloucester County with early Middle Woodland II seasonal occupation dating to BC 170+/-100.

name: Gloucester County
description: County in Virginia where the Jenkins Neck Site is located and clay samples were analyzed for comparison with Mockley ceramics.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: Cultural period representing the early phase of Middle Woodland, associated with seasonal occupation at Jenkins Neck Site.

name: Townsend
description: A type of ceramic ware studied alongside Mockley and Potomac Creek sherds from the Davis Site.

name: Potomac Creek
description: A type of ceramic ware studied alongside Mockley and Townsend sherds from the Davis Site.

name: Davis Site
description: Archaeological site in Lancaster County where Mockley, Townsend, and Potomac Creek sherds were analyzed.

name: Lancaster County
description: County in Virginia where the Davis Site is located.

name: Key
description: Co-author of x-ray diffraction and color analysis study of ceramic sherds from the Davis Site.

name: Gaskin
description: Co-author of x-ray diffraction and color analysis study of ceramic sherds from the Davis Site.

name: Sedgefield clay
description: Kaolinite-rich clay widely available in the outer coastal plain, used continuously from Middle Woodland through Late Woodland periods.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period during which potters began using Sedgefield clay and Mockley ceramics were produced.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period that continued the use of Sedgefield clay and represents the late prehistoric era.

name: Lower Peninsula
description: Geographic region where earlier radiocarbon dates extend the appearance of Mockley ware to 2100 BP.

name: 44NN7
description: Archaeological site where Mockley ware has been dated to 2100 BP, studied by Geier.

name: Geier
description: Researcher who documented early radiocarbon dates for Mockley ware at site 44NN7 in 1983.

name: 44HT36
description: Archaeological site where Mockley ware has been dated to 1900 BP, studied by Edwards et al.

name: 44HT37
description: Archaeological site where Mockley ware has been dated to 1900 BP, studied by Edwards et al.

name: Edwards
description: Lead researcher who studied sites 44HT36 and 44HT37, documenting 1900 BP dates for Mockley ware.

name: Taft Site
description: Archaeological site on Mason's Neck that yielded Mockley ceramics with radiocarbon date of 1940+/-40 BP.

name: Mason's Neck
description: Geographic location where the Taft Site is situated.

name: Norton
description: Co-author who studied the Taft Site and documented feature-related Mockley ceramics.

name: Baird
description: Co-author who studied the Taft Site and documented feature-related Mockley ceramics.

name: Albemarle
description: Type of ceramic ware found co-occurring with Mockley and Popes Creek sherds at Fletcher's Boathouse Site.

name: Popes Creek
description: Type of ceramic ware found co-occurring with Mockley and Albemarle sherds, within whose temporal range Mockley falls.

name: Fletcher's Boathouse Site
description: Archaeological site in the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park where Mockley, Albemarle, and Popes Creek sherds co-occur.

name: Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park
description: National park in Washington, D.C. where the Fletcher's Boathouse Site is located.

name: Washington, D.C.
description: Location of the Chesapeake and Ohio Canal National Historical Park and Fletcher's Boathouse Site.

name: Barse
description: Researcher who studied the Fletcher's Boathouse Site and documented co-occurrence of ceramic types in 2002.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where the earliest occurrence of Mockley ware has been documented through AMS dating.

name: McLearen
description: Co-researcher who studied the overlap between Prince George ware and Mockley in the Lower James River Valley.

name: Mouer
description: Co-researcher who studied the overlap between Prince George ware and Mockley in the Lower James River Valley.

name: Prince George ware
description: Ceramic ware considered a cognate of Popes Creek that co-occurs with Mockley in the Lower James River Valley.

name: Lower James River Valley
description: Geographic region where Prince George ware and Mockley co-occur at archaeological sites.

name: Aignor#3 Site
description: Archaeological site where Prince George ware and Mockley co-occur in features dated to BC 250+/-60 and BC 450+/-60.

name: Barse
description: Researcher who hypothesized in 2002 the emergence of Mockley from Popes Creek in the Potomac Valley.

name: Mockley
description: A ceramic ware type hypothesized to emerge from Popes Creek ceramics, characterized by specific rim form, vessel shapes, and sizes.

name: Popes Creek
description: An earlier ceramic ware type that represents the earlier end of a ceramic continuum leading to Mockley, derived from the Accokeek horizon.

name: Potomac Valley
description: Geographic region where Barse hypothesizes the emergence of Mockley ceramics from Popes Creek occurred.

name: Accokeek horizon
description: A chronological/cultural horizon from which Popes Creek ceramics are derived.

name: Johnson
description: Researcher who described in 1991 an intermediate ceramic form from the Neha Site that could represent a phase of either Popes Creek or Mockley.

name: Neha Site
description: Archaeological site where an intermediate ceramic form was recovered that may represent a phase of either Popes Creek or Mockley ceramics.

name: Culpeper Ware
description: Sandstone-tempered, cord- and net-impressed ceramic ware with Mockley-like paste and hardness, dubbed by Moore in 1992 and radiocarbon-dated to BC 510+/-90.

name: Moore
description: Researcher who dubbed the intermediate ceramic form 'Culpeper Ware' in 1992.

name: Potter
description: Researcher who proposed in 2002 a southern origin for Mockley ceramics in the Virginia Beach/Currituck Sound area.

name: Green
description: Researcher from 1987 whose work Potter built upon in proposing a southern origin for Mockley ceramics.

name: Virginia Beach/Currituck Sound area
description: Geographic region where Potter proposes Mockley ceramics originated, characterized by Early Woodland ceramic traditions with cord and net impression, shell tempering and beaker-shaped vessels.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period characterized by ceramic traditions with attributes of cord and net impression, shell tempering and beaker-shaped vessels.

name: Painter
description: Researcher who in 1977 documented Early Woodland ceramic traditions in the Virginia Beach/Currituck Sound area.

name: Hodges
description: Researcher who in 1998 reviewed evidence for the 'southern origins' hypothesis for Mockley ceramics through analysis of Middle Woodland beaker vessels from the Great Neck Site.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period characterized by flat-bottomed beaker vessels and specific projectile point types including Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed.

name: Great Neck Site
description: Archaeological site where Middle Woodland flat-bottomed beaker vessels falling within the Mockley series were recovered from a pit feature radiocarbon-dated to BC 260+/-60.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: Subregion of southeastern Virginia where Hodges identifies a subregional Mockley tradition of beaker vessels.

name: Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed
description: Well-documented Middle Woodland II diagnostic projectile point type that often occurs with Mockley ceramics, documented for the 1800-1100 BP period in the Maryland Coastal Plain.

name: Dent
description: Researcher who documented the Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed projectile point type.

name: Gibb
description: Researcher who with Hines documented the Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed projectile point type in 1997.

name: Hines
description: Researcher who with Gibb documented the Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed projectile point type in 1997.

name: Virginia Chesapeake Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where there are no absolute dates for the Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed projectile point type.

name: Maryland Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where the Selby Bay/Fox Creek Stemmed projectile point type is well-documented for the 1800-1100 BP period.

name: Stewart
description: Researcher who in 1989 noted the implications of rhyolite and argillite procurement for Middle Woodland II intra-regional exchange systems.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region from which rhyolite and argillite resources had to be procured from outside areas for projectile point manufacture.

name: Nomini
description: A late Middle Woodland Coastal Plain projectile point type documented by Potter in 1993.

name: Jacks Reef Corner Notched
description: A late Middle Woodland Coastal Plain projectile point type with corner-notched variety.

name: Pentagonal
description: A variety of late Middle Woodland Coastal Plain projectile point with pentagonal form.

name: Levanna
description: A large-medium triangular projectile point form from the late Middle Woodland Coastal Plain period.

name: Yadkin
description: A large-medium triangular projectile point form from the late Middle Woodland Coastal Plain period.

name: Virginia Piedmont
description: A geographic region of Virginia characterized by Middle Woodland ceramic sequences and cultural areas divided into watersheds.

name: Middle Woodland
description: An archaeological period in Virginia characterized by specific ceramic traditions and projectile point types, subdivided into phases I and II.

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia that forms a watershed boundary for Middle Woodland cultural areas.

name: Chesapeake
description: A watershed region in the Virginia Piedmont associated with crushed rock-tempered Albemarle ware during Middle Woodland I.

name: Albemarle Sound
description: A watershed region associated with grit- and sand-tempered Vincent and Clements-like wares during Middle Woodland periods.

name: Potomac
description: A watershed region in the Virginia Piedmont associated with finely crushed quartz- and mica-tempered Hell Island-like wares during Middle Woodland II.

name: Rappahannock
description: A watershed region in the Virginia Piedmont that formed one of three culture areas during Middle Woodland II.

name: Albemarle ware
description: Crushed rock-tempered ceramics adopted by Chesapeake Piedmont groups during Middle Woodland I.

name: Vincent
description: Grit- and sand-tempered ceramic wares manufactured by Albemarle Sound groups during Middle Woodland periods.

name: Clements
description: Grit- and sand-tempered ceramic wares manufactured by Albemarle Sound groups during Middle Woodland periods.

name: Hell Island
description: Finely crushed quartz- and mica-tempered ceramic wares found in the Potomac Piedmont during Middle Woodland II.

name: Shockoe Fabric Impressed
description: Fabric impressed ceramics similar to Hell Island found in association with Jacks Reef points in the James River Piedmont/Fall Line area.

name: Jacks Reef
description: Projectile points found in association with Shockoe Fabric Impressed ceramics in the James River Piedmont/Fall Line area.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic area where Shockoe Fabric Impressed ceramics and Jacks Reef points have been found together.

name: Late Woodland I
description: An archaeological period commonly associated with sites in the southern Ridge and Valley along with Middle Woodland II.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region where Grayson series ceramics are commonly associated with Middle Woodland II/Late Woodland I sites.

name: Grayson
description: Ceramic series commonly associated with Middle Woodland II/Late Woodland I sites in the southern Ridge and Valley, also found at the Clark Site in the southwestern Piedmont.

name: Clark Site
description: An archaeological site in the far southwestern Piedmont where Grayson series ceramics have been found in feature contexts.

name: North Carolina
description: A state that provides a perspective on the southern Virginia Piedmont Middle Woodland ceramic sequence.

name: Yadkin
description: Cord impressed and fabric impressed ceramics that characterize the southern Virginia Piedmont Middle Woodland ceramic sequence from a North Carolina perspective. Also refers to medium to large triangular projectile points used as general Middle Woodland markers in the Albemarle Sound watershed.

name: Uwharrie
description: Ceramic wares that appear in the southern Virginia Piedmont Middle Woodland ceramic sequence.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region whose Middle Woodland projectile point sequence overlaps with that of the Piedmont.

name: Piscataway
description: A projectile point type that continued into Middle Woodland I or later in the Piedmont region, found at the Neha Site.

name: Rossville
description: A projectile point type grouped with Piscataway that continued into Middle Woodland I or later in the Piedmont region.

name: Early Woodland
description: An archaeological period characterized by side-notched projectile point forms like Vernon that may have continued into Middle Woodland I.

name: Vernon
description: Side-notched projectile points from the Early Woodland that may have continued into Middle Woodland I in the Potomac and Rappahannock Piedmont, found at the Neha Site.

name: Teardrop
description: Projectile points associated with Middle Woodland components in the Piedmont, though sometimes described as a bifacial tool unrestricted to any cultural or temporal period, found at the Neha Site.

name: Neha Site
description: An archaeological site where Piscataway, Vernon, and Teardrop points along with Neha ceramics are dated to BC 510+/-90.

name: Neha ceramics
description: Ceramic artifacts found at the Neha Site in association with various projectile points and dated to BC 510+/-90.

name: Potts Side-Notched
description: Projectile points well-documented in the Chesapeake Piedmont for Middle Woodland I, often occurring with Popes Creek and net-marked cognates.

name: Popes Creek
description: Ceramic artifacts that often occur with Potts Side-Notched points in the Chesapeake Piedmont during Middle Woodland I.

name: Selby Bay points
description: Middle Woodland II projectile points documented as intrusive forms in the Virginia Potomac Piedmont.

name: Fox Creek points
description: Middle Woodland II projectile points documented as intrusive forms in the Virginia Potomac Piedmont.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: Archaeological period during which Selby Bay/Fox Creek points were manufactured and used.

name: Virginia Potomac Piedmont
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Selby Bay/Fox Creek points have been documented as intrusive forms.

name: 44LD250
description: Archaeological site where Selby Bay/Fox Creek points have been excavated from contexts dated to BC 580+/- 140.

name: 44LD283
description: Archaeological site where Selby Bay/Fox Creek points have been excavated from contexts dated to BC 370+/-415.

name: James River Fall Line
description: Geographic location where Jacks Reef points have been found.

name: Jacks Reef point
description: Projectile point type with pentagonal and corner-notched varieties, known as a late Middle Woodland form throughout the Potomac and Rappahannock Piedmont.

name: Rappahannock Piedmont
description: Geographic region where Jacks Reef points are known as a late Middle Woodland form.

name: late Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which Jacks Reef points were used in the Potomac and Rappahannock Piedmont.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A chronological subdivision of the Middle Woodland period, characterized by specific ceramic and projectile point types in Virginia's Piedmont and Blue Ridge regions.

name: Middle Woodland I

name: Albemarle
description: A ceramic type or series that ranges in date from 2100 BP to 630 BP, often identified as Late Woodland ware but with some examples falling within Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Hell Island
description: A ceramic type associated with Middle Woodland II period in the Piedmont and Blue Ridge regions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Shockoe
description: A ceramic type associated with Middle Woodland II period in the Piedmont and Blue Ridge regions.

description: A ceramic type associated with Middle Woodland I period, often grouped with Rossville type.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

description: A projectile point type associated with Middle Woodland I period in the Piedmont and Blue Ridge regions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Vernon
description: A projectile point type associated with Middle Woodland I period in the Piedmont and Blue Ridge regions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

description: An archaeological site that yielded a radiocarbon date of BC 650+/-140 for Albemarle ceramics and Madison projectile points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

description: A ceramic type found at Fletcher's Boathouse Site alongside Albemarle and Popes Creek ceramics, suggesting cultural interaction.

description: An archaeological site that yielded a radiocarbon date of BC 540+/-60 for Albemarle cord and fabric-impressed ceramics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Spessard Site
description: An archaeological site that yielded a radiocarbon date of BC 570+/-50 for Albemarle cord-impressed ceramics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Wood Site
description: An archaeological site where Gallivan applied Klein's absolute seriation method to ceramic assemblages containing Albemarle types.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Cabin Run

source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Piedmont
description: A physiographic region in Virginia characterized by specific Middle Woodland cultural sequences and artifact types.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain region containing rhyolite quarries that were accessed by Selby Bay groups during Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Potomac Fall Line
description: A geographic boundary that functioned as both a cultural boundary and point of contact between Coastal Plain and Piedmont groups during the Middle Woodland.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Coastal Plain
description: A physiographic region whose groups interacted with Piedmont groups at sites like Fletcher's Boathouse during Middle Woodland I.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Great Valley
description: A geographic region where Selby Bay projectile points and ceramics are found, evidencing travel routes to rhyolite quarries.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Monocacy Valley
description: A geographic region where Selby Bay projectile points and ceramics are found, evidencing travel routes to rhyolite quarries.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: James River
description: A major river in Virginia, used as a geographic reference point for cultural boundaries in the Piedmont region.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Rappahannock
description: A river and associated Piedmont region in Virginia referenced in discussions of Middle Woodland cultural sequences.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Warren County
description: A county in Virginia where the Cabin Run site yielded late-dating Albemarle ceramics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Washington, D.C.
description: Location of the Fletcher's Boathouse Site, which yielded early-dating Albemarle ceramics at 2100 BP.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Maryland
description: State containing Blue Ridge rhyolite quarries accessed by Selby Bay groups during Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Late Woodland
description: A chronological period following the Middle Woodland, with transition occurring around 1100 BP according to radiocarbon dates.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Early Woodland
description: A chronological period preceding the Middle Woodland, showing cultural continuity with Middle Woodland in the Chesapeake Piedmont.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.3. Middle Woodland DiagnosticArtifacts/Piedmont and Blue Ridge

name: Virginia Radiocarbon Database

name: Albemarle ceramics

source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A chronological period during the Middle Woodland era, poorly documented in some regions but associated with specific ceramic traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Wood
description: An archaeological site that yielded crushed feldspar-tempered, cord-impressed sherds considered diagnostic of Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Ramp 3 Site
description: An archaeological site in southwest Washington, D.C. where Albemarle chert-tempered, cord-impressed sherds provided radiocarbon dates.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Whitehurst West Site

source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Blue Ridge
description: A mountain region where Middle Woodland period is poorly known, extending from narrow northern sections to wider southern areas reaching 5730' elevation at Mount Rogers.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

description: A river marking a geographical boundary in the Blue Ridge region, south of which the Blue Ridge massif widens significantly.

name: Mount Rogers
description: The highest peak in the Blue Ridge region at 5730 feet above sea level.

name: Inner Piedmont

name: Ridge and Valley

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A valley region where groups used lithic raw materials and demonstrated movement through the Blue Ridge during Middle Woodland periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 8.2. Virginia Radiocarbon Database AlbemarleCeramics Radiocarbon Years/One Sigma

name: Jacks Reef

name: Appalachian Plateau

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region in Virginia encompassing the Shenandoah/Potomac and James River drainages.

description: A valley region where fabric impression stylistic phenomena moved from the southwest into the Piedmont.

description: A river drainage in the Ridge and Valley region, part of the Shenandoah/Potomac drainage system.

description: A river drainage system in the Ridge and Valley region parallel to the Chesapeake Piedmont.

name: Chesapeake Piedmont
description: A geographic region whose Middle Woodland sequences parallel those of the Ridge and Valley.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region eastward from the northern Shenandoah Valley where fabric impression phenomena extended.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Pendleton County
description: A county in West Virginia where the Mouth of Seneca Site is located at the western margin of the Ridge and Valley province.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: West Virginia
description: A state containing Pendleton County and the Mouth of Seneca Site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Maryland Great Valley
description: A geographic region where Buck Garden ceramics have been documented for the upper Potomac.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Peer Site
description: An archaeological site where a frequency seriation of sherds was used to hypothesize a Middle Woodland crushed rock temper tradition.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Cement Plant Site
description: An archaeological site where chert-tempered sherds have been recovered from Middle Woodland II radiocarbon-dated contexts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Mouth of Seneca Site
description: An archaeological site in Pendleton County, West Virginia, showing Middle Woodland II association for crushed rock temper ceramics with radiocarbon dates from BC 70-690.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Fout Site
description: An archaeological site in far northwestern Virginia where sherds identified as Albemarle have been recovered.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Middle Woodland Period
description: A chronological period divided into phases I and II, characterized by specific ceramic and projectile point sequences in the Ridge and Valley region.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Early Woodland Period
description: A chronological period preceding the Middle Woodland, characterized by sand tempered ceramics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Late Woodland Period
description: A chronological period following the Middle Woodland, into which fabric impression phenomena temporally extended.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Accokeek
description: A ceramic type characterized by cord-impressed sand-tempered sherds that decreased in frequency during the transition to Middle Woodland I.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Clemson's Island
description: A ceramic type similar in description to the South Branch series from the Mouth of Seneca Site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: Buck Garden
description: A ceramic type documented for the upper Potomac in the Maryland Great Valley, similar to the South Branch series.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: South Branch
description: A new ceramic series identified at the Mouth of Seneca Site, characterized by crushed rock-tempered cord impressed ceramics similar to Clemson's Island and Buck Garden.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part0

name: 44SH133
description: Archaeological site where shale-tempered cord-impressed Albemarle variety pottery was excavated by Gardner and Nash.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Albemarle
description: A shale-tempered cord-impressed ceramic type found with steatite- and sand-tempered sherds, associated with late Early Woodland occupation.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Seldon Island
description: A ceramic type generally dated to 2850 to 2700 BP that Gardner believed to be cognate to the Albemarle variety.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Warwick Mansion Site
description: Archaeological site in the upper James River Valley where similar ware to Albemarle has been excavated.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: James River Valley
description: Geographic region where the Warwick Mansion Site is located.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Middle Woodland I
description: Cultural period during which the stone and earthen burial mound tradition developed in the Shenandoah Valley.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region where the stone and earthen burial mound tradition developed during Middle Woodland I.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Potomac River
description: River drainage area contiguous to the Shenandoah Valley where burial mound tradition developed.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part1

name: Hopewell
description: A cultural group associated with distinctive artifact assemblages including cut mica, polyhedral cores, and prismatic blades found at Middle Woodland sites.

name: Ohio
description: A state where the Seip Site in Ross County is located, part of ceramic exchange networks during the Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

name: Wright Check Stamped
description: A Middle Woodland ceramic type recovered from southwest Virginia archaeological sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

name: Mulberry Creek Plain
description: A Middle Woodland ceramic type recovered from southwest Virginia archaeological sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

description: A Middle Woodland ceramic type recovered from southwest Virginia archaeological sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural and chronological period characterized by specific diagnostic artifacts including ceramic types and projectile points in Virginia archaeology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

name: 1800-1200 BP
description: The chronological range for the sand-tempered Connestee series ceramics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Table 8.4. Middle Woodland Diagnostic Artifacts/Northern and Southern Ridge and Valley_part2

name: Eastern North America
description: A broad geographic region characterized by macro-scale paleoenvironmental reconstructions showing climate and vegetational cover stabilization by ca. 3000 BP.

name: Sub Atlantic episode
description: A paleoenvironmental period beginning ca. 3000 BP when climate and vegetational cover became effectively stabilized to modern conditions.

name: Middle Woodland
description: An archaeological period during which two major zones of the Eastern deciduous forest divided Virginia, and which experienced climatic and environmental shifts.

name: Eastern deciduous forest
description: A forest zone that was divided into two major regions during the Middle Woodland period in Virginia.

name: Oak Pine Forest
description: A forest zone of the Outer Piedmont and Coastal Plain during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Outer Piedmont
description: A geographic region that was part of the Oak Pine Forest zone during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region that was part of the Oak Pine Forest zone and inhabited by hunter-gatherers who focused on estuarine-based resources.

name: Appalachian Oak Forest
description: A forest zone of the Inner Piedmont, Blue Ridge and Ridge and Valley during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Inner Piedmont
description: A geographic region that was part of the Appalachian Oak Forest zone during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Blue Ridge
description: A geographic region that was part of the Appalachian Oak Forest zone during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: A geographic region that was part of the Appalachian Oak Forest zone during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: A geographic region whose extreme westernmost outliers fell within the Mixed Mesophytic Forest region.

name: Mixed Mesophytic Forest
description: A forest region that included extreme westernmost outliers of the Appalachian Plateau.

name: Early Woodland
description: An archaeological period for which changing hunter-gatherer lifeways are hypothesized.

name: Vandal Minimum
description: A period from 1600-1200 B.P. characterized by a mild decline in average global temperature.

name: Medieval Warm episode
description: A climatic period beginning around 1200 BP, preceded by three centuries of cooler than normal conditions.

name: Chesapeake Bay
description: A bay whose sediment cores have been analyzed palynologically to demonstrate late Holocene climatic shifts.

name: late Holocene
description: A geological period from 3,000 to 250 BP characterized by climatic shifts and generally wetter and cooler climate than the middle Holocene.

name: middle Holocene
description: A geological period that was generally drier and warmer than the late Holocene.

name: North Atlantic
description: A region where ice rafting events occurred that may correspond to hemispheric or global climate events during the late Holocene.

name: Virginia
description: The state divided by two major zones of the Eastern deciduous forest during the Middle Woodland period, with various physiographic zones studied for paleoenvironmental reconstruction.

name: Indian Creek Site
description: An archaeological site in Prince Georges County, Maryland that provides one of the more complete pollen records for the late Pleistocene and Holocene Inner Coastal Plain.

name: Prince Georges County
description: A county in Maryland where the Indian Creek Site is located.

name: Maryland
description: A state where the Indian Creek Site and Prince Georges County are located.

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region for which the Indian Creek Site provides important pollen records during the late Pleistocene and Holocene periods.

name: late Pleistocene
description: A geological time period for which the Indian Creek Site provides pollen records.

name: Holocene
description: A geological time period for which the Indian Creek Site provides pollen records.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: A cultural period characterized by drier conditions and major reduction in arboreal pollen with expansion of herbaceous species.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A cultural period that followed Middle Woodland I, during which there was a return to cooler, moister conditions and settlement pattern changes.

name: wild rice
description: Protein-rich plant that thrives in freshwater and low salinity brackish water environments of the Inner Coastal Plain.

name: arrow arum
description: A plant species that thrives in the wetland environments of the Inner Coastal Plain.

name: smartweed
description: A plant species found in the wetland environments of the Inner Coastal Plain.

name: Sub-Atlantic episode
description: A climatic period during which significant ecological changes occurred at the transition from Middle Woodland I to Middle Woodland II.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where settlement pattern changes occurred during the Middle Woodland period, with groups fusing in rich estuarine locations.

name: Tidewater
description: Geographic region in Virginia characterized by cool and moist conditions during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period characterized by cool and moist conditions in the Tidewater region and associated with various cultural developments.

name: Delmarva Peninsula
description: Peninsula region that experienced drought conditions during the Sub-Atlantic period, showing regional climatic variability.

name: Sub-Atlantic
description: Climatic period characterized by regional variability, including drought conditions in the Delmarva Peninsula.

name: Gum Branch Site
description: Archaeological site in the Nanticoke River drainage with stratigraphy indicating periods of drought from 3000 BP to 1000 BP.

name: Nanticoke River
description: River drainage system where the Gum Branch Site is located.

name: Woodland I
description: Archaeological period from which artifacts were recovered from aeolian deposits at the Gum Branch Site.

name: Woodland II
description: Archaeological period from which artifacts were recovered from aeolian deposits at the Gum Branch Site.

name: Delaware
description: State where eastern marshes provided palynological evidence of extremely dry conditions from 1900 BP to 1450 BP.

name: Carey Complex
description: Cultural complex that experienced decline of mortuary ceremonialism and elaborate exchange networks due to extreme drought conditions.

name: Delmarva Adena Complex
description: Cultural complex that preceded the Carey Complex and featured mortuary ceremonialism and elaborate exchange networks.

name: Virginia Blue Ridge
description: Mountain region in Virginia that experienced catastrophic environmental change during Middle Woodland I.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: Archaeological subperiod during which catastrophic environmental change occurred in the Virginia Blue Ridge.

name: Madison County
description: County in Virginia where a 1995 rapid mass movement event was studied.

name: Rapidan River
description: River system in Virginia where the Kinsey Run debris flow occurred.

name: Kinsey Run
description: Location of a debris flow that exposed geomorphological records of past rapid mass movement events, with documented archaeological sites from Middle Archaic through Early Woodland periods.

name: Nelson County
description: County in Virginia with studies indicating similar patterns of rapid mass movement events.

name: Upper Tye River
description: River basin in Nelson County showing similar patterns of rapid mass movement events.

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which the Kinsey Run area saw repeated occupation before abandonment around 2200 BP.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which the Kinsey Run area saw repeated occupation before abandonment around 2200 BP.

name: James River
description: River system in Virginia studied for alluvial settings and floodplain accumulation patterns affecting archaeological site formation.

name: Potomac River
description: River system in Virginia studied for alluvial settings and floodplain accumulation patterns affecting archaeological site formation.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic boundary upstream of which James and Potomac River alluvial settings were analyzed for floodplain accumulation.

name: James River Basin
description: River basin where substantial numbers of archaeological features have been excavated, with earliest stage correlated to declining flood frequency around BC 1000.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which floodplain burial of occupations occurred less often due to decline in high flood interval.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where particular site discovery techniques are favored for archaeological investigation.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period characterized by gradually increasing population, sedentism, and inter-area integration with more clearly identifiable territories.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period that preceded the Middle Woodland, characterized by a settlement pattern that was gradually refined to accommodate more people.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where the stone burial mound tradition reflected greater social complexity during Middle Woodland I.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted by Hodges and Potter.

name: Upper Chickahominy
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted by Blanton and others.

name: Mattaponi
description: River region in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted.

name: Chickahominy
description: River in Virginia, part of the drained uplands region between the Chickahominy and James Rivers where Middle Woodland studies were conducted.

name: James River
description: Major river in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted in both the Piedmont region and drained uplands.

name: Potomac
description: River in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted in both the Piedmont region and Fall Line area.

name: Rappahannock
description: River in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement studies have been conducted in the Piedmont region.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Broader geographic region for which generalized archaeological models have been developed and are still referenced.

name: Virginia
description: State where Middle Woodland settlement studies and archaeological investigations have been conducted throughout various regions.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period in eastern North America, with contexts discussed regarding timing and distribution of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: Eastern Agricultural Complex
description: A complex of domesticated varieties of starchy and oily seed plants including sunflower, sumpweed, goosfoot, Jerusalem artichoke, knotweed, and squash, documented in eastern North America as early as the late third millennium BC.

name: sunflower
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: sumpweed
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: goosfoot
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: Jerusalem artichoke
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: knotweed
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: squash
description: A domesticated starchy and oily seed plant that was part of the Eastern Agricultural Complex.

name: eastern North America
description: A geographic region where the Eastern Agricultural Complex has been well documented as early as the late third millennium BC.

name: Midwest
description: A region where more complex forms of social organization developed by 2250 B.P. based on indigenous cultigen production.

name: Midsouth
description: A region where more complex forms of social organization developed by 2250 B.P. based on indigenous cultigen production.

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by maize-centered agriculture that followed the Middle Woodland period.

name: Virginia
description: A state where few Middle Woodland sites have yielded direct evidence of Eastern Agricultural Complex domesticates.

name: Great Neck Site
description: An archaeological site analyzed by Hodges regarding the absence of cultivated foods from Middle Woodland sites.

name: maize
description: A crop first introduced into eastern North America before 2000 BP but did not contribute significantly to the Eastern Woodlands diet until after 1200-1000 BP.

name: Eastern Woodlands
description: A cultural region where maize did not contribute significantly to the diet until after 1200-1000 BP.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia where Middle and Late Woodland settlement patterns have been studied through archaeological excavations.

name: Great Neck Peninsula
description: A peninsula in the southern Outer Coastal Plain where archaeological excavations have revealed Middle and Late Woodland settlements.

name: Great Neck Site
description: An archaeological site located on the Great Neck Peninsula that contains overlapping Middle and Late Woodland settlements extending 640 meters along the south shore of Broad Bay.

name: Broad Bay
description: A bay along whose south shore the Great Neck Site extends for at least 640 meters.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by macro-band base camps and shell-tempered ceramic (Mockley) producing groups in the Outer Coastal Plain.

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period with settlements that overlapped with Middle Woodland occupations in the southern Outer Coastal Plain.

name: Mockley
description: A type of shell-tempered ceramic produced by groups that cycled within a limited Outer Coastal Plain/Inner Coastal Plain territory during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region that, along with the Outer Coastal Plain, formed the territory where Mockley ceramic producing groups cycled during the Middle Woodland period.

name: VDHR
description: The Virginia Department of Historic Resources, which conducted recent archaeological work at the Great Neck Site.

name: Elizabeth Moore
description: A researcher at the Virginia Museum of Natural History directing an ambitious program of waterscreening and flotation to recover information on diet and seasonality of occupation at the Great Neck Site.

name: Virginia Museum of Natural History
description: The institution where Dr. Elizabeth Moore is conducting analysis of archaeological materials from the Great Neck Site.

name: Patuxent Point Site
description: A Mockley Phase archaeological site on the Maryland western shore that contains an oval house pattern and associated covered pit, analogous to features at the Great Neck Site.

name: Maryland
description: The state where the Patuxent Point Site is located on its western shore.

name: Mockley Phase
description: A cultural phase associated with the Middle Woodland period, characterized by specific ceramic and settlement patterns.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A broader geographic region that includes both Virginia and Maryland, where Middle Woodland feature complexes have been documented.

name: Great Neck
description: Archaeological site in the Outer Coastal Plain where botanical and faunal remains suggest a broad-based subsistence economy, with evidence of intensive Middle Woodland use followed by intermittent occupation between 1600-700 BP.

name: Virginia Radiocarbon Database
description: Database containing radiocarbon dating information for Virginia archaeological sites, referenced for dating charcoal samples from Great Neck.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where archaeological investigations have searched for evidence of Middle Woodland maize cultivation.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period during which maize agriculture may have been practiced in the Coastal Plain, with dates ranging from approximately AD 200-1000.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period that followed the Middle Woodland, well documented for sedentary society development and associated with possible contamination of earlier archaeological contexts.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period preceding the Middle Woodland, during which sedentism did not develop on the Great Neck Peninsula according to archaeological evidence.

name: Hampton School Site
description: Archaeological site associated with the earliest occurrence of maize in Virginia, where a possible maize kernel was recovered with Mockley ceramics from a feature dated to AD 239-561.

name: Mockley ceramics
description: Type of pottery recovered at the Hampton School Site along with possible early maize remains.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: Location where Zea sp. pollen dated to 2250-2050 BP was recovered from a peat profile, representing early evidence of maize in the region.

name: Smithsonian Pier Site
description: Archaeological site in Ann Arundel County, Maryland, where maize pollen was recovered in subsoil beneath a Selby Bay Phase shell midden, raising questions about Middle Woodland maize associations.

name: Ann Arundel County
description: County in Maryland where the Smithsonian Pier Site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where the Smithsonian Pier Site is located in Ann Arundel County.

name: Selby Bay Phase
description: Cultural phase associated with shell midden deposits at the Smithsonian Pier Site.

name: maize cupules
description: Plant remains identified in flotation samples from Middle Woodland features at Great Neck, directly dated to AD 1032-1210.

name: Zea sp.
description: Maize pollen recovered from Dismal Swamp peat profile dated to 2250-2050 BP, representing potential early evidence of maize cultivation.

name: Great Dismal Swamp
description: A swamp region where small Middle Woodland I procurement camps were located on the margins but were abandoned after 1800 B.P.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: A cultural period during which small procurement camps existed on the margins of the Great Dismal Swamp before being abandoned after 1800 B.P.

name: Chesapeake
description: A region where Algonquian speakers may have migrated during the late Middle Woodland period, possibly causing regional cultural disruption.

name: Algonquian
description: A cultural-linguistic group that may have migrated into the Chesapeake during the late Middle Woodland period and historically documented in the Chicacoan area.

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period during which Algonquian speakers may have migrated into the Chesapeake region.

name: northern Virginia Outer Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where Potter conducted detailed synthesis of Middle Woodland II settlement data.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A cultural period (1800-1450 BP) characterized by various settlement types including large midden sites and small interior camps, with estuarine-based settlements.

name: Coan River
description: A river in the Northern Neck region where excavations and surveys provided detailed Middle Woodland II settlement data.

name: Northern Neck
description: A geographic region where the Coan River is located and where the Chicacoan Locality archaeological investigations took place.

name: Chicacoan Locality
description: An archaeological locality along the Coan River, named after the historically documented Algonquian chiefdom of the area.

name: Chicacoan
