description: Cultural period during which various archaeological components and settlements existed in the Ridge and Valley region.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where Middle Woodland settlement systems have been studied.

name: Elk Garden Site
description: Archaeological site located on the floodplain of Elk Garden Creek with Middle Woodland occupation dating from BC 40-BC 830.

name: Elk Garden Creek
description: A tributary to the Clinch River where the Elk Garden Site is located.

name: Clinch River
description: River to which Elk Garden Creek is a tributary.

name: 44LE121
description: Archaeological site in the Indian Creek drainage of Lee County with evidence of Middle Woodland horticulture dating from 260 BC-170 BC.

name: Indian Creek
description: Creek drainage in Lee County where site 44LE121 is located.

name: Lee County
description: County in extreme southwestern Virginia where site 44LE121 is located.

name: Little Tennessee Valley
description: Region known as a center of domestication, providing context for Middle Woodland horticulture in southwestern Virginia.

name: ceramics
description: Pottery artifacts found infrequently at Middle Woodland sites, used to interpret site function and occupation patterns.

name: storage pits
description: Archaeological features typical of sedentary lifestyles, absent at some Middle Woodland sites but present at others containing domesticated plant remains.

name: post molds
description: Archaeological features discovered at the Elk Garden Site, some forming patterns of substantial structures with central hearths.

name: Cumberland Plateau
description: A physiographic province spanning eastern Kentucky and extending into Virginia, where Late Archaic through Fort Ancient cultures practiced small-scale environmental manipulation.

name: Kentucky
description: Eastern portion of Kentucky where the Cumberland Plateau study by Delcourt et al. was conducted.

name: Virginia
description: State containing part of the Cumberland Plateau physiographic province, where rockshelter excavations could reveal evidence of cultivation and fire management.

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which cultures began small-scale environmental manipulation and cultivation of native plants around 3000 BP.

name: Fort Ancient
description: Cultural group that practiced environmental manipulation and cultivation in the Cumberland Plateau region.

name: Cliff Palace Pond Rockshelter
description: Archaeological site in the Cumberland Plateau where palynological and sedimentological analyses revealed evidence of forest clearing and cultivation around 3000 BP.

name: Eastern Agricultural Complex
description: A complex of native plants including Curcubita, sunflower, sumpweed, goosefoot, maygrass, knotweed, and amaranth that were cultivated by indigenous peoples.

name: Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which horticultural practices and plant cultivation were practiced, evidenced by ceramic sherds and soil samples.

name: 44LE121
description: Archaeological site where ceramic sherds from a roasting pit feature were analyzed for pollen and residue, revealing evidence of ground meal made from grass seeds.

name: 44LE165
description: Archaeological site in Powell Valley of Lee County where macrobotanical evidence of maize was recovered from a transitional Middle Woodland/Late Woodland context.

name: Powell Valley
description: Valley in Lee County where site 44LE165 is located, containing evidence of early maize cultivation.

name: Lee County
description: County in Virginia where site 44LE165 and evidence of transitional Middle Woodland/Late Woodland maize cultivation was found.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which early experimentation with maize cultivation occurred, evidenced at site 44LE165 with radiocarbon dates of BC 645-880 and BC 780-1000.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period transitional from Middle Woodland, during which maize cultivation continued and Radford ceramics were produced.

name: Radford ceramics
description: Ceramic type associated with the late Middle Woodland/early Late Woodland transition, found co-occurring with maize remains at site 44LE165.

name: Middle-Late Woodland transition
description: Archaeological period marking a transition characterized by the adoption of bow and arrow technology among Virginia's Native peoples.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Virginia
description: Geographic region where the adoption of bow and arrow technology is being studied archaeologically.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: bow and arrow
description: Hunting technology that was adopted by Virginia's Native peoples during the Middle-Late Woodland transition, requiring at least a half-millennium for full integration.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: triangular point form
description: Standardized projectile point type that blanketed much of Eastern North America by the end of the Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Eastern North America
description: Large geographic region that was blanketed by standardized triangular point forms by the end of the Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Middle Woodland period
description: Archaeological period during which triangular projectile point forms became widespread across Eastern North America.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Southeast
description: Geographic region where archaeologists have applied discriminant function analyses to study bow and arrow adoption.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Ohio Valley
description: Geographic region where archaeologists have studied projectile point morphological changes to understand bow and arrow adoption.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period associated with continued use of triangular projectile point forms.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: notched or stemmed hafted bifaces
description: Earlier projectile point technology used by Eastern Native cultures before the shift to triangular forms.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: atlatls
description: Weapon system associated with dart projectiles before the adoption of bow and arrow technology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Middle Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which triangular forms comparable to Middle and Late Woodland types are documented in southern New England and the northeastern Middle Atlantic.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Late Archaic
description: Archaeological period during which triangular forms comparable to Middle and Late Woodland types are documented in southern New England and the northeastern Middle Atlantic.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: southern New England
description: Geographic region where early triangular projectile point forms are documented during the Middle and Late Archaic periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: northeastern Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where early triangular projectile point forms are documented during the Middle and Late Archaic periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: North Carolina
description: Geographic region where the shift to triangular projectile point forms began during the Early Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Badin Phase
description: Early Woodland cultural phase of the Piedmont region in North Carolina when the shift to triangular forms began.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region in North Carolina associated with the Badin Phase during the Early Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Swannanoa Phase
description: Early Woodland cultural phase of the Appalachian Summit region when the shift to triangular forms began.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Appalachian Summit
description: Geographic region associated with the Swannanoa Phase during the Early Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which the shift to triangular projectile point forms began in North Carolina.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Middle Woodland I
description: Archaeological period in Virginia showing smooth evolution of projectile point types representing an evolving regional cultural tradition.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Levanna
description: Thin, broad, and lightweight projectile point style that represents an interruption to the in situ development of regional point traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Yadkin
description: Thin, broad, and lightweight projectile point style that represents an interruption to the in situ development of regional point traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Jacks Reef
description: Thin, broad, and lightweight projectile point style that represents an interruption to the in situ development of regional point traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part0

name: Ohio Valley
description: Geographic region where arrowpoints are recognized by statistically significant reductions in projectile point dimensions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Central Arkansas
description: Geographic region where arrowpoints show statistically significant reductions in mean projectile point characteristics.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Jacks Reef Corner-Notched type
description: A projectile point type that appeared in the Northeast during the Kipp Island Phase, believed to be either a fully developed arrow point or transitional form.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region where the Jacks Reef Corner-Notched type appeared during the Kipp Island Phase.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Kipp Island Phase
description: Cultural phase during which the Jacks Reef Corner-Notched type appeared in the Northeast.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Daugherty's Cave
description: Archaeological site with radiocarbon dates on deposits directly associated with Jacks Reef points, dating from late Middle Woodland to terminal Late Woodland.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period with dates around 1050+/- 60 BP and transitional phases including Middle Woodland I/Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period that includes the terminal phase when some Jacks Reef points were deposited.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Catoctin Creek Site
description: Archaeological site with limestone-tempered cord and fabric-marked ceramics and a Jacks Reef Pentagonal point above transitional Middle Woodland assays.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Jacks Reef Pentagonal point
description: A variant of Jacks Reef point found at the Catoctin Creek Site ten centimeters above transitional Middle Woodland assays.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where bipolar techniques and flake tool assemblages associated with Middle and Late Woodland components are well documented.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Trenton Complex
description: Cultural complex with sites where bifacially reduced triangular forms were made on split cobbles of locally available cryptocrystallines.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Fout Site
description: Archaeological site in the Virginia Ridge and Valley with 584 triangular preforms demonstrating bifacial reduction sequence at a basecamp/hamlet.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Virginia Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where the Fout Site is located.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Licking Creek Formation
description: Geological formation providing thick lenses of chert adjacent to the Fout Site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Virginia Radiocarbon Database
description: Archaeological database maintained by the Virginia Department of Historic Resources containing radiocarbon dating information.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part1

name: Yadkin
description: A type of triangular projectile point associated with the Middle Woodland period, found at various Virginia archaeological sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Levanna
description: A type of triangular projectile point associated with the Middle Woodland period, often found together with Yadkin points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Large Triangular Points
description: A category of Middle Woodland triangular projectile points documented in the radiocarbon database.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Small and Medium Triangular Points
description: Categories of Middle Woodland triangular projectile points documented in the radiocarbon database.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Triangular Points
description: General category of Middle Woodland projectile points that mark the introduction of bow and arrow technology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Skiffes Creek Site
description: An archaeological site in Newport News with early examples of Yadkin/Levanna points associated with Middle Woodland I radiocarbon dates.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Newport News
description: A location in Virginia where the Skiffes Creek Site is situated.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Mockley Series
description: A series of ceramics that co-occur with triangular projectile points in Middle Woodland contexts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Middle Woodland I
description: An early phase of the Middle Woodland period associated with Mockley ceramics and early triangular points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period in Virginia archaeology marked by the introduction of triangular projectile points and bow and arrow technology.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period following Middle Woodland, characterized by gradually declining sizes of triangular projectile points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A later phase of the Middle Woodland period representing progressive refinement after initial bow and arrow innovation.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: A geographic region in Virginia where some of the triangular projectile point sites are located.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia where some of the triangular projectile point sites are located.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia where some of the triangular projectile point sites are located.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region in Virginia where recent excavations support Middle Woodland I Mockley associations.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: bow and arrow
description: A hunting and warfare technology introduced during the Middle Woodland period, associated with triangular projectile points.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: atlatl
description: A spear-throwing device used with dart points, continued to be used alongside bow and arrow during Middle Woodland II.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Piscataway
description: A type of stemmed projectile point associated with dart technology during the Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Rossville
description: A type of stemmed projectile point associated with dart technology during the Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Vernon
description: A type of projectile point identified as a dart point associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Fox Creek
description: A type of projectile point identified as a dart point associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Potts
description: A type of projectile point identified as a dart point associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Nomini
description: A type of projectile point identified as a dart point associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Teardrop
description: A type of projectile point identified as a dart point associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Merom
description: A type of small stemmed projectile point that some analysts classify as arrowpoints, associated with Middle Woodland period.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part2

name: Spessard Site
description: An archaeological site along the James River with Late Woodland and Middle Woodland components, analyzed for faunal assemblages showing changes in species utilization patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: James River
description: A river in Virginia along which Late Woodland and Middle Woodland archaeological sites are located, showing contrasting faunal assemblage patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by reduced species variety in faunal assemblages compared to Middle Woodland times, possibly due to greater reliance on horticulture.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural period characterized by more varied species utilization in faunal assemblages along the James River compared to later periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: bow and arrow
description: A hunting technology that required wider range of raw materials and higher skill level than the atlatl, possibly encouraging labor specialization and affecting prey selection patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: atlatl
description: A hunting technology that required fewer raw materials and less skill than the bow and arrow.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: triangular point
description: Projectile points associated with bow and arrow technology, used in archaeological analysis to study faunal assemblage changes.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: pretriangular point
description: Projectile points that predate triangular points and bow and arrow technology adoption.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: Vandal Minimum
description: A period of global temperature decline that coincided with bow and arrow proliferation in Virginia post-1650 B.P., associated with collapse of exchange systems and reduced organizational complexity.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: Middle Woodland II
description: A cultural period during which tribal boundaries may have been solidified, possibly in response to climatic changes and increased hostilities.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Social Context of Technology: Introductionand Adoption of the Bow and Arrow_part3

name: Middle Woodland
description: A cultural or chronological period in Virginia archaeology during which distinctive ceramic traditions and social organization patterns can be observed.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural or chronological period following the Middle Woodland, characterized by changes in cordage technology and ceramic traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: James River
description: A river region in Virginia where archaeological studies of Middle and Late Woodland ceramic traditions and cordage patterns have been conducted.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: Virginia
description: The geographic region that is the focus of this archaeological gender study, particularly regarding Middle and Late Woodland cultures.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: ceramic sherds
description: Pottery fragments that preserve cordage impressions, used to study twist direction patterns and infer social organization in Middle and Late Woodland societies.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: cordage
description: Twisted fiber technology used in prehistoric Virginia societies, with different twist directions (S and Z) indicating possible gender-based production traditions and social organization patterns.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: net-impressed wares
description: Ceramic vessels impressed with fishing or hunting nets, predominantly showing Z twist cordage patterns in both Middle and Late Woodland periods.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: cord-impressed varieties
description: Ceramic vessels impressed with cordage, showing predominantly S twist patterns and interpreted as products of female cordage-making traditions.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part0

name: Powhatan
description: Native American group for whom Rountree documented net manufacture as part of women's daily schedules.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Eastern North America
description: Geographic region where nets were documented as a regular component of the female meat-acquisition toolkit.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which ceramics with cordage twist were studied from the Chickahominy and James Rivers region.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Chickahominy River
description: River in Virginia where Middle Woodland ceramics with cordage twist were studied.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: James River
description: River in Virginia where Middle Woodland ceramics with cordage twist were studied alongside the Chickahominy River.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where twist direction on ceramics did not correspond to specific archaeological sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Varina
description: Type of sand and crushed rock-tempered pottery that exhibited equal proportions of Z and S twist.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Prince George
description: Type of sand and crushed rock-tempered pottery that exhibited equal proportions of Z and S twist.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Popes Creek
description: Type of sand and crushed rock-tempered pottery that exhibited equal proportions of Z and S twist.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Mockley
description: Type of shell-tempered pottery where almost 100% exhibited S twist, indicating homogenization of ceramic technology and associated with potential Algonquian migration.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Big Man, Big Woman: Gender Studies in MiddleWoodland Archaeology, with a Focus on Virginia_part1

name: Algonquian

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where bluff-top stone and earthen burial mounds are found, representing localized Middle Woodland traditions.

name: Delmarva Adena
description: Late Early Woodland/Middle Woodland I cultural phenomenon characterized by mortuary-exchange centers that provided sanctification of exchange relationships.

name: Pig Point Complex
description: Archaeological site in Anne Arundel County, Maryland where Delmarva Adena ritual mortuary pits were discovered.

name: Anne Arundel County
description: County in Maryland where the Pig Point Complex archaeological site is located.

name: Maryland
description: State where Anne Arundel County and the Pig Point Complex are located.

name: Ohio Valley
description: Geographic region that was previously thought to be the source of Middle Woodland mortuary traditions and trade goods in Virginia.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region mentioned as a potential source of trade goods during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where Hopewell and Adena influences are viewed as diffused, ideological overlays for indigenous social complexity.

name: Adena
description: Cultural tradition from the Ohio Valley that previously was thought to influence Virginia mortuary practices but is now seen as diffused ideological overlay.

name: Hopewell
description: Cultural tradition from the Ohio Valley that previously was thought to influence Virginia mortuary practices but is now seen as diffused ideological overlay.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period that preceded the Middle Woodland and saw the development of focused exchange systems and restricted mobility patterns.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period characterized by mortuary ceremonialism, social complexity, and exchange networks, spanning from ca. 2450 BP to 1850 BP.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period that followed the Middle Woodland, characterized by more egalitarian accretional mound traditions and continuity with historically documented Native cultures.

name: Archaic
description: Earlier cultural period that developed exchange systems that formed the roots of later Middle Woodland social complexity.

name: Lewis Creek Mound Culture
description: Late Woodland cultural tradition characterized by more egalitarian burial mound practices, separated by 800 years from Middle Woodland mound building.

name: Algonquian
description: Cultural/linguistic group hypothesized to have migrated during the Middle Woodland period, though this hypothesis is challenged by mortuary practice analysis.

name: Iroquoian
description: Cultural/linguistic group suggested by mortuary practice analysis as having possible migration or cultural association during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Gardner
description: Archaeologist who defined aspects of the Middle Woodland period and argued for the 600-year time span of bluff-top burial mound phenomenon based on radiocarbon dates.

name: Anderson
description: Archaeologist who collaborated with Gardner in describing the localized traditions of northern Ridge and Valley burial mounds.

name: Custer
description: Archaeologist who studied Delmarva Adena phenomenon and Middle Woodland exchange systems, describing them as growing out of ephemeral, ranked social systems.

name: Luckenback
description: Archaeologist who discovered Delmarva Adena ritual mortuary pits at the Pig Point Complex in 2013.

name: Stewart
description: Archaeologist who described Middle Woodland trade in extra-regional exotics as lacking structure and studied Early Woodland focused exchange systems.

name: MacCord
description: Archaeologist who studied the Lewis Creek Mound Culture, the Late Woodland accretional mound tradition.

name: Fowke
description: Researcher who conducted mound investigations in 1894 that documented accretional burial mounds in Virginia river drainages.

name: Dunham
description: Archaeologist who reanalyzed Fowke's 1894 mound investigations in 1994 and hypothesized about bounded cemeteries for high ranking individuals.

name: James River
description: Major river drainage system in Virginia containing documented accretional burial mounds.

name: Shenandoah
description: River drainage system in Virginia containing documented accretional burial mounds.

name: Rappahannock
description: River drainage system in Virginia containing documented accretional burial mounds.

name: Cowpasture
description: One of four documented accretional burial mounds hypothesized to have served as bounded cemeteries for high ranking individuals.

name: Linville
description: Accretional burial mound containing Middle Woodland Point Peninsula-style artifacts in sub-mound pits.

name: Hayes Creek
description: One of four documented accretional burial mounds hypothesized to have served as bounded cemeteries for high ranking individuals.

name: Rapidan
description: Accretional burial mound with a radiocarbon date of BC 660+/-110 from a sub-mound pit.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period associated with mortuary ceremonialism, stone/earthen mounds, and Point Peninsula-style artifacts.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period associated with accretional mounds and descended from earlier Middle Woodland populations.

name: Point Peninsula-style artifacts
description: Diagnostic artifacts from the Middle Woodland period found in sub-mound pits at Linville Mound.

name: Gala Site
description: Archaeological site in the upper James River basin with at least 28 burials and cremations interpreted as initial stages of mound construction.

name: Bowden
description: Archaeologist who studied the Gala Site and noted similarities between its features and accretional mound components.

name: Boyd
description: Archaeologist who studied the Gala Site and noted similarities between its features and accretional mound components.

name: Gardner
description: Researcher who studied evidence of social disintegration and possible regional abandonment of interior Virginia north of the James River.

name: Nash
description: Researcher who in 2006 proposed using landscape approaches and locational studies to understand Middle Woodland mounds and settled communities.

name: northern Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where three mound clusters were analyzed for sight lines and mortuary ceremonialism.

name: Indian Grave Ridge
description: Archaeological mound cluster site in the northern Shenandoah Valley containing three documented stone mounds.

name: Sandy Hook
description: Archaeological site in the northern Shenandoah Valley containing a single mound (44SH100), the largest Middle Woodland I mound documented for western Virginia at 33 feet in length.

name: Thunderbird Ranch
description: Archaeological site in the northern Shenandoah Valley containing 11 mounds with sight lines connecting them to each other and to Middle Woodland I sites on the floodplain below.

name: 44PA0106
description: The largest stone mound at Indian Grave Ridge, positioned on a bluff overlooking a fish weir and Middle Woodland I settlement.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: River location where a fish weir was positioned, visible from the largest mound at Indian Grave Ridge.

name: 44PA0114
description: Middle Woodland I settlement site accompanying a fish weir in the South Fork of the Shenandoah River.

name: 44SH100
description: Single mound at Sandy Hook, the largest Middle Woodland I mound documented for western Virginia at 33 feet in length.

name: western Virginia
description: Geographic region where the largest Middle Woodland I mound (44SH100) is documented.

name: Middle Woodland I
description: Cultural period associated with settlements and mounds in the Shenandoah Valley sites discussed.

name: Adena
description: Cultural group referenced in landscape-based studies that shifted ritual centers to edges of corporate group territories.

name: Rock Creek
description: Creek that confluences with the Potomac River near the Georgetown waterfront where the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit was discovered.

name: Potomac River
description: River that confluences with Rock Creek near the Georgetown waterfront where the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit was discovered.

name: Georgetown
description: Location with waterfront area near the confluence of Rock Creek and Potomac River where Middle Woodland mortuary practices were documented.

name: Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit
description: Middle Woodland mortuary site discovered during 1996 CRM assessment, containing Pope's Creek and Mockley ceramics with radiocarbon dates of BC 640-790.

name: Middle Woodland period
description: Cultural period associated with the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit, specifically the late portion dated to BC 640-790.

name: Pope's Creek
description: Type of ceramics found in the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: Mockley ceramics
description: Type of ceramics found in the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: incised elk antler comb
description: Exotic artifact found in association with human remains in the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: fossilized shark teeth
description: Artifacts recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: slate pendants
description: Artifacts recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: wooden bead
description: Artifact recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: sandstone phallus
description: Artifact recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: antler discs
description: Artifacts recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: chert triangular projectile points
description: Stone tools recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: preserved fabric
description: Textile artifact recovered from the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: feather fan
description: Ceremonial object indicated by the tailbone of a large bird found in the Whitehurst Freeway cremation burial pit.

name: Point Peninsula Focus
description: Middle Woodland cultural group or phase documented from Ontario to southeastern Virginia, showing archaeological parallels to the Whitehurst burial.

name: Kipp Island Phase
description: Cultural phase within the Point Peninsula Focus, defined by Ritchie (1994) and documented from Ontario to southeastern Virginia.

name: Ontario
description: Northern extent of the geographic range where Point Peninsula Focus, Kipp Island Phase is documented.

name: southeastern Virginia
description: Southern extent of the geographic range where Point Peninsula Focus, Kipp Island Phase is documented.

name: Jacks Reef Site
description: Archaeological site in northern New York State with Point Peninsula burials showing the greatest affinity to the Whitehurst burial.

name: northern New York State
description: Geographic location of the Jacks Reef Site, which contains burials with similarities to the Whitehurst cremation burial.

name: Whitehurst Freeway Site
description: Archaeological site that contained a burial with an antler comb and a stone pendant manufactured from Shenandoah Valley silicified slate.

name: Linville Mound
description: Archaeological site in Rockingham County with cremated sub-mound burials containing antler combs similar to those found at Whitehurst.

name: Hand Site
description: Archaeological site in Southampton County with burials that evidenced 'the fire ceremony' and contained unique grave goods including fossil shark teeth, antler combs, bone discs, and bar gorgets.

name: Rockingham County
description: County in Virginia where the Linville Mound archaeological site is located.

name: Southampton County
description: County in Virginia where the Hand Site archaeological site is located.

name: Nottoway
description: Historic Native American group associated with the Hand Site location.

name: Meherrin
description: Historic Native American group associated with the Hand Site location.

name: Late Woodland Period
description: Cultural period associated with the Hand Site locus.

name: Protohistoric Period
description: Cultural period associated with the Hand Site locus, following the Late Woodland.

name: Middle Woodland Period
description: Cultural period associated with the Whitehurst feature and Hand Site Burial 55, dated to BC 690-970.

name: Kipp Island Phase
description: Cultural phase of the Point Peninsula culture that influenced groups in Virginia's Coastal Plain and Ridge and Valley regions.

name: Point Peninsula Culture
description: Archaeological culture associated with mortuary practices found at Virginia sites, with connections to the Ohio area and Northeast.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region in Virginia where some of the groups influenced by Point Peninsula culture were located.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia where some of the groups influenced by Point Peninsula culture were located.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region in Virginia that was the source of silicified slate used to manufacture a stone pendant found in the Whitehurst burial.

name: Ohio
description: Geographic region identified as a source of post-Hopewellian cultural developments that diffused into the Southeast during late Middle Woodland times.

name: Southeast
description: Geographic region that received post-Hopewellian cultural developments from the Ohio area during late Middle Woodland times.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region that experienced similar cultural developments parallel to those in the Southeast during late Middle Woodland times.

name: Owasco Culture
description: Archaeological culture that succeeded Point Peninsula, with questions about the nature of this transition.

name: Ontario Iroquois
description: Cultural tradition that grew out of Point Peninsula culture according to some interpretations.

name: Iroquoian
description: Cultural group associated with mortuary practices of interring high status individuals with other community members, and potentially intrusive in the Great Lakes region.

name: Algonquian
description: Cultural group with mortuary practices of separating high status individuals from the community in death, different from Iroquoian practices.

name: Proto-Algonquian
description: Linguistic community that some argue is archaeologically represented by the Point Peninsula culture group.

name: Central Algonquian
description: Language group that separated from Eastern Algonquian in the Great Lakes region during BC 200-700.

name: Eastern Algonquian
description: Language group that separated from Central Algonquian in the Great Lakes region during BC 200-700.

name: Great Lakes
description: Geographic region where the separation of Central and Eastern Algonquian language groups occurred.

name: Jacks Reef
description: Exotic projectile point style associated with Algonquian migration from BC 200-700.

name: Levanna
description: Exotic projectile point style associated with Algonquian migration from BC 200-700.

name: Webb Complex
description: Mortuary complex associated with the spread of Algonquian migration from BC 200-700.

name: Parkline Phase
description: Mortuary complex associated with the spread of Algonquian migration from BC 200-700.

name: BC 200-700
description: Time period of proposed second wave of Algonquian migration marked by spread of exotic projectile point styles and mortuary complexes.

name: BC 690-970
description: Radiocarbon date range from Hand Site Burial 55 charcoal, overlapping with dates from the Whitehurst feature.

name: Fiedel
description: Scholar referenced in discussions of prestige goods networks and Algonquian population movements.

name: Potter
description: Scholar who argued in 2002 that the prestige goods network exemplified by Kipp Island Phase artifacts marked the movement of Eastern Algonquian-speaking peoples into the Chesapeake Bay watershed between BC 650 and 800.

name: Kipp Island Phase
description: Archaeological phase characterized by exotic artifacts and mortuary rituals, associated with prestige goods networks and potentially marking Algonquian population movements.

name: Eastern Algonquian-speaking peoples
description: Cultural group that moved from their Proto-Algonquian homeland near Lakes Ontario and Erie into the Chesapeake Bay watershed between BC 650 and 800.

name: Proto-Algonquian homeland
description: Original homeland of Algonquian-speaking peoples located in the vicinity of Lakes Ontario and Erie.

name: Lakes Ontario and Erie
description: Geographic region identified as the vicinity of the Proto-Algonquian homeland.

name: Chesapeake Bay watershed
description: Geographic region into which Eastern Algonquian-speaking peoples moved between BC 650 and 800.

name: Hand Site
description: Archaeological site with Iroquoian occupation that Potter identifies as restricted to the historic era.

name: Iroquoian
description: Cultural group associated with occupation at the Hand Site during the historic era.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which Colington Phase ceramics were produced by Algonquian-speakers.

name: Colington Phase
description: Late Woodland archaeological phase with ceramics associated with Algonquian-speakers.

name: Point Peninsula
description: Archaeological culture or phase associated with Algonquian-speaking peoples.

name: Curry
description: Scholar who in 1999 questioned the wisdom of identifying population migrations through mortuary practices, noting confusion and uncertainty in the archaeological record.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where mortuary sites have limited documentation and incomplete archaeological records.

name: Boyd and Boyd
description: Scholars who in 1992 made observations about statewide mortuary practices similar to Curry's concerns.

name: Middle Woodland II
description: Archaeological period found in the northern Ridge and Valley and Inner Coastal Plain physiographic zones.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: Physiographic zone where Middle Woodland II Kipp Island-like burials are found.

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: Physiographic zone where Middle Woodland II Kipp Island-like burials are found.

name: Gardner
description: Scholar who in 1993 documented indigenous burial practices including sub-mound cremation burials of bluff-top earthen and stone mounds.

name: bluff-top earthen and stone mounds
description: Indigenous burial structures containing sub-mound cremation burials documented by Gardner.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region identified as the origin of new alliance and exchange networks.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which alliance formation occurred.

name: Stewart
description: Scholar who studied Abbott Zoned Incised ceramics and proposed a model for Middle Woodland alliance formation in 1998.

name: Abbott Zoned Incised
description: Elaborate, zoned decorated pottery most closely associated with the Trenton Complex at Abbott Farm, New Jersey, dating between AD 200-900.

name: Trenton Complex
description: Archaeological complex at Abbott Farm, New Jersey, most closely associated with Abbott Zoned Incised ceramics.

name: Abbott Farm
description: Archaeological site in New Jersey associated with the Trenton Complex and Abbott Zoned Incised ceramics dating between AD 200-900.

name: New Jersey
description: State where Abbott Farm archaeological site is located.

name: Mockley-like ceramics
description: Ceramic type bearing Abbott Zoned incised motifs, known from six sites in the Virginia Coastal Plain.

name: Virginia Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where six sites with Mockley-like ceramics bearing Abbott Zoned incised motifs are located.

name: Maycock's Point
description: Intensively occupied aggregate site located in rich riverine environments in the Virginia Coastal Plain, associated with Mockley-like ceramics.

name: Lattanzi et al
description: Scholars who in 2011 studied the contexts of Abbott Zoned Incised ceramics at Abbott Farm.

name: New England
description: Northern extent of the geographic distribution of Abbott Zoned Incised pottery, extending to southern Virginia.

name: southern Virginia
description: Southern extent of the geographic distribution of Abbott Zoned Incised pottery, extending from New England.

name: Abbott Zoned ceramics
description: A distinctive ceramic type that originated in the north and was found at Virginia sites, serving as visible markers of identity used in feasting settings and representing kinship connections between peoples.

name: Abbott Farm
description: An archaeological site where Abbott Zoned ceramics were found, with clay pastes distinct from Virginia sites and some AZI vessels brought from Piedmont or Coastal Plain settings.

name: Mockley
description: Utilitarian ceramic vessels with different production processes from AZI vessels, some of which were transported between sites in Virginia coastal plain and to the Delaware River Valley.

name: Hatch Site
description: A ritualistic aggregation site where AZI tradition appeared during the Late Woodland period, with AMS dates on contexts containing AZI placing it at 1000-1400 CE in features associated with feasting rituals.

name: Maycock's Point
description: A Virginia site where AZI samples were found to be mostly locally produced vessels rather than products of long-distance trade, with conventional radiocarbon dates of AD 354-657.

name: Late Woodland period
description: The period during which the AZI tradition appeared at the Hatch Site according to recent research.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region that was one possible source for clay used to produce some AZI vessels brought to Abbott Farm.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region that was one possible source for clay used to produce some AZI vessels brought to Abbott Farm.

name: Virginia coastal plain
description: A region where utilitarian Mockley vessels were transported between sites.

name: Delaware River Valley
description: A region to which some utilitarian Mockley vessels were transported from Virginia.

name: James River Inner Coastal Plain
description: The region where AZI ceramic type had longevity of at least 600 years according to radiocarbon dates.

name: 1000-1400 CE
description: The timespan indicated by AMS dates on contexts containing AZI at the Hatch Site.

name: AD 354-657
description: The conventional radiocarbon date reported for Maycock's Point that aligns with early dates on AZI contexts at Abbott Farm.

name: Marginella Burial Cave Project
description: Archaeological project that documented 32 Native American burial caves in southwestern Virginia through 1995.

name: Powell River

name: Clinch River

name: Holston River
description: River drainage in southwestern Virginia where Native American burial caves were documented.

name: Ridge and Valley

name: Appalachian Plateau
description: Physiographic region in southwestern Virginia where Middle Woodland mortuary practices have been studied.

name: Tennessee

name: Virginia/Tennessee cluster

name: Lee County

name: 44LE169

name: Late Woodland

name: prismatic blades

name: Maryland

name: Kirsti Uunila
