name: Late Woodland period
description: A cultural period usually considered to have begun around AD 900 and ended with European Contact, ca. AD 1600, characterized by maize horticulture and settled village life.

name: Potomac River
description: A river that served as a conduit for movement of people, ideas, and items during the Late Woodland period.

name: Atlantic Coast
description: A coastal region connected to the Potomac River system for movement of people and goods.

name: Ohio River
description: A river system adjacent to the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system.

name: Susquehanna
description: A drainage system adjacent to the Potomac-Shenandoah system.

name: Ridge and Valley
description: One of Virginia's five physiographic provinces through which the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system cuts, located from west to east.

name: Blue Ridge
description: One of Virginia's five physiographic provinces through which the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system cuts.

name: Piedmont
description: One of Virginia's five physiographic provinces through which the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system cuts, separated from the Coastal Plain by the Fall Line.

name: Coastal Plain
description: One of Virginia's five physiographic provinces through which the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system cuts, separated from the Piedmont by the Fall Line.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic feature that separates the Coastal Plain from the Piedmont and represents a major physical and cultural barrier.

name: European Contact
description: The period around AD 1600 that marked the end of the Late Woodland period.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex concentrated along the Middle Potomac River and major tributary streams, dated from AD 900 to ca. AD 1300, characterized by villages with ring-shaped layouts and Shepard wares pottery.

name: Middle Potomac River
description: The area where the Montgomery Complex cultural area was concentrated.

name: Shenandoah River
description: A major tributary stream of the Potomac River in Virginia where Montgomery Complex peoples were located.

name: Monocacy River
description: A major tributary stream in Maryland where Montgomery Complex peoples were located.

name: Maryland
description: The state where the Monocacy River and part of the Montgomery Complex cultural area was located.

name: wigwam-style houses
description: Type of houses that surrounded open plazas in Montgomery Complex villages with ring-shaped layouts.

name: Shepard wares
description: The major ceramic type associated with the Montgomery Complex, tempered with crushed quartz and/or igneous rock.

name: Lewis Creek Mound Culture
aliases: Central Virginia Late Woodland Mound Complex
description: A Late Woodland complex consisting of over a dozen mounds within a 50 mile radius of Staunton, Virginia, overlapping the southern part of the Northern Virginia area, dating from AD 950 to 1450 or 1350.

name: Staunton
description: A Virginia city that served as the center of the Lewis Creek Mound Culture/Central Virginia Late Woodland Mound Complex.

name: Mason Island Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex that appeared by AD 1450 and may have forced Montgomery Complex groups out of the Middle and Upper Potomac Valley, characterized by Page wares pottery tempered with limestone.

name: Upper Potomac Valley
description: A region from which Mason Island Complex groups may have forced Montgomery Complex peoples.

name: Potomac River Complex
description: A cultural group that Montgomery Complex peoples may have become after being forced down the Potomac River by Mason Island Complex groups.

name: Page wares
description: The major ceramic type associated with the Mason Island Complex, typically tempered with limestone.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex located around the Fall Line along the Potomac River, generally thought to have been intrusive to the region with controversial origins.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: Archaeological complex dated from AD 1300 to 1600, associated with nucleated and palisaded villages and ossuary burial practices.

name: Patawomeck
description: Historically known group that the Potomac Creek Complex peoples became after 1600.

name: Piscataway
description: Historically known group that the Potomac Creek Complex peoples became after 1600.

name: Potomac Creek wares
description: Major associated ceramic type of the Potomac Creek Complex, with vessels tempered with crushed rock such as quartz or sand.

name: Luray Complex
description: Archaeological complex distributed along the middle Potomac and Shenandoah Rivers, considered intrusive with western origins.

name: Potomac River
description: River along which the Luray Complex was distributed (middle section).

name: Shenandoah River
description: River along which the Luray Complex was distributed.

name: Mason Island Complex
description: Earlier archaeological complex that was replaced by the intrusive Luray Complex.

name: Keyser wares
description: Major ceramic type of the Luray/Keyser Complex, with vessels tempered with shell.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Archaeological period in Northern Virginia being transformed through new excavation strategies and technologies.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland period understanding is being transformed through new excavations.

name: Fisher site
description: Montgomery Complex village site first excavated in 1938 and more recently in 2002.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: Archaeological complex associated with village sites including Fisher and Winslow sites.

name: Winslow site
description: Montgomery Complex village site first excavated in 1940 and more recently in 2002 and 2003.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: Potomac Creek Complex village site first excavated in 1935 and again in 1996.

name: Accokeek Creek site
description: Potomac Creek Complex village site first excavated in 1935, with recent research focusing on curated artifacts.

name: Keyser Farm site
description: Luray Complex village site first excavated in 1940 and again from 2003 to 2007.

name: maize
description: Specific crop that people in Northern Virginia began to intensively grow, dated through radiocarbon analysis.

name: beans
description: Specific crop that people in Northern Virginia began to intensively grow, dated through radiocarbon analysis.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Cultural period in Northern Virginia that began around AD 900 and ended with sustained European Contact when Jamestown was established in AD 1607.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland peoples shifted to maize-based horticulture and settled village life.

name: Jamestown
description: English colony established in AD 1607, marking the end of the Late Woodland period through sustained European Contact.

name: maize
description: Cultivated crop that became the basis for full-scale horticulture during the Late Woodland period, though its adoption date is debated.

name: ceramic vessels
description: Pottery artifacts used by archaeologists to define cultural groups based on changes in technology and surface impressions.

name: Potomac River Valley
description: River valley explored by first Europeans, with strongest ethnohistoric data concentrated around its mouth.

name: Atlantic Coast of Virginia
description: Coastal region of Virginia explored by first Europeans and associated with historically known social groups.

name: T. Dale Stewart
description: Archaeologist who initiated systematic excavations at the Potomac Creek site beginning in 1937.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: Archaeological site where T. Dale Stewart began systematic excavations in 1937, initiating seven decades of investigation.

name: Potomac River
description: River flowing into and through Northern Virginia and adjacent states, with headwaters in Maryland. Served as a conduit for movement of people, ideas, and items to and from the Atlantic Coast.

name: Shenandoah River
description: River flowing into and through Northern Virginia, part of the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system that served as a conduit for movement to and from the Atlantic Coast.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region through which the Potomac and Shenandoah Rivers and their tributaries flow.

name: Maryland
description: Adjacent state where the headwaters of the Potomac River are located.

name: West Virginia
description: Adjacent state through which the Potomac and Shenandoah Rivers flow.

name: Pennsylvania
description: Adjacent state through which the Potomac and Shenandoah Rivers flow.

name: Ohio River
description: River system geographically adjacent to the headwaters of the Potomac River in Maryland, connected via the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system.

name: Susquehanna River
description: River system geographically adjacent to the headwaters of the Potomac River in Maryland, connected via drainage systems.

name: Atlantic Coast
description: Coastal region connected to the Ohio River and Susquehanna drainage systems via the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Cultural period during which the climate was essentially modern, and peoples in Northern Virginia would have been well versed in available wild plant and animal resources.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Physiographic province known for abundant natural resources including oysters and shellfish that supported large populations with complex social and political organizations.

name: Virginia
description: State where the first Europeans set foot on the shores and encountered complex societies supported by abundant natural resources.

name: Piedmont
description: Physiographic province where groups hunted deer, raccoon and beaver.

name: Blue Ridge
description: Mountainous physiographic province that was a source of deer, elk, black bear, and wild turkey.

name: Valley and Ridge
description: Mountainous physiographic province that was a source of deer, elk, black bear, and wild turkey.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic feature on the Potomac River that would have been a major fishing area.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: Archaeological site on the Potomac River at the Fall Line that contained evidence for sturgeon, garfish, yellow perch, catfish, sucker, and consumption of tuckahoe, wild rice, and edible roots of cattails.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region that has been the focus of considerable archaeological research over several decades, with American Indian interactions occurring within drainage systems that extended beyond Virginia's borders.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Archaeological period during which village societies developed throughout parts of the Middle Atlantic region, with villages forming after AD 1450 in the northern Shenandoah Valley.

name: Maryland
description: State bordering Virginia where major recent archaeological investigations have been conducted along the Potomac River, providing important context for understanding Northern Virginia archaeology.

name: West Virginia
description: State mentioned as one of Virginia's borders, indicating that American Indian interactions occurred across these arbitrary political boundaries.

name: Potomac River
description: Major river along which American Indian families lived and where major archaeological investigations have been conducted, particularly on the Maryland side.

name: North Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: River along which American Indian families lived before joining together to form compact, planned village communities after AD 1450.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: River along which American Indian families lived before joining together to form compact, planned village communities after AD 1450.

name: Shenandoah River
description: River system including North and South Forks where American Indian families lived and formed village communities during the Late Woodland period.

name: northern Shenandoah Valley
description: Geographic region where village formation is thought to have occurred after AD 1450 during the Late Woodland period.

name: upper Shenandoah River valley
description: Geographic region where the lack of burials at village sites or extensive rebuilding suggests villages were occupied for short periods.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Broader geographic region throughout which village societies developed during the Late Woodland period, including Northern Virginia.

name: AD 1450
description: Date after which American Indian families are thought to have joined together to form compact, planned village communities in the northern Shenandoah Valley.

name: AD 1200
description: Date after which Late Woodland peoples in Northern Virginia are argued to have lived in villages, having previously lived in hamlets.

name: Contact Period
description: A chronological period when archaeological evidence suggests the Shenandoah Valley was abandoned by village dwellers, possibly used as a hunting reserve by the Iroquois.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: A geographic region that was abandoned by village dwellers by the beginning of the Contact Period and possibly used as a hunting reserve by the Iroquois.

name: Iroquois
description: A cultural group that possibly used the Shenandoah Valley as a hunting reserve during the Contact Period.

name: Fall Line
description: A geographic area of the Potomac River where Contact Period village settlements are known.

name: Potomac River
description: A river where Contact Period village settlements existed at the Fall Line and downstream areas.

name: 44ST1
description: A Patawomeck village site visited by Captain John Smith.

name: Patawomeck
description: An Algonquian cultural group encountered by Captain John Smith who lived in a village (44ST1) near the Potomac Creek site.

name: Captain John Smith
description: A historical figure who visited a Patawomeck village site (44ST1).

name: Potomac Creek site
description: An archaeological site near which a Patawomeck village visited by Captain John Smith was located, and one of the major palisaded village sites in Northern Virginia.

name: Algonquians
description: A cultural group to which the Patawomecks encountered by Smith belonged.

name: wigwams
description: Historically recorded domed structures similar to the houses with circular floor plans where individual families lived in hamlets and village communities.

name: palisade
description: A protective structure that often surrounded village settlements to protect villagers from enemies and wild animals while fostering community identity.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A geographic region that includes major palisaded village sites such as the Potomac Creek site and Keyser Farm site.

name: Keyser Farm site
description: One of the major palisaded village sites in Northern Virginia, located along the South Fork of the Shenandoah River.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: The location where the Keyser Farm site (44PA1) is situated.

name: maize
description: An agricultural crop whose widespread adoption may have been related to living in nucleated villages, with most valid radiocarbon dates in Virginia falling after AD 1100.

name: AD 1100
description: The time period after which most valid radiocarbon dates on maize in Virginia fall.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic province where people ate more maize than those who inhabited the Coastal Plain, according to analyses of human skeletal remains.

name: Ridge and Valley provinces
description: Geographic provinces where people ate more maize than those who inhabited the Coastal Plain.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region where inhabitants ate less maize than those in the Piedmont and Ridge and Valley provinces, possibly because they were the last to adopt maize due to access to abundant natural resources like shellfish.

name: Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system
description: A geographic region where the exact paths of maize adoption are not well known and where Late Woodland peoples lived.

name: beans
description: A dietary crop adopted some centuries after maize horticulture began in the region, likely after AD 1300.

name: AD 1300
description: The time period after which beans were likely adopted as part of the diet in the region.

name: Late Woodland peoples
description: A cultural group in the Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system for whom locally available wild plants probably continued to be critical to their diet.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A cultural complex defined by the presence of Shepard wares, which were slab-constructed and tempered with crushed quartz and/or igneous rock.

name: Shepard wares
description: Ceramic vessels that were slab-constructed and tempered with crushed quartz and/or igneous rock, with the majority having final Z-twist cordage impressions and radiocarbon dated between AD 1100 and 1370.

name: AD 1100 and 1370
description: The time period during which Shepard wares have been radiocarbon dated.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex characterized by villages with circular single-family houses arranged around central plazas, associated with Shepard wares and found in the Monocacy River drainage area.

name: Winslow site
description: A Montgomery Complex archaeological site that was reinvestigated in 2002-2003, revealing circular dwelling outlines and traces of a palisade, demonstrating similarities to Monongahela tradition village sites.

name: Monongahela tradition
description: A cultural tradition characterized by ring-shaped village sites located north and west of the Montgomery Complex area.

name: Monocacy River
description: The largest tributary of the Potomac River in the Piedmont physiographic province, along which Montgomery Complex sites with Shepard wares have been found.

name: Potomac River
description: A major river in the Piedmont physiographic province of which the Monocacy River is the largest tributary.

name: Piedmont physiographic province
description: A geographic region where the Potomac River and its tributary the Monocacy River are located.

name: Monocacy Valley
description: The valley area along the Monocacy River where Montgomery Complex groups practiced a seasonal subsistence strategy involving gathering, hunting, and some horticulture.

name: Maryland
description: The state where Montgomery Complex sites in the Monocacy River drainage are located.

name: Shepard wares
description: A type of pottery associated with Montgomery Complex sites, found at 20 sites along the Monocacy River.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: An archaeological complex in Northern Virginia that preceded the Mason Island Complex around AD 1450, with peoples who may have moved downstream to become the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A geographic region in Virginia where various Late Woodland archaeological complexes are found.

name: Lewis Creek Mound Culture
aliases: Central Virginia Late Woodland mound complex
description: An archaeological complex consisting of over a dozen accretional mounds with hundreds to thousands of burials, used largely between AD 950 and 1450.

name: Monacan
description: A Native American group suggested as possible descendants of the people who created the Central Virginia mounds.

name: Manahoak
description: A Native American group suggested as possible descendants of the people who created the Central Virginia mounds.

name: Mason Island Complex
description: An archaeological complex that succeeded Montgomery Complex groups in the middle and upper Potomac River valley around AD 1450, practicing limited maize agriculture.

name: Potomac River valley
aliases: middle and upper Potomac River valley
description: A geographic region where Mason Island Complex groups are thought to have succeeded Montgomery Complex groups around AD 1450.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: An archaeological complex that Montgomery Complex peoples supposedly became after being forced downstream by Mason Island groups.

name: Eastern Woodlands
description: A broad cultural region where Late Woodland mortuary customs were practiced, providing context for the Central Virginia Mound Complex.

name: Page wares
description: Ceramic vessels associated with Mason Island sites, frequently tempered with crushed limestone and characterized by cordmarked surfaces, added rim strips, and various decorative treatments.

name: Page wares
description: Ceramic type used throughout the Late Woodland period with limestone or limestone and calcite tempering, characterized by collars with cord impressions parallel to the rim and notches or pinches along collar bases.

name: Keyser Farm site
description: Type site for Page wares located on the South Fork of the Shenandoah River, also recognized as the site where the Luray Focus was first identified with a component dated to the early 15th century AD.

name: South Fork of the Shenandoah River
description: River location where the Keyser Farm site is situated.

name: Huffman site
description: Archaeological site investigated as part of the Gathright Dam project that uncovered the most impressive assemblage of Page ceramics in Virginia.

name: Gathright Dam project
description: Archaeological compliance project that involved excavations at the Huffman site.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Archaeological period during which Page wares were used and when cultural ties with groups in Maryland and Pennsylvania increased after AD 1300.

name: Radford wares
description: Limestone-tempered ceramic type that differs from Page wares.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: Archaeological complex traditionally dated between AD 1300 and 1600.

name: Potomac Creek ware
description: Ceramic type first described in 1903 by William Henry Holmes, tempered with crushed rock such as quartz or coarse sand, replacing fabric impressed shell-tempered pottery.

name: William Henry Holmes
description: Person who first described the Potomac Creek ware type in 1903 using sherds from the Potomac Creek site surface.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: Archaeological site containing five ossuaries, one with remains of at least 287 individuals, where Potomac Creek ceramics replaced earlier fabric impressed shell-tempered pottery.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where Potomac Creek ceramics replaced earlier pottery types.

name: Potomac Creek Cord impressed vessels
description: Ceramic vessels with final Z-twist cordage impressions.

name: Accokeek Creek site
description: Archaeological site on the Maryland side of the Potomac River where over 1400 individuals were interred in four large ossuaries.

name: Potomac River
description: River along which the Accokeek Creek site is located on the Maryland side.

name: Patawomeck Indians
description: Historically known group in Virginia that Potomac Creek groups became by AD 1600, related to the Piscataway Indians of Maryland.

name: Piscataway Indians
description: Cultural group of Maryland related to the Patawomeck Indians of Virginia.

name: middle Potomac
description: River region where the Mason Island Complex was replaced by the Luray Focus or Complex.

name: Shenandoah rivers
description: River region along with the middle Potomac where the Mason Island Complex was replaced by the Luray Focus.

name: Mason Island Complex
description: Archaeological complex that was replaced by the Luray Focus or Complex along the middle Potomac and Shenandoah rivers.

name: Luray Focus
description: Archaeological complex first recognized at the Keyser Farm site with a component dated to the early 15th century AD, characterized by large, circular or oval, palisaded villages.

name: Hughes site
description: Luray Complex village site located in the Piedmont region of Maryland.

name: Biggs Ford site
description: Luray Complex village site located in the Piedmont region of Maryland.

name: Ridge and Valley province
description: Geographic region where Luray Complex village sites are primarily known from.

name: Piedmont
description: Geographic region in Maryland where the Hughes and Biggs Ford sites are located.

name: Potomac Valley
description: Valley region into which the Luray Complex peoples represent a clear intrusion from points well to the west.

name: Keyser ceramics
description: Dominant ceramic type of the Luray Complex, tempered with crushed mussel shell and most were cord-marked.

name: Keyser Cord-marked
description: Ceramic ware that became predominant in the upper Potomac region sometime after AD 1400, characterized by vertical cord markings on rims and final S-twist cordage impressions.

name: Potomac River
description: River where the upper region saw predominance of Keyser Cord-marked ceramics after AD 1400.

name: Upper Potomac
description: Geographic region where Keyser Cord-marked ceramics became predominant after AD 1400.

name: Luray Focus
description: Peoples who MacCord suggested left the Upper Potomac and Shenandoah Valleys to move to Cumberland, Maryland, and become the historically known Shawnee.

name: Shenandoah Valley
description: Valley region that Luray Focus peoples left to move upriver to Cumberland, Maryland.

name: Cumberland
description: Location in Maryland where Luray Focus peoples moved to become the historically known Shawnee.

name: Maryland
description: State where Cumberland is located, destination of the Luray Focus peoples.

name: Shawnee
description: Historically known group that MacCord suggested the Luray Focus peoples became after moving to Cumberland, Maryland.

name: Luray Complex
description: Archaeological culture suggested to be eastern Siouan in origin and perhaps related to the Manahoac, with sites possibly inhabited by an intrusive group from the west.

name: Siouan
description: Linguistic/cultural group that Wall suggested the Luray Complex peoples were related to, rather than Algonquian.

name: Algonquian
description: Linguistic/cultural group that Wall suggested the Luray Complex peoples were not related to.

name: Manahoac
description: Group that Wall suggested the Luray Complex peoples were perhaps related to.

name: Shepard
description: Earlier ceramic type from the region showing preference for final Z-twist cordage impressions.

name: Page
description: Earlier ceramic type from the region showing preference for final Z-twist cordage impressions.

name: Potomac Creek
description: Earlier ceramic type from the region showing preference for final Z-twist cordage impressions.

name: Monongahela Tradition
description: Archaeological tradition that Johnson determined the Luray Complex people were clearly not, despite some arguing superficial ceramic similarities.

name: Allegheny Mountain
description: Geographic region outside of which Monongahela Tradition ceramics are typically tempered with shell and have final Z-twist cordage impressions.

name: Susquehannock
description: Group that moved into the area inhabited by the Luray Complex peoples around AD 1600 to 1620.

name: Upper Potomac Valley
description: Valley region that the Luray Complex peoples may have abandoned, possibly due to Susquehannock movement into the area.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland peoples lived and where archaeological excavations have provided crucial information.

name: William and Mary Center for Archaeological Research
description: Archaeological research organization that teamed with American University to conduct excavations at the Fisher Site.

name: American University
description: University that teamed with WMCAR to conduct excavations at the Fisher Site in June 2002.

name: Fisher Site
description: American Indian village located on the Potomac River in Loudon County, Virginia, excavated in 2002 and previously tested in 1938 and 1981.

name: Loudon County
description: County in Virginia where the Fisher Site (44LD4) is located.

name: Virginia
description: State where Loudon County and the Fisher Site are located.

name: Richard Slattery
description: Archaeologist who first tested the Fisher Site in a limited fashion in 1938.

name: Hugh Stabler
description: Archaeologist who worked with Richard Slattery to first test the Fisher Site in 1938.

name: Howard MacCord
description: Archaeologist who tested the Fisher Site again in 1981 and suggested that Luray Focus peoples became the Shawnee.

name: William J. Hranicky
description: Archaeologist who worked with Howard MacCord to test the Fisher Site in 1981.

name: Shepard ware
description: Ceramic type that comprised the majority (n=340) of the 558 vessel fragments recovered from 21st century excavations at the Fisher Site.

name: Late Woodland Period
description: Cultural period associated with peoples of Northern Virginia and sites like Fisher where subsistence remains were recovered through flotation analysis.

name: AD 1400
description: Time period after which Keyser Cord-marked ceramics became predominant in the upper Potomac region.

name: AD 1600 to 1620
description: Time period when the Susquehannock moved into the area inhabited by the Luray Complex peoples.

name: Dent
description: Researcher who argued that Luray Complex sites were inhabited by an intrusive group from the west.

name: Wall
description: Researcher who suggested that Luray Complex peoples were eastern Siouan in origin and perhaps related to the Manahoac.

name: Johnson
description: Researcher who used cordage impression preferences to support arguments about intrusive groups and determined that Luray Complex people were not Monongahela Tradition.

name: Barber
description: Researcher who contributed to knowledge about Keyser vessel decoration being limited to lips and adjacent rim parts.

name: Walker
description: Researcher who, along with Miller, argued for superficial ceramic similarities between Luray Complex and Monongahela Tradition.

name: Miller
description: Researcher who, along with Walker, argued for superficial ceramic similarities between Luray Complex and Monongahela Tradition.

name: Pullins
description: Researcher who, along with Lewes, reported on the 2002 excavations at the Fisher Site including ceramic analysis and flotation results.

name: Lewes
description: Researcher who, along with Pullins, reported on the 2002 excavations at the Fisher Site including ceramic analysis and flotation results.

name: Slattery and Woodward
description: Authors who provided information about the Fisher Site's excavation history.

name: Beta-159908
description: An AMS radiocarbon assay yielding 590 ± 40 BP (cal2 σ AD 1296 to 1417) obtained from fragments of charred corn.

name: UGa-4470
description: An earlier radiocarbon assay obtained on deer bone dating to 1025 ± 70 BP (cal 2 σ AD 832 to 1186).

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex characterized by specific ceramic traditions and associated with sites in the Middle Potomac Valley.

name: Fisher site
description: An archaeological site with Montgomery Complex components that has yielded radiocarbon dates and deer remains indicating specific butchering patterns.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: An archaeological site where a more even distribution of deer elements indicates entire carcasses were brought to the site.

name: Winslow site
description: An archaeological site in Maryland within the Middle Potomac Valley assigned to the Montgomery Complex, featuring circular structures and a palisade.

name: Middle Potomac Valley
description: A geographic region in the Piedmont physiographic province where the Winslow site is located.

name: Piedmont physiographic province
description: A physiographic region where the Winslow site is located on expansive floodplains.

name: Maryland
description: The state where the Winslow site is located.

name: Richard J. Dent
description: Archaeologist who conducted recent excavations at the Winslow site and studied Montgomery Complex sites.

name: Shepard wares
description: A type of ceramic found at Montgomery Complex sites, particularly abundant at the Winslow site.

name: Beta-177862
description: An AMS radiocarbon assay from Feature 36 at the Winslow site yielding 580+40 BP (cal 2 σ AD 1300 to 1420).

name: Beta-177863
description: An AMS radiocarbon assay from Feature 37 at the Winslow site yielding 580+40 BP (cal 2 σ AD 1300 to 1420).

name: Feature 36
description: An archaeological feature at the Winslow site from which radiocarbon dating material was obtained.

name: Feature 37
description: An archaeological feature at the Winslow site from which radiocarbon dating material was obtained.

name: Late Woodland
description: A cultural period during which the Montgomery Complex and associated sites were occupied.

name: northern Virginia
description: A geographic region relevant to understanding Late Woodland occupation patterns studied through the Winslow site investigations.

name: Winslow site
description: Late Woodland village site where excavated structures suggest nuclear family organization, with evidence of plant cultivation including maize and beans, and rich faunal remains dominated by deer and fish.

name: Rosenstock site
description: Montgomery Focus site containing an arcing line of trash pits, keyhole structures interpreted as sweatlodges, and a plaza area, with radiocarbon dates ranging from AD 1000 to nearly AD 1600.

name: Montgomery Focus
description: Cultural complex associated with the Rosenstock site, characterized by specific village organization patterns including keyhole structures and arcing pit arrangements.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: Cultural period dated to approximately AD 1335-1400 based on radiocarbon dating from the Rosenstock site.

name: Shepard ware
description: Ceramic type that predominantly characterizes the artifact assemblage from the Rosenstock site.

name: keyhole structures
description: Semi-subterranean structures interpreted as sweatlodges found at the Rosenstock site.

name: Maryland State Archeologists' office
description: Archaeological organization that conducted excavations at the Rosenstock site in 1990-1992.

name: Rosenstock
description: An archaeological site with diverse faunal remains dominated by white-tailed deer, showing evidence of year-round occupation with intensive use of seasonal resources.

name: Montgomery Focus
description: A cultural complex or focus associated with archaeological sites in the region, with the Rosenstock site being one of the most diverse in terms of taxa.

name: Potomac Valley
description: A geographic region where archaeologists study the origins of the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: A cultural complex whose origins are debated, with theories suggesting either Coastal Plain incursion by Montgomery Complex people or origins from the Eastern Shore based on Piscataway oral history.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region proposed as one possible origin area for the Potomac Creek Complex through incursion by Montgomery Complex people.

name: Eastern Shore
description: A geographic region proposed as the origin of Piscataway chieftainship according to oral history recorded in 1660.

name: Piscataway
description: A Native American group whose oral history from 1660 indicates their chieftainship predecessor came from the Eastern Shore.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: A large, palisaded village site in Stafford County, Virginia, excavated between 1935-1940 and again in 1996, which partially defined the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Stafford County
description: A county in Virginia where the Potomac Creek site is located.

name: Virginia
description: The state where Stafford County and the Potomac Creek site are located.

name: WMCAR
description: An archaeological organization that conducted new excavations at the Potomac Creek site in November and December 1996.

name: T. Dale Stewart
description: A researcher from the Smithsonian Institution who undertook major excavations at the Potomac Creek site between 1935 and 1940.

name: Smithsonian Institution
description: The institution where T. Dale Stewart worked when conducting excavations at the Potomac Creek site.

name: Lower Potomac basin
description: A geographic region where the Potomac Creek Complex was previously thought to represent a foreign culture relocation.

name: Upper Susquehanna River
description: A geographic region where proto-Iroquoian cultures lived, from which the Potomac Creek Complex is now believed to be derived.

name: proto-Iroquoian cultures
description: Cultural groups that lived along the Upper Susquehanna River and are now believed to be the source from which the Potomac Creek Complex derived.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: A Late Woodland cultural complex in the Potomac River region that underwent significant transformations over time, with sites showing evidence of fortified villages, chiefly residences, and ossuary burials.

name: WMCAR
description: An organization that conducted archaeological investigations that uncovered critical new information about the Potomac Creek site chronology.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: A Late Woodland archaeological site that experienced three major occupational episodes from ca. AD 1300-1650, evolving from a fortified circular village to an ossuary burial location.

name: Accokeek Creek site
description: A major Potomac Creek Complex site on the Maryland side of the Potomac River that yielded a surprisingly early AMS date of ca. AD 1160.

name: Indian Town Site
description: The site where villagers from the Potomac Creek site relocated, which was visited by Captain John Smith in 1608.

name: Potomac River
description: A river that served as a geographic boundary, with the Accokeek Creek site located on its Maryland side.

name: John Smith
description: A historical figure who visited the Indian Town Site in 1608.

name: University of Michigan's Museum of Anthropology
description: The institution where artifacts from the Accokeek Creek site were curated for later archival archaeology research.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A cultural complex whose groups may have migrated from the Piedmont to the Coastal Plain as one scenario for the origins of the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Luray Complex
description: A cultural complex that may have forced Montgomery Complex groups out of the Piedmont region.

name: Mason Island groups
description: Cultural groups that may have forced Montgomery Complex groups out of the Piedmont region.

name: Piedmont
description: A geographic region from which Montgomery Complex groups may have migrated to the Coastal Plain.

name: Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region to which Montgomery Complex groups may have migrated from the Piedmont.

name: Stage I
description: The first occupational episode at Potomac Creek site when outsiders established a fortified circular village settlement.

name: Stage II
description: The second occupational episode when inhabitants became well integrated into the regional landscape and decreased fortifications.

name: Stage III
description: The third occupational episode when the Potomac Creek site was abandoned as a residential community and transformed into an ossuary burial location.

name: Contact Era
description: The period when Europeans first encountered chiefdom-level societies in the region.

name: Eastern Shore
description: Geographic region from which migration to Potomac Creek sites may have originated, according to oral tradition and archaeological evidence of ossuary burials.

name: Piscataway
description: Native American group that held oral tradition about east to west migration from the Eastern Shore.

name: Potomac Creek sites
description: Archaeological sites that share cultural characteristics including ossuary burials and are part of the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Potomac Creek groups
description: Cultural groups associated with the Potomac Creek Complex, possibly derived from the Owasco according to some theories.

name: Owasco
description: Northern cultural group from which Potomac Creek groups may have been derived, according to Blanton and colleagues.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: Earlier Late Woodland cultural complex characterized by individual flexed interments, possibly the same as or ancestral to the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Potomac Creek Complex
description: Late Woodland cultural complex characterized by ossuary burial protocol, possibly developed from or contemporaneous with Montgomery Complex.

name: Dent
description: Archaeologist who, along with Jirikowic, favored the Eastern Shore hypothesis for Potomac Creek Complex origins based on linguistic evidence and oral tradition.

name: Jirikowic
description: Archaeologist who, along with Dent, favored the Eastern Shore hypothesis for Potomac Creek Complex origins.

name: ossuary burial
description: Burial practice involving collective interment, characteristic of the Potomac Creek Complex and found on both the Eastern Shore and at Potomac Creek sites.

name: individual flexed interments
description: Burial practice characteristic of the Montgomery Complex, representing a different tradition from ossuary burials.

name: Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where Potomac Creek Complex territory was located and where Montgomery Complex groups may have migrated.

name: Hartwell site
description: Late Woodland Potomac Creek Complex site in Fairfax County consisting of two adjacent stratified shell middens, excavated in the late 1980s and early 1990s.

name: Fairfax County
description: County in Northern Virginia where the Hartwell site and Lee Road 2 site are located.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland Potomac Creek Complex sites like Hartwell are located.

name: Occoquan River
description: River near whose mouth the Hartwell site is located.

name: shell middens
description: Archaeological features at the Hartwell site consisting of two adjacent and stratified deposits, one assigned to Potomac Creek phase and one to Rappahannock phase.

name: Potomac Creek phase
description: Cultural phase associated with the upper midden at Hartwell site, dated to ca. AD 1360.

name: AD 1360
description: Radiocarbon date obtained from the upper midden at Hartwell site associated with the Potomac Creek phase.

name: Rappahannock phase
description: Earlier cultural phase associated with the lower, partly submerged midden at the Hartwell site.

name: M. Johnson
description: Archaeologist who suggested that further analyses of the Hartwell site collection could help clarify the origin of the Potomac Creek Complex.

name: Potomac Creek rim motifs
description: Decorative patterns on ceramic vessels from Potomac Creek sites, examined in detail by George Svokos in his master's thesis.

name: George Svokos
description: Researcher who conducted a master's thesis study of Potomac Creek rim motifs, identifying 23 distinct motifs as variations on a shared theme.

name: ceramic vessels
description: Pottery containers that bore rim motifs studied by Svokos, made by select community members.

name: Lee Road 2 site
description: The westernmost site in Fairfax County with Potomac Creek pottery, where small sherds were recovered through water screening.

name: Potomac Creek pottery
description: Ceramic artifacts characteristic of the Potomac Creek Complex, found at sites including Lee Road 2, often in small sherds that require careful recovery methods.

name: Mike Johnson
description: Archaeologist who argued for more careful consideration of recovery methods, noting that Potomac Creek pottery may be more common than previously thought.

name: Blanton
description: Archaeologist who, along with colleagues, argued that Potomac Creek groups were derived from the Owasco through northern migration.

name: Keyser Farm site
description: Archaeological site in Page County, Virginia where as many as 200 people are estimated to have lived. Originally excavated in 1940, then re-excavated from 2003-2007 using more intensive archaeological recovery techniques including flotation.

name: Page County
description: County in Virginia where the Keyser Farm site is located.

name: Virginia
description: State where archaeological investigations and sites discussed in the text are located.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Region of Virginia where significant recent archaeological investigations have taken place.

name: Carl Manson
description: Archaeologist who conducted original excavations at the Keyser Farm site in 1940.

name: Howard MacCord
description: Archaeologist who worked with Carl Manson on the original 1940 excavations at the Keyser Farm site.

name: U.S. Forest Service
description: Federal agency that conducted recent investigations at the Keyser Farm site from 2003-2007.

name: Virginia Department of Historic Resources
description: State agency that participated in conducting recent investigations at the Keyser Farm site from 2003-2007.

name: Archaeological Society of Virginia
description: Organization that participated in conducting recent investigations at the Keyser Farm site from 2003-2007.

name: Shepard wares
description: Ceramic artifacts found in pit features at Keyser Farm, associated with the Montgomery Complex.

name: Page wares
description: Ceramic artifacts found in pit features at Keyser Farm, associated with the Mason Island Complex.

name: Keyser wares
description: Ceramic artifacts found in pit features at Keyser Farm, associated with the Luray Complex.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: Archaeological complex associated with Shepard wares found at the Keyser Farm site.

name: Mason Island Complex
description: Archaeological complex associated with Page wares found at the Keyser Farm site.

name: Luray Complex
description: Archaeological complex associated with Keyser wares found at the Keyser Farm site.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period during which the Keyser Farm site was occupied.

name: Potomac-Shenandoah
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland occupation is being studied through archaeological investigations.

name: Keyser Farm site
description: Late Woodland period archaeological site known for intensive deer hide production, bone tools, shell disk beads, and evidence of trade relationships with both Iroquoian groups to the north and Coastal Plain groups.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Bernard Means
description: Archaeologist who photographed excavations and flotation work at the Keyser Farm site in 2007.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Barber
description: Archaeologist who conducted analyses of the Keyser Farm site and proposed theories about deer hide overproduction, trade relationships, and symbolic meaning of artifacts.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Fall Line
description: Geographic feature that marks a boundary in Virginia's landscape, referenced in relation to Late Woodland period settlements.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Cultural period in Virginia's prehistory characterized by village settlements and intensive subsistence practices.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: deer metatarsal beamers
description: Specialized bone tools made from deer metatarsals, used for hide processing and found in large quantities at the Keyser Farm site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Iroquoian
description: Cultural group to the north that may have traded with the occupants of Keyser Farm site for deer hides.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Coastal Plain groups
description: Native American groups living along Virginia's Coastal Plain who traded marine shell materials and Potomac Creek vessels with interior groups.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: shell disk beads
description: Small beads (3-5 mm diameter) manufactured from marine bivalves, used as wealth storage, status symbols, and worn as necklaces or sewn into clothing.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: native copper beads
description: Beads manufactured from native copper found at the Keyser Farm site.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Atlantic Coast
description: Coastal region that was the source of marine bivalves used to manufacture shell beads at inland sites.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Potomac Creek vessels
description: Ceramic vessels produced by Coastal Plain groups and traded into the Valley and Ridge province, possibly having symbolic value.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Valley and Ridge province
description: Geographic region of Virginia where Potomac Creek vessels were traded from Coastal Plain groups.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Powhatan
description: Historical figure whose mantle is referenced as an example of shell beads being used as status symbols in clothing.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: bone beads and pendants
description: Ornamental objects that served as visual tools reflecting social nuances such as rank, status, clan membership, or wealth.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 9.5. Excavations at the Keyser Farm site(44PA1) in 2007. Photograph by Bernard Means.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A geographic region of Virginia that contains archaeological sites with Late Woodland period radiocarbon dates and ceramic sequences.

name: Late Woodland
description: An archaeological period in Northern Virginia characterized by ceramic typologies and intensive village occupations.

name: Shepard-to-Page-to-Keyser sequence
description: A ceramic typological sequence that forms the chronological framework for the Late Woodland period in Northern Virginia.

name: Kerns site
description: An archaeological site in Clarke County that provided the first radiocarbon date obtained for the entire state of Virginia in 1957.

name: Clarke County
description: A county in Northern Virginia that contains the Kerns archaeological site.

name: Virginia
description: The state where over 800 radiocarbon dates have been obtained from archaeological contexts since 1957.

name: Keith Egloff
description: A former member of the VDHR who originally created and maintained a database of Virginia's radiocarbon dates.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Chronological Framework forNorthern Virginia’s Late Woodland

name: VDHR
description: An organization where Keith Egloff was formerly employed.

name: Means
description: A researcher who assessed all of Virginia's radiocarbon dates in 2008 and 2009 with support of a COVA research grant.

name: Council of Virginia Archaeologists
description: An organization that provided research grant support for assessing Virginia's radiocarbon dates.

name: Calib Radiocarbon Calibration Program
description: A program used to recalibrate radiocarbon assays presented in the research.

name: Stuiver and Reimer
description: The creators of the Calib Radiocarbon Calibration Program in 1993 and 2005.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Chronological Framework forNorthern Virginia’s Late Woodland

name: Potomac Creek site
description: One of the best dated archaeological sites within Northern Virginia, largely due to AMS dating of recently collected material.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Chronological Framework forNorthern Virginia’s Late Woodland

name: Keyser Farm site
description: One of the best dated archaeological sites within Northern Virginia, with new AMS dating assays that are detailed in the next section.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/The Chronological Framework forNorthern Virginia’s Late Woodland

name: Shepard ware
description: A ceramic type that was traditionally thought to follow a chronological progression to Page and then Keyser wares, but recent 15th century dates challenge this linear sequence.

name: Page ware
description: A ceramic type that was traditionally thought to follow Shepard ware chronologically but precede Keyser ware, though recent dating suggests a more complex relationship.

name: Keyser ware
description: A ceramic type that was traditionally thought to be the latest in a chronological sequence after Shepard and Page wares, but recent 15th century dates suggest contemporaneous use.

name: Potomac Creek ware
description: Ceramic types associated with the Potomac Creek people that represent new ceramic technology possibly resulting from interaction networks rather than migration.

name: Potomac Creek people
description: A cultural group linked to ceramic technology changes through interaction networks with their neighbors rather than through migration.

name: Potomac-Shenandoah drainage system
description: A geographic region where major late Woodland research has been conducted on artifacts from various archaeological sites.

name: Potomac Valley
description: A geographic region where ceramic variation has been studied in relation to both social and technological factors underlying pottery production.

name: Potomac River basin
description: A geographic region where cordage twist patterns have been extensively studied, showing geographic stability for 2,200 years of the Woodland period.

name: Valley and Ridge province
description: A physiographic province within the Potomac River Basin that showed greater variation in cordage patterns during the last 400 years of the Late Woodland period.

name: Piedmont province
description: A physiographic province within the Potomac River Basin that showed greater variation in cordage patterns during the last 400 years of the Late Woodland period.

name: Northern Virginia
description: A geographic region where Late Woodland settlement patterns require further research and strengthening of understanding.

name: Middle and Upper Drainages of Opequon
description: An area investigated as representing fragments of larger settlement systems focused elsewhere along the Shenandoah River or Potomac Drainages.

name: Shenandoah River
description: A river drainage area that may have been the primary focus of larger settlement systems, of which the Opequon drainage represents fragments.

name: Potomac Drainages
description: River drainage areas that may have been the primary focus of larger settlement systems, of which the Opequon drainage represents fragments.

name: Late Woodland period
description: An archaeological time period during which ceramic technology, settlement patterns, and cordage patterns have been extensively studied, with the last 400 years showing greater variation.

name: Middle Woodland period
description: An archaeological time period covered in research publications alongside the Late Woodland period.

name: Woodland period
description: An archaeological time period spanning at least 2,200 years, during which cordage twist patterns showed geographic stability for most of the duration.

name: 15th century
description: A time period for which recent dates have been obtained for Shepard, Page, and Keyser ceramic groups, indicating their complex and dynamic contemporaneous relationship.

name: Late Woodland Period
description: A chronological period in Virginia archaeology during which subsistence practices, including the adoption and intensification of cultivated crops, are being studied.

name: Montgomery Complex
description: A cultural complex whose people exploited a wide variety of taxa from all available habitats and focused on hunting deer of maximum meat-bearing age and seasonally abundant resources.

name: Shephard Site
description: An archaeological site associated with the Montgomery Complex that was analyzed for faunal remains.

name: Winslow Site
description: An archaeological site associated with the Montgomery Complex that was analyzed for faunal remains and later excavated with flotation recovery methods.

name: Rosenstock Site
description: An archaeological site associated with the Montgomery Complex that was analyzed for faunal remains.

name: Luray Focus
description: A cultural complex whose inhabitants focused their animal resource use on fewer taxa, particularly those characterized as garden pests, suggesting increased cultivation and 'garden-hunting' practices.

name: Shepard Barracks Site
description: An archaeological site associated with the Luray Focus that was analyzed for faunal remains.

description: An archaeological site associated with the Luray Focus that was analyzed for faunal remains.

description: A researcher who conducted analyses of faunal remains from Montgomery Complex and Luray Focus sites in 1994, 2005, and 2006, contributing to understanding of subsistence differences between cultural complexes.

name: Linares
description: A researcher referenced in connection with the concept of 'garden-hunting' in 1976.

name: Luray Focus
description: Cultural group that practiced garden-hunting and shows evidence of dependence on domesticated crops during the Late Woodland period in Northern Virginia.

name: Late Woodland
description: Cultural period beginning around AD 900 with the presumed adoption of maize horticulture, characterized by increased reliance on domesticated crops.

name: Northern Virginia
description: Geographic region where Late Woodland peoples practiced horticulture and where radiocarbon dating has been underutilized for archaeological sites.

name: American Southwest

name: Virginia
description: State where early direct dates on maize have been found and where archaeobotanical database projects have been conducted.

name: maize
description: Cultivated crop whose adoption marked the beginning of the Late Woodland period around AD 900, with archaeological visibility being more recent than previously thought.

name: common bean

name: Speth
description: Researcher who, along with Scott, observed adaptations to increased horticultural commitment in the American Southwest.

name: Scott

name: Watson

name: Kennedy

name: Chapter 10
description: A chapter reference in what appears to be a book about the archaeology of Virginia's first peoples.

name: Potomac Creek site
description: An archaeological site investigated nearly twenty years ago that represents a conceptual shift in studying late prehistory of Virginia's Coastal Plain. The site includes evidence of a large, compact 14th century settlement with defensive features and later developments.

name: Virginia's Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region of Virginia where late prehistoric archaeological investigations have been conducted.

name: Council of Virginia Archaeologists
description: An archaeological organization that published volumes on the Woodland period in the early 1990s.

name: Woodland period
description: A cultural period that was the subject of COVA publications and archaeological study in Virginia.

name: Patawomeke community
description: An Algonquian-speaking people who established settlements along the Potomac River and figured prominently in early colonial history as a counterweight to the Powhatan paramountcy.

name: Chesapeake
description: A geographic region where the Patawomeke and other communities lived, central to early colonial history and archaeological research.

name: 14th century AD
description: The time period when 250 to 300 immigrants constructed a large, compact settlement at Potomac Creek with defensive features.

name: 15th century
description: The period when the Potomac Creek immigrants created a flourishing Tidewater culture and the village lost its defensive orientation.

name: Owasco communities
description: Communities of the upper Susquehanna region that may have been the origin of the immigrants who settled at Potomac Creek.

name: upper Susquehanna
description: A geographic region where Owasco communities lived, possibly the origin of immigrants to Potomac Creek.

name: Tidewater culture
description: A cultural development created by immigrants at Potomac Creek during the 15th century.

name: Potomac
description: A river along which the Patawomekes established settlements and conducted trade.

name: Patawomeck
description: A settlement meaning 'trading place' that was central to Patawomeke community and served as their political center.

name: Powhatan paramountcy
description: A political entity that exercised authority across coastal Virginia during the early colonial era, counterbalanced by the Patawomekes.

name: coastal Virginia
description: A geographic region where the Powhatan paramountcy exercised authority during the early colonial era.

name: early colonial era
description: The historical period during which the Powhatan paramountcy exercised authority and the Patawomekes figured prominently in Chesapeake history.

name: Precontact
description: The period before European contact, characterized by diverse cultural settings and developments that linked to Colonial-era events.

name: Eastern Virginia
description: A geographic region whose Precontact archaeology has been characterized as a parochial backwater until recent decades.

name: Colonial-era
description: The historical period following European contact, linked to earlier Precontact developments through archaeological research.

name: Virginia
description: A state where avocationalists have played a prominent role in the study of native archaeological sites.

name: Maryland
description: A state where avocationalists have also been involved in archaeological studies, similar to Virginia.

description: Geographic regions that have influenced archaeological developments.

name: Precontact Period
