name: Leone
description: Prominent figure in historical archaeology with publications in 1995 and 2005, associated with urban archaeological programs.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Monacan
description: Native American cultural group whose burial mound was excavated by Jefferson in an early archaeological study.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Hantman
description: Archaeological researcher who co-authored work about Jefferson's excavation of the Monacan burial mound in 1993.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Dunham
description: Archaeological researcher who co-authored work about Jefferson's excavation of the Monacan burial mound in 1993.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Rountree
description: Researcher who co-authored innovative studies of native social complexity combining archaeology and ethnohistory in 2002.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Turner
description: Researcher who co-authored studies of native social complexity in 2002 and conducted settlement pattern studies in 1976.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Jamestown
description: Early English colonial settlement site where archaeological excavations have been conducted, representing important historical archaeology work.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Kelso
description: Archaeologist who conducted excavations at the Jamestown colonial settlement, with work published in 2006.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region that benefits from pioneering archaeological research programs and is prominent in historical archaeology, though this often overshadows the region's Native American past.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Brown
description: Researcher associated with archaeological research programs at outdoor museums, co-authoring work in 2006.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Edwards
description: Researcher associated with archaeological research programs at outdoor museums, co-authoring work in 2006.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Miller
description: Researcher associated with archaeological research programs at outdoor museums, with work published in 2007.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Cressey
description: Researcher associated with urban archaeological programs, co-authoring work in 2007.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Vinton
description: Researcher associated with urban archaeological programs, co-authoring work in 2007.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Custer
description: Researcher who focused on cultural-ecological frameworks and neoevolutionary models in 1989 to characterize the archaeological record of native societies.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Gardner
description: Researcher who focused on cultural-ecological frameworks and neoevolutionary models in 1982 to characterize the archaeological record of native societies.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Potter
description: Researcher who conducted settlement pattern studies in 1982 during the era when such studies took center stage in archaeological research.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Woodland period
description: Cultural chronological period that was the focus of settlement pattern studies, emphasizing productive estuarine and riverine environments.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Holocene
description: Geological time period during which environmental changes occurred that influenced cultural developments understood as adaptations by native societies.
source_section: The-Archaeology-of-Virginias-First-Peoples/Figure 10.1. Archaeological Sites from Virginia andMaryland Mentioned in Overview.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period dating to 1200-500 BC characterized by the adoption of pottery and increasing sedentism among hunter-foragers.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region where Early Woodland research and archaeological studies have been conducted, including investigations of ceramics and settlement patterns.

name: Klein
description: Researcher who has contributed to Early Woodland studies including interpretations of ceramics, settlement organization, and floodplain geoarchaeology.

name: Custer
description: Scholar who developed the fusion-fission settlement model for Early Woodland research in the Chesapeake.

name: Klein and Klatka
description: Researchers who studied the connection between increasing population, sedentism, and resource exploitation during the Early Woodland period.

name: Turner
description: Scholar who contributed to research on resource exploitation in localized areas during the Early Woodland period.

name: Mouer
description: Researcher who studied the Transitional period and development of storage and container technologies in Early Woodland base camps.

name: Transitional period
description: Period during the Early Woodland when hunter-foragers developed new storage and container technologies within semisedentary base camps.

name: Marcey Creek pottery
description: Early Woodland pottery made from clay slabs molded by hand into trough or bowl shapes, tempered with crushed steatite and mimicking soapstone vessel forms.

name: Dent
description: Researcher who studied Early Woodland experimental pottery wares and vessel construction techniques.

name: Egloff
description: Scholar who contributed to research on Early Woodland ceramics and pottery adoption by hunter-foragers.

name: Egloff and Potter
description: Research team that studied Early Woodland pottery construction and vessel forms.

name: Late Archaic
description: Cultural period preceding the Early Woodland that featured soapstone vessel exchange networks.

name: soapstone vessels
description: Stone containers that moved through Late Archaic exchange networks and whose shapes were mimicked by Early Woodland ceramic vessels.

name: Blanton
description: Researcher who studied the limited access to soapstone bowls in the Chesapeake region.

name: northeastern Maryland
description: Geographic area with a concentration of Late Archaic and Early Woodland soapstone vessels bearing elaborate decorations.

name: Pennsylvania Uplands
description: Geographic region representing a distinct social sphere separate from the Chesapeake area.

name: Shaffer
description: Researcher who identified northeastern Maryland as a potential gateway zone between the Chesapeake and Pennsylvania Uplands cultural spheres.

name: Klein
description: Researcher who presented evidence about Chesapeake soapstone vessels and their use for indirect cooking and ceremonial purposes, and developed models for ceramic adoption in the Chesapeake region.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region where soapstone vessels were used and where Early Woodland ceramic traditions developed.

name: Early Woodland
description: Cultural period characterized by the adoption of ceramics and development of container technologies in the Chesapeake region.

name: Sassaman
description: Researcher who provided arguments about specialized soapstone vessel use that Klein drew upon in his model.

name: Marcey Creek vessels
description: Early ceramic vessels that moved across the Chesapeake and appear to represent attempts to extend ceremonial practices that originated with soapstone bowls.

name: soapstone vessels
description: Stone containers used for indirect cooking, stone boiling, and serving meats and ritual teas, associated with ceremonial spheres and exchange networks.

name: Mouer
description: Researcher who documented high frequencies of Marcey Creek ceramics on a limited number of sites.

name: Hantman
description: Researcher who, along with Gold, studied the presence of individuals or communities who controlled access to soapstone.

name: Gold
description: Researcher who, along with Hantman, studied the presence of individuals or communities who controlled access to soapstone.

name: Accokeek Creek
description: Coil constructed, quartz- and sand-tempered ceramic wares that made vessels more widely available and were found at intensive base camps with residential architecture.

name: Stewart
description: Researcher who documented that Accokeek Creek vessels were generally limited to intensively reused macrogroup base camps in ecologically attractive settings.

name: McLearen
description: Researcher who documented some of the earliest evidence of residential architecture in the region.

name: Virginia's Eastern Shore
description: Coastal region of Virginia where shell tempered ceramics have been found to have a deeper history than traditionally thought.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period traditionally thought to be when shell tempered ceramics were first produced, though recent evidence suggests they have deeper origins.

name: Waterlily
description: Shell tempered ceramic type found at Savage Neck Shell Midden dating to 1100 BC and also recovered from coastal North Carolina.

name: Mockley
description: Shell tempered ceramic type found at Savage Neck Shell Midden dating to 1100 BC and reemerging around AD 700.

name: Savage Neck Shell Midden
description: Archaeological site on Virginia's Eastern Shore where shell tempered Waterlily and Mockley sherds were recovered, with radiocarbon dates indicating shell tempered wares were produced by 1100 BC.

name: Rick
description: Lead researcher who provided radiocarbon dates of shell tempered ceramics from Savage Neck Shell Midden indicating production by 1100 BC.

name: Currituck
description: Shell tempered ceramic type recovered from coastal northern North Carolina with ages comparable to Waterlily Plain.

name: North Carolina
description: State where early shell tempered ceramics were found and where shell tempering may have emerged as early as 800 BC.

name: Painter
description: Researcher who recovered Waterlily Plain and Currituck sherds from a site in coastal northern North Carolina.

name: Herbert
description: Researcher who contends that shell tempering may have emerged in northern North Carolina as early as 800 BC before disappearing and reemerging as Mockley ware around AD 700.

name: Algonquian speaking communities
description: Linguistic group whose inmigration from the north was previously thought to explain the spread of Mockley ceramics beginning around AD 200.

name: Custer
description: Researcher who proposed that the spread of Mockley ceramics beginning around AD 200 was the result of an inmigration of Algonquian speaking communities from the north.

name: Early Woodland
description: A cultural period in Virginia archaeology characterized by hunter-forager aggregation sites with seasonal occupations and expanded social networks.

name: Late Archaic
description: A cultural period preceding the Early Woodland, during which a shift to riverine adaptations occurred and changes in subsistence, technology, site size, and site density were triggered.

name: James River Piedmont
description: A geographic region containing large, densely occupied settlements that provided data for understanding Early Woodland settlement patterns.

name: Fall Line zone
description: A geographic zone in the Piedmont where sites exhibit extensive artifact concentrations, platform hearths, and pit features.

name: platform hearths
description: Large hearth features used for seasonal food preparation events found at Piedmont sites near the Fall Line zone.

name: grooved axes
description: Stone tools found in artifact assemblages tied to land clearing activities.

name: steatite vessels
description: Stone vessels found in artifact assemblages tied to exchange activities.

name: stone gorgets
description: Stone ornaments found in artifact assemblages tied to expressions of identity.

name: Chesapeake
description: A region containing several concentrations of hunter-forager aggregation sites dating to the Late Archaic and Early Woodland periods.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: A wetland area where an almost continuous concentration of Late Archaic and Early Woodland sites has been identified at its western rim and edge.

name: Delmarva Peninsula
description: A geographic region where hunter-forager aggregation sites have been identified, including the Clyde Farm site.

name: Clyde Farm site
description: An archaeological site on the Delmarva Peninsula with features dated c. 1000 BC comprising a household cluster including a rectangular pit house, external storage pit and hearth, and lithic tool production area.

name: rectangular pit house
description: A dwelling structure identified at the Clyde Farm site as part of a household cluster dated c. 1000 BC.

name: bola weights
description: Stone tools found in dense concentrations at Dismal Swamp sites, part of groundstone tool assemblages.

name: roller-type pestles
description: Groundstone tools found in dense concentrations at Dismal Swamp sites.

name: bannerstones
description: Elaborately crafted weights for spear-throwers that may represent expressions of assertive identities by distinct individuals in hunter-forager aggregation contexts.

name: Atlantic Coast
description: A geographic region where places of hunter-forager aggregation with expressions of assertive identities have been noted.

name: Chesapeake
description: A region where Early Woodland peoples established locations for seasonal aggregation, feasting, and social gatherings, characterized by evidence of specialized containers and food preparation facilities.

name: Early Woodland period
description: A chronological period during which people in the Chesapeake region engaged in seasonal aggregation, feasting, social integration, and status competition at persistent places.

name: Dismal Swamp
description: A location identified as requiring more intensive fieldwork to understand social integration and status competition during the Early Woodland period.

name: Delmarva Peninsula
description: A location identified as requiring more intensive fieldwork to understand social integration and status competition during the Early Woodland period.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period in the Chesapeake region dating from 500 BC to AD 900, characterized by technological homogenization and the spread of shell-tempered Mockley ceramics.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period preceding the Middle Woodland, noted for having poorly understood aggregation sites.

name: Chesapeake
description: Regional geographic area where Middle Woodland archaeological developments occurred, including population movements and ceremonial centers.

name: Mockley ceramics
description: Shell-tempered pottery that spread rapidly across the coastal Chesapeake during the Middle Woodland period, first appearing on Maryland's Eastern Shore.

name: Outer Coastal Plain
description: Geographic region where settlement shifts occurred toward estuarine zones during the Middle Woodland period, containing sites with large shell middens.

name: James River
description: River near whose mouth excavations revealed large burial grounds, deep storage pits, and sedentary communities from the Middle Woodland period.

name: Maryland
description: State where dramatic increases in site numbers occurred during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Virginia
description: State where dramatic increases in site numbers occurred during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Maryland's Eastern Shore
description: Geographic region where Mockley ceramics first appeared before spreading throughout the Chesapeake.

name: Algonquian-speaking populations
description: Cultural and linguistic group believed to be ancestral to populations present at European contact, associated with the spread of Mockley ceramics.

name: Klein
description: Researcher who developed ceramic seriation techniques and regression equations for dating Middle Woodland through Contact period assemblages in Virginia using ceramic attributes.

name: Braun
description: Developer of the ceramic engineering model that Klein drew upon for ceramic analysis.

name: Virginia
description: State where radiocarbon dated features were used to develop ceramic dating regression equations.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period for which ceramic seriation and dating techniques have been developed.

name: Contact period
description: Archaeological period that followed the Middle Woodland, included in ceramic dating assemblages.

name: Richmond
description: Researcher who conducted a 2016 study of ceramics from the Kiskiak site examining connections between subsistence patterns and drought periods.

name: Kiskiak site
description: Archaeological site located along the York River where ceramic studies have been conducted to examine subsistence pattern changes.

name: York River
description: River where the Kiskiak archaeological site is located.

name: Binford
description: Developer of the collector model in 1980 that was later modified for Middle Woodland settlement patterns.

name: Blanton
description: Researcher who proposed a fusion-fission settlement pattern model centered on macroband base camps in 1992.

name: Potter
description: Researcher who conducted a 1993 survey of Middle and Late Woodland settlements on Virginia's Northern Neck.

name: Late Woodland
description: Archaeological period studied alongside Middle Woodland in settlement pattern research on Virginia's Northern Neck.

name: Northern Neck
description: Geographic region in Virginia where detailed Middle and Late Woodland settlement studies have been conducted.

name: Boathouse Pond
description: Large shell midden site that appeared after AD 550 and served as a hunter-forager aggregation location for several centuries.

name: Delmarva Adena
description: Cultural phenomenon from 500 BC to AD 1 involving sites on the Delmarva Peninsula with Midwestern Adena trade items and elaborate mortuary rituals.

name: Delmarva Peninsula
description: Geographic region where sites dating to 500 BC to AD 1 contain trade items from the Midwestern Adena culture.

name: Midwestern Adena
description: Cultural group from the Midwest whose trade items and ceremonial practices appeared at sites on the Delmarva Peninsula.

name: Custer
description: Researcher who studied the Delmarva Adena phenomenon and proposed models for exchange networks and social ranking.

name: Stewart
description: Researcher who developed a model of Middle Atlantic exchange networks including focused networks for obtaining exotic goods.

name: tubular pipes
description: Distinctive Adena artifacts found at Delmarva Peninsula sites as part of trade networks.

name: copper beads
description: Distinctive Adena artifacts found at Delmarva Peninsula sites as trade items.

name: copper gorgets
description: Distinctive Adena artifacts found at Delmarva Peninsula sites as trade items.

name: Flint Ridge chalcedony bifaces
description: Distinctive Adena stone tools found at Delmarva Peninsula sites, made from specific stone material.

name: Pig Point site
description: Archaeological site along the Patuxent River where Midwestern artifacts were found ritually shattered and intermingled ceremonially.

name: Patuxent River
description: River where the Pig Point archaeological site is located.

name: Luckenbach
description: Researcher who studied the ritual killing and shattering of artifacts at the Pig Point site in 2013.

name: Ohio Valley
description: Geographic region where groups engaged in ritualized exchange with Chesapeake groups during the Delmarva Adena phenomenon.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region where groups engaged in exchange with Ohio Valley groups during the Delmarva Adena phenomenon.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where focused exchange networks arose sporadically according to Stewart's model.

name: Abbott zoned-incised pottery
description: Distinctive Middle Woodland ceramic type with elaborate combinations of incised lines unlike other regional ceramics, used in ceremonial feasting contexts.

name: Abbott Farm site
description: Archaeological site in New Jersey containing substantial numbers of Abbott zoned-incised pottery from Middle Woodland deposits.

name: New Jersey
description: State where the Abbott Farm site is located.

name: Maycock's Point site
description: Archaeological site on the James River containing Abbott zoned-incised pottery in shell middens dating from AD 200 to 900, with evidence of fish consumption and possibly year-round occupation.

name: James River
description: River where the Maycock's Point site is located.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Cultural period during which Abbott ceramics were produced and used, and when Proto-Algonquian populations may have expanded into the Middle Atlantic region.

name: Delaware Valley
description: Region located 400 km north of Maycock's Point, possibly connected through trade relationships.

name: Mockley ceramics
description: Ceramic type that dominates the assemblage at Maycock's Point, distinct in size and shape from Abbott vessels.

name: Proto-Algonquian
description: Unified linguistic community that existed during the Middle Woodland period and expanded from southern Ontario between 500 BC and AD 900, with traditional social structures including large villages, totemic clans, ranked lineages, and hereditary chiefs.

name: southern Ontario
description: Region where Proto-Algonquians initially developed according to linguistic evidence.

name: Middle Atlantic region
description: Geographic region into which Proto-Algonquian populations moved during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Chesapeake
description: Region into which Proto-Algonquian populations moved during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Northeast
description: Geographic region where items like smoking pipes, earthworks, bows, and arrows became prominent during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Algonquian languages
description: Language family sharing cognate terms and protowords that provide evidence for shared cultural practices and social structures.

name: Fiedel
description: Researcher who developed a reconstruction of Proto-Algonquian and uses glottochronology to study migration patterns.

name: Proto-Algonquian
description: An ancient language reconstructed by Fiedel in linguistic analysis.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region where stone tool technologies, pottery styles, and mortuary practices crossed social and linguistic boundaries during the Contact period.

name: Contact period
description: Historical period when stone tool technologies, pottery styles, and mortuary practices crossed social and linguistic boundaries in the Chesapeake.

name: Cobb
description: Scholar cited regarding processual archaeologists' distaste for migration as an explanatory tool (2005).

name: Kipp Island phase
description: Culture-historic taxa used as direct correlates of ethnic or language groups in the Middle Atlantic.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Geographic region where culture-historic taxa like Kipp Island phase are used to correlate with ethnic or language groups.

name: Hart
description: Darwinian archaeologist who highlighted problems of essentialism in culture-historic taxa (with Brumbach, 2003).

name: Brumbach
description: Darwinian archaeologist who highlighted problems of essentialism in culture-historic taxa (with Hart, 2003).

name: Campbell
description: Historical linguist who doubts the efficacy of glottochronology's methodological and analytical principles (2004).

name: Bellwood
description: Researcher who uses glottochronology in recent studies of migration (with Renfrew, 2002).

name: Renfrew
description: Researcher who uses glottochronology in recent studies of migration (with Bellwood, 2002).

name: Brown
description: Researcher studying the spread of domesticates using glottochronology (2006).

name: Mockley spread
description: Archaeological phenomenon occurring around AD 200 in the Chesapeake region, followed by the appearance of ceremonial centers.

name: Stewart
description: Researcher who studied ceremonial centers that appeared after the Mockley spread (1998b).

name: Gallivan
description: Researcher who studied earthwork enclosures that appeared after the Mockley spread (2007).

name: Woodard
description: Researcher who studied chiefly lineages that appeared after the Mockley spread (2008).

name: Eastern Woodlands
description: Geographic region where archaeologists are developing detailed late prehistoric sequences and where migration is emerging as a viable alternative explanation for culture change.

name: Snow
description: Researcher who noted migration as a viable alternative to gradualist assumptions about culture change in the Eastern Woodlands (1995).

name: Dent
description: Researcher developing nuanced models of population movements in the Chesapeake using ethnohistorical analysis (2005).

name: Precontact Eastern Woodlands
description: Geographic and temporal context where studies draw on migration, conflict, and long-range interaction to explain culture change.

name: Nassaney
description: Researcher studying migration, conflict, and long-range interaction in the Precontact Eastern Woodlands (with Sassaman, 1995).

name: Sassaman
description: Researcher studying migration, conflict, and long-range interaction in the Precontact Eastern Woodlands (with Nassaney, 1995).

name: Island Field site
description: An archaeological site near the Delaware Bay where excavations beginning in 1968 identified several hundred burials dating from AD 410 to 1180, with sections incorporated into an on-site museum.

name: Ramp 3 site
description: An archaeological site in Washington, DC containing features with antler combs, antler disks, perforated shark teeth, and other materials associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: Chesapeake
description: A region in the Middle Atlantic associated with Middle Woodland archaeological sites and communities, later surrounded by Townsend complex sites.

name: Delaware Bay
description: A bay near which the Island Field archaeological site is located.

name: Washington, DC
description: Location of the Ramp 3 archaeological site.

name: Nanticoke
description: A tribal group whose leadership protested the public display of human remains at the Island Field site museum.

name: Kipp Island phase
description: A phase of the Point Peninsula complex (AD 500–800) characterized by specific ceramics, grave good assemblages, and mortuary patterns, centered in New York and Ontario.

name: Point Peninsula complex
description: An archaeological complex that includes the Kipp Island phase (AD 500–800).

name: New York
description: A state where the Kipp Island phase was centered, along with Ontario.

name: Ontario
description: A province where the Kipp Island phase was centered, along with New York.

name: New Jersey
description: A state where Kipp Island-related sites have been identified.

name: Delaware
description: A state where Kipp Island-related sites have been identified and where the Island Field site experienced discontinuities in ceramics, settlement, and exchange patterns.

name: Virginia
description: A state where Kipp Island-related sites have been identified.

name: Potomac River
description: A river whose Inner Coastal Plain has provided evidence of relationships between Chesapeake communities and northern peoples during the terminal Middle Woodland.

name: Inner Coastal Plain
description: A geographic region along the Potomac River where archaeological research has revealed Middle Woodland connections to northern practices.

name: Middle Woodland
description: An archaeological period during which Algonquian cultures spread through the Middle Atlantic and into the Chesapeake from the Northeast.

name: Late Woodland
description: An archaeological period by which populations can be seen in the distribution of Townsend complex sites around Chesapeake Bay.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: A region through which Algonquian cultures spread during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Northeast
description: A region from which Algonquian cultures spread into the Chesapeake during the Middle Woodland.

name: Algonquian
description: A cultural and linguistic group that may have intruded into the Chesapeake region during the Middle Woodland period, spreading from the Northeast through the Middle Atlantic.

name: Townsend complex
description: An archaeological complex whose sites surround the Chesapeake Bay and may represent populations from the Late Woodland period.

name: Colington complex
description: An archaeological complex closely related to the Townsend complex, found in the Carolina sounds region.

name: Carolina sounds region
description: A region where the Colington complex is found, closely related to the Townsend complex.

name: antler comb
description: An artifact found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: antler disks
description: Artifacts found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: perforated shark teeth
description: Artifacts found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: groundstone pendants
description: Artifacts found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: stone phallic effigy
description: An artifact found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: wooden bead
description: An artifact found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: charred textile fragments
description: Artifacts found at the Ramp 3 site associated with the Kipp Island tradition.

name: shell-tempered ceramics
description: A type of pottery that spread rapidly across the Chesapeake region during a time of identified linkages to northern communities.

name: James-York Peninsula
description: Peninsula region in Virginia where research has revealed evidence of coexisting hunter-forager traditions during the Middle Woodland period.

name: Varina ceramics
description: Grit-tempered pottery type with a long history in the James-York Peninsula area, often found on interior sites that lack midden deposits.

name: Prince George ceramics
description: Grit-tempered pottery type that co-occurs with Varina ceramics and has a long history in the James-York Peninsula area.

name: Mockley ceramics
description: Shell-tempered pottery that appears after AD 200 and generally occurs at midden sites in estuarine locations.

name: Middle Woodland Period
description: Archaeological period during which hunter-foragers from distinct traditions coexisted and interacted in the James-York Peninsula area.

name: Chesapeake
description: Region where Middle Woodland research is being conducted with new interpretive frameworks focusing on population movements and social interaction.

name: Potomac River
description: River near whose fall line Middle Woodland mortuary centers are located and interpreted in terms of Algonquian cultural landscapes.

name: Algonquian
description: Cultural group whose cosmology and cultural landscapes are used to interpret Middle Woodland mortuary centers near the Potomac River fall line.

name: AD 200
description: Time period when shell-tempered Mockley ceramics appear and when two distinct social traditions came into contact.

name: Late Woodland period
description: Archaeological period in Virginia characterized by the settlement of large permanent towns, adoption of horticulture, and emergence of chiefly political structures.

name: Chesapeake
description: Geographic region that has been the focus of archaeological research tracing Late Woodland settlements and political structures.

name: Powhatan
description: Hierarchical Algonquian society in the Chesapeake region, organized as a pyramidal political structure with a paramount chief dominating the Tidewater region.

name: Monacan
description: One of the hierarchical societies in the Chesapeake region during the Late Woodland period.

name: Piscataway
description: One of the hierarchical societies in the Chesapeake region during the Late Woodland period.

name: Patawomeke
description: One of the hierarchical societies in the Chesapeake region during the Late Woodland period.

name: Nanticoke
description: One of the hierarchical societies in the Chesapeake region during the Late Woodland period.

name: Middle Woodland
description: Archaeological period preceding the Late Woodland, characterized by societies described as 'harvesters of the Chesapeake'.

name: Early Woodland
description: Archaeological period that preceded the Middle Woodland, whose societies were transformed into the cultures observed at European Contact.

name: Potomac
description: River region where Algonquian cultural development has been analyzed in archaeological studies.

name: Delaware Valley
description: Regional area characterized by the absence of defensive communities, settlement nucleation, and population displacements during Late Woodland period.

name: Susquehanna
description: Valley region included in comparative studies of Late Woodland societies along with Delaware and Potomac valleys.

name: Middle Atlantic
description: Broader geographic region encompassing various Late Woodland societies and their transformations.

name: Owasco societies
description: Northern societies that had relationships with communities in the Delaware Valley during the Late Woodland period.

name: Lenape
description: Native American group that served as middlemen between Iroquois and Algonquian groups during both Late Woodland and historic periods.

name: Iroquois
description: Native American group mentioned in context of trade relationships mediated by the Lenape.

name: Algonquian
description: Cultural group that included societies like the Powhatan and was involved in trade relationships mediated by the Lenape.

name: European Contact
description: Historical period when European colonists first encountered and documented Native American chiefdoms in the Chesapeake region.

name: Precontact era
description: Period before European contact when Native American polities existed independently.

name: Late Woodland period
description: A chronological period in Native American prehistory characterized by horticultural development and complex settlement patterns.

name: Potomac Valley
description: A river valley and drainage basin studied for its long-term environmental and cultural history from earliest native settlement through the 18th century.

name: Rice
description: A researcher who conducted a study of Potomac Valley history examining the relationship between environmental conditions and cultural adaptations.

name: Precontact transition to horticulture
description: A cultural transition that resulted in demographic patterns among native societies that influenced early colonial settlement history.

name: James–York Peninsula
description: A peninsula that was the subject of a comprehensive survey documenting local environmental conditions and cyclical changes in settlement patterns.

name: Kiskiak polity
description: One of the communities encompassed within the Powhatan chiefdom by 1607, located on the James-York Peninsula and positioned to exploit estuarine resources.

name: Powhatan chiefdom
description: A Native American political structure that encompassed communities including Kiskiak by 1607.

name: Chesapeake
description: A geographic region referenced in cultural ecology models for understanding Native American settlement patterns.

name: Blanton et al.
description: Researchers who conducted a comprehensive survey on the James–York Peninsula and documented settlement patterns and the development of the Kiskiak polity.

name: Strickland
description: A researcher who demonstrated the interpretive strength of Geographic Information Systems for exploring Late Woodland settlement and subsistence patterns.
